Phase 1

Java Core Mechanics & OOP Deep Dive

1.1 How Java Works Under the Hood

Deep Dive: The Java Execution Pipeline

Imagine you are a chef writing a recipe in English. Your friend, who only speaks French, wants to cook the same dish. Instead of translating the recipe for every French-speaking friend, you write it once in a universal "food language" that any chef can understand. A local translator then converts it to their native tongue.

Java works the same way. Your source code (.java) is like the English recipe. The compiler (javac) translates it to bytecode (.class) - the universal food language. The JVM then translates bytecode to native machine code for whichever operating system (Windows, Linux, macOS) is running the program.

This "write once, run anywhere" philosophy is Java's superpower. The JVM is your universal translator.

Compilation vs. Execution Flow

flowchart LR
    A[Source Code<br/>.java] -->|javac| B[Bytecode<br/>.class]
    B -->|ClassLoader| C[Verification]
    C -->|JIT| D[Native Machine Code]
    D -->|CPU| E[Program Output]

    style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style D fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff
    style E fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
Java Compilation Pipeline Stages
StageTool/ComponentInputOutput
WriteText Editor / IDEDeveloper logic.java source files
Compilejavac.java.class bytecode
LoadClassLoader.classIn-memory class object
VerifyBytecode VerifierRaw bytecodeValidated bytecode
ExecuteJIT Compiler + CPUBytecodeNative machine code

JVM Architecture: The Five Subsystems

The Java Virtual Machine is not a single piece - it is a carefully orchestrated engine with five interconnected subsystems:

Analogy: The JVM is a Factory

Think of the JVM as a car manufacturing factory. The ClassLoader is the shipping dock that receives raw materials (class files). The Runtime Data Areas are the factory floor, warehouse, and worker stations. The Execution Engine is the assembly line that builds the cars. The JIT Compiler is the efficiency expert who notices patterns and builds specialized tools to speed up production. And Garbage Collection is the cleanup crew that removes scrap metal so the factory never runs out of space.

flowchart TB
    subgraph JVM["JVM Architecture"]
        CL[ClassLoader<br/>Loading → Linking → Initialization]
        CL --> RDA[Runtime Data Areas]

        subgraph RDA["Runtime Data Areas"]
            Heap[Heap<br/>objects + GC]
            Stack[Stack<br/>frames + refs]
            Method[Method Area<br/>class data + static]
            PC[PC Registers<br/>thread pointer]
        end

        RDA --> EE[Execution Engine<br/>Interpreter + JIT Compiler]
        EE --> NMI[Native Method Interface<br/>JNI]
    end

    style JVM fill:#0d1117,stroke:#30363d,color:#c9d1d9
    style CL fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style RDA fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style Heap fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style Stack fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style Method fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style PC fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style EE fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff
    style NMI fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ff7b72,color:#fff

Runtime Data Areas in Detail

JVM Runtime Memory Areas
Memory AreaWhat Lives HereThread-Safe?Size
HeapAll objects and arraysNo (shared)Configurable (-Xmx)
StackMethod frames, local variables, partial resultsYes (per thread)Fixed per thread (-Xss)
Method AreaClass metadata, static fields, method codeNo (shared)Part of heap (Java 8+)
PC RegistersCurrent instruction address per threadYes (per thread)Very small
Native Method StackNative (C/C++) method callsYes (per thread)System-dependent

Memory Management: Stack vs. Heap

java 
// Stack allocation: primitives and references stored on thread stack
public void calculate() {
    int x = 10;                    // Stored on Stack
    String name = "Java";          // Reference on Stack, Object on Heap
    Person p = new Person("Alice"); // 'p' reference on Stack, Person object on Heap
} // Stack frame popped - references gone, but Heap object remains until GC

// Heap allocation: all objects live here
public class Person {
    private String name;  // 'name' String object also on Heap
    private int age;      // 'age' primitive stored inside object on Heap
}

Garbage Collection Algorithms

GC Process Flow

Garbage Collection follows a simple principle: find objects that are no longer reachable and reclaim their memory. Modern JVMs use generational collection based on the observation that most objects die young. The Heap is divided into Young Generation (Eden + 2 Survivor spaces) and Old Generation. New objects are allocated in Eden. When Eden fills, a minor GC moves survivors to a Survivor space. Objects that survive multiple GC cycles get promoted to Old Generation. When Old Generation fills, a more expensive major GC (Full GC) runs.

Garbage Collection Algorithms Comparison
CollectorAlgorithmBest ForPause Time
Serial GCSingle-threaded, stop-the-worldSingle-core, small heaps (<100MB)~100ms+
Parallel GCMulti-threaded throughputBatch processing, high throughput~100ms+
G1GC (default Java 9+)Region-based, incrementalGeneral purpose, balancedTarget <200ms
ZGC (Java 15+)Concurrent, colored pointersUltra-low latency, large heaps<1ms
ShenandoahConcurrent evacuationLow latency, consistent response<10ms
java
GC Algorithm Selector
Select your use case to find the best Garbage Collector for your JVM.
Select a use case to get a recommendation...
// Enable G1GC (default in modern Java)
java -XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200 -jar app.jar

// Enable ZGC for ultra-low latency
java -XX:+UseZGC -XX:+ZGenerational -jar app.jar

// GC logging (Java 17 unified logging)
java -Xlog:gc*:file=gc.log:time,uptime,level,tags -jar app.jar

Just-In-Time (JIT) Compiler

The JIT compiler transforms Java from an "interpreted language" into a high-performance competitor to C++. As the JVM executes bytecode, the HotSpot Profiler monitors which methods are called frequently - these are called "hot methods." Once a method crosses an invocation threshold (typically 10,000 calls for C1, 15,000+ for C2), the JIT compiles the bytecode to highly optimized native machine code.

Analogy: The JIT is a Smart Translator

Imagine translating a speech in real-time. At first, you translate word-by-word (interpretation). But you notice the speaker keeps saying "machine learning model training" - so you prepare a pre-translated phrase and swap it in instantly. The JIT does exactly this: it starts interpreting, identifies hot code, then replaces it with optimized native code. Future calls skip interpretation entirely and run at native speed.

JIT Compiler Tiers
CompilerWhen UsedOptimization LevelCompilation Speed
C1 (Client)Method entry, low invocation countBasic inlining, constant foldingFast
C2 (Server)Hot methods, high invocation countAggressive inlining, loop unrolling, escape analysisSlower
GraalJava 10+ (alternative to C2)Partial escape analysis, speculative optimizationsModerate
Production Gotcha: The Warm-Up Problem

Never measure performance immediately after JVM startup. Code starts in interpreted mode and only gets JIT-compiled after thousands of invocations. In production, always implement a warm-up period (send synthetic traffic for 30-60 seconds) before routing real traffic. Use -XX:+PrintCompilation (or unified logging) to see when methods get compiled. Also beware of deoptimization: if a method's assumptions fail (e.g., a usually-monomorphic call site suddenly receives a new type), the JVM falls back to interpreted mode, causing latency spikes.

1.2 Core Java Foundations

The Java Collections Framework

Deep Dive: Collections Hierarchy Design

The Collections Framework is Java's answer to "how do I store and organize data?" It follows a tiered interface design: Iterable at the top ("you can loop over me"), branching into Collection (unordered bags of items) and Map (key-value pairs). The beauty is programming to interfaces - write your method to accept List<T> and it works with ArrayList, LinkedList, or any future List implementation.

flowchart TD
    Iterable --> Collection
    Collection --> List
    Collection --> Set
    Collection --> Queue

    List --> ArrayList
    List --> LinkedList

    Set --> HashSet
    Set --> TreeSet

    Queue --> PriorityQueue

    Map --> HashMap
    Map --> TreeMap
    Map --> ConcurrentHashMap

    style Iterable fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style Collection fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style List fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style Set fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style Queue fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style Map fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff
    style ArrayList fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style LinkedList fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style HashSet fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style TreeSet fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style PriorityQueue fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style HashMap fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style TreeMap fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style ConcurrentHashMap fill:#161b22,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9

HashMap Internal Mechanics

A HashMap is the most widely-used collection in Java. Understanding its internals is essential for writing performant code and debugging mysterious production issues.

java 
// How HashMap works under the hood (Java 17)
// 1. hash() method:扰动函数, reduces hash collisions
static final int hash(Object key) {
    int h;
    return (key == null) ? 0 : (h = key.hashCode()) ^ (h >>> 16);
    // XOR with higher 16 bits ensures even distribution across buckets
}

// 2. put() flow: compute index = (n - 1) & hash
// This is equivalent to hash % n but faster (bitwise AND)
public V put(K key, V value) {
    return putVal(hash(key), key, value, false, true);
}

// 3. Treeification: when a bucket has 8+ entries, it converts
// from linked list to Red-Black Tree for O(log n) lookups
static final int TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8;
static final int UNTREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 6;

// 4. Resize: triggered when load factor (0.75) is exceeded
// Capacity doubles: 16 -> 32 -> 64 -> 128 ...
final Node<K,V>[] resize() {
    Node<K,V>[] oldTab = table;
    int oldCap = (oldTab == null) ? 0 : oldTab.length;
    int newCap = oldCap << 1;  // Double the capacity
    // ... rehash all entries into new table
}
HashMap Simulator
Enter a key to see how HashMap computes the bucket index using the hash function.
Type a key above to see the hash computation...
HashMap Key Parameters
ParameterDefaultPurpose
initialCapacity16Number of buckets at creation
loadFactor0.75Threshold for resize: capacity * 0.75
TREEIFY_THRESHOLD8Convert linked list to Red-Black Tree
MAXIMUM_CAPACITY2^30Hard cap to prevent integer overflow

ConcurrentHashMap: Thread-Safe Maps

java 
// ConcurrentHashMap: lock-free reads, segmented writes
import java.util.concurrent.ConcurrentHashMap;

public class ConcurrentCache {
    // Java 8+: uses CAS + synchronized on first node per bucket
    // 16 segments by default, each bucket independently lockable
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, User> cache =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>(1000);

    public User getUser(String id) {
        // computeIfAbsent: atomic check-then-act, no race conditions
        return cache.computeIfAbsent(id, this::loadFromDatabase);
    }

    // Atomic updates without explicit synchronization
    public void incrementCounter(String key) {
        cache.merge(key, 1, Integer::sum);
    }
}

Java Generics

Deep Dive: Type Erasure

Java Generics use Type Erasure - a design decision made in 2004 for backward compatibility. At compile time, List<String> and List<Integer> are distinct types that catch errors. But after compilation, both become raw List with type information erased. The compiler inserts casts automatically. This is why you cannot do new T() or instanceof T at runtime - the JVM simply doesn't know what T was.

java 
// PECS Principle: Producer Extends, Consumer Super
// Use ? extends T when you only READ from the collection (Producer)
public void processAnimals(List<? extends Animal> animals) {
    // Can read: Animal a = animals.get(0);
    // Cannot write: animals.add(new Dog()); // COMPILE ERROR
    animals.forEach(Animal::makeSound);
}

// Use ? super T when you only WRITE to the collection (Consumer)
public void addDogs(List<? super Dog> list) {
    list.add(new Dog());
    list.add(new Puppy());
    // Can write, but reading only guarantees Object type
}

// Mnemonic: "PECS" = Producer-Extends, Consumer-Super

Exception Handling

java 
// Modern exception handling with try-with-resources (Java 7+)
public void processFile(String path) {
    // AutoCloseable resources closed in reverse order of opening
    try (BufferedReader reader = new BufferedReader(new FileReader(path));
         FileWriter writer = new FileWriter("output.txt")) {

        String line;
        while ((line = reader.readLine()) != null) {
            writer.write(line.toUpperCase() + "\n");
        }
    } catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
        // Specific: file doesn't exist
        throw new BusinessException("File not found: " + path, e);
    } catch (IOException e) {
        // General: any I/O error during read/write
        throw new BusinessException("I/O error processing file", e);
    }
}
Exception Hierarchy
TypeExtendsHandlingExamples
CheckedExceptionMust handle or declareIOException, SQLException
UncheckedRuntimeExceptionOptional handlingNPE, IllegalArgumentException
ErrorErrorDo not catchOutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError
Production Gotcha: Swallowing Exceptions

Never use empty catch blocks. The worst production bug is the silent failure. If you catch an exception, either (1) log it with context using SLF4J: log.error("Failed to process order {}", orderId, e);, (2) wrap and rethrow with a domain exception, or (3) use a recovery strategy. Also, never catch Throwable - catching OutOfMemoryError leaves the JVM in an undefined state. Catch specific exceptions, log with correlation IDs, and always preserve the stack trace when wrapping.

Java I/O and NIO

java 
// NIO.2: Modern non-blocking I/O (Java 7+)
import java.nio.file.*;
import java.nio.channels.*;
import java.nio.ByteBuffer;

public class NioServer {
    public void start() throws IOException {
        Selector selector = Selector.open();
        ServerSocketChannel server = ServerSocketChannel.open();
        server.bind(new InetSocketAddress(8080));
        server.configureBlocking(false);
        server.register(selector, SelectionKey.OP_ACCEPT);

        while (true) {
            selector.select(); // Blocks until at least one channel is ready
            Set<SelectionKey> keys = selector.selectedKeys();
            for (SelectionKey key : keys) {
                if (key.isAcceptable()) {
                    SocketChannel client = server.accept();
                    client.configureBlocking(false);
                    client.register(selector, SelectionKey.OP_READ);
                } else if (key.isReadable()) {
                    SocketChannel client = (SocketChannel) key.channel();
                    ByteBuffer buffer = ByteBuffer.allocate(1024);
                    client.read(buffer);
                    // Process the buffer...
                }
            }
            keys.clear();
        }
    }
}

Primitive Data Types

Deep Dive: Java's Primitive Type System

Java has 8 primitive types that are not objects — they hold raw values directly on the stack (when local variables) or inline in object memory. Everything else in Java is an object reference.

TypeSizeRangeDefaultUse Case
byte1 byte-128 to 1270File I/O, raw binary data
short2 bytes-32,768 to 32,7670Memory-conscious arrays (rare)
int4 bytes-2³¹ to 2³¹-10Default integer type
long8 bytes-2⁶³ to 2⁶³-10LTimestamps, large counters
float4 bytes~±3.4×10³⁸0.0fGraphics, scientific (rare)
double8 bytes~±1.7×10³⁰⁸0.0Default decimal type
char2 bytes0 to 65,535 (Unicode)'\u0000'Single Unicode character
booleanJVM-dependenttrue / falsefalseFlags, conditions

When to choose which: Use int unless you have a specific reason not to. Use long for timestamps or when int overflows. Use byte for raw binary data. Never use float for money — use BigDecimal instead.

Analogy: Boxes at the Post Office

Imagine a post office with different-sized mailboxes. A byte is a tiny key-safe box that only holds numbers from -128 to 127 — perfect for a locker code. An int is a standard PO box that holds most things you need. A long is a giant freight container for when you need to count every grain of sand on a beach. A boolean is just a light switch: on or off, nothing else. The post master (the JVM) gives you the right-sized box automatically, but if you try to stuff a giant package into a tiny box, it overflows and breaks!

Homework: Build a Smart Calculator

Task: Write a Java program that declares one variable of each primitive type, assigns it a value, then prints the value and its wrapper class name.

Expected output format:

byte: 127 (java.lang.Byte)
int: 2147483647 (java.lang.Integer)
double: 3.14159 (java.lang.Double)
...

Edge case to test: What happens when you assign 128 to a byte? Why does the compiler complain?

Wrapper Classes & Autoboxing

Gotcha: The Integer Cache Trap

Java caches Integer objects for values -128 to 127. This creates a classic interview trap:

Integer a = 127;   Integer b = 127;   // a == b  → true (cached)
Integer c = 128;   Integer d = 128;   // c == d  → false (new objects!)
Integer e = new Integer(127);         // e == a  → false (explicit new)

Always use .equals() for wrapper comparisons. The == operator compares references, not values. The cache range is configurable via -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=<n>.

PrimitiveWrapperCached RangeNotes
byteByte-128 to 127All values cached
shortShort-128 to 127Partial cache
intInteger-128 to 127Configurable via flag
longLong-128 to 127Partial cache
charCharacter0 to 127ASCII range cached
booleanBooleantrue, falseBoth cached
floatFloatNoneNever cached
doubleDoubleNoneNever cached

Performance trap: Unboxing in a tight loop causes repeated heap allocations. Use primitives in loops:

// BAD: creates 1 million Integer objects
for (Integer i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++) { sum += i; }

// GOOD: uses primitive int, no heap allocation
for (int i = 0; i < 1_000_000; i++) { sum += i; }
Analogy: The Toy Box with Numbered Slots

Imagine a toy store with a special display case that has pre-made toys numbered -128 to 127. When a kid asks for toy #127, the storekeeper just hands them the pre-made one from the case — super fast! But when someone asks for toy #128, the storekeeper has to build a brand new one from scratch. Both toys look identical, but they are different physical objects. If you check "is this the exact same toy?" (==), the answer is no for #128 but yes for #127. However, if you check "does this toy have the same number?" (.equals()), the answer is always yes. The store never caches toys with decimal numbers (float/double) because there are infinite possibilities!

Homework: Cache Boundary Detector

Task: Write a program that tests == vs .equals() on Integer objects at values -128, -127, 127, 128, and 129. Print which values show == as true vs false.

Expected output:

-128 == -128: true
127 == 127: true
128 == 128: false  ← cache boundary!

Edge case: Run with -XX:AutoBoxCacheMax=200 and test again. Does 128 now return true?

String Constant Pool Memory Diagram

Deep Dive: How Strings Live in Memory
flowchart TB
    subgraph Heap["Java Heap (since Java 7+)"]
        subgraph YoungGen["Young Generation"]
            SCP1["String Constant Pool
(interned literals)"]
        end
        subgraph OldGen["Old Generation"]
            SCP2["Long-lived interned strings"]
        end
        HeapObj1["new String('hello')
(heap object)"]
        HeapObj2["new String('hello')
(heap object)"]
    end

    subgraph Meta["Metaspace (Java 8+) / PermGen (Java 6-)"]
        ClassMeta["Class metadata
(not strings!)"]
    end

    SCP1 --"references"--> SCP2
    HeapObj1 -."intern() points to".-> SCP1
    HeapObj2 -."intern() points to".-> SCP1

    style SCP1 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style SCP2 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style HeapObj1 fill:#161b22,stroke:#ff7b72,color:#c9d1d9
    style HeapObj2 fill:#161b22,stroke:#ff7b72,color:#c9d1d9
    style ClassMeta fill:#161b22,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style Heap fill:#0d1117,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#c9d1d9
    style Meta fill:#0d1117,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#c9d1d9
Gotcha: String Literal vs new String()
String a = "hello";              // SCP reference (1 object total)
String b = "hello";              // Same SCP reference (a == b → true)
String c = new String("hello");  // Heap object + SCP literal (2 objects)
String d = new String("hello");  // Another heap object (c == d → false)
String e = c.intern();           // e points to SCP (a == e → true)

Rule of thumb: Never use new String() — it creates unnecessary heap duplicates. Use string literals directly; they automatically use the SCP.

Analogy: The Library Card Catalog

Imagine a library with a master card catalog (the String Constant Pool). When you ask for "The Hobbit," the librarian checks the catalog first. If it's already there, they just point you to the same book on the shelf — everyone shares that one copy. That's what String a = "hello" does. But if you special-order a brand new copy with fancy binding (new String("hello")), you get your own private book that looks identical but sits on a different shelf. The librarian's catalog doesn't know about your private copy unless you register it (.intern()). The library used to keep its catalog in a tiny locked room (PermGen), but now it's in the main hall where everyone can reach it (Heap).

Homework: String Detective

Task: Write a program that creates 4 strings as shown in the code example above, then prints the result of == and .equals() for every pair combination (a-b, a-c, a-d, a-e, c-d, c-e).

Expected output:

a == b: true    | a.equals(b): true
a == c: false   | a.equals(c): true
c == d: false   | c.equals(d): true
c == e: false   | c.equals(e): true  ← e is interned!

Edge case: What happens if you call .intern() on a string created with new String() and then compare with == to the literal? Why?

Loop Comparison

Deep Dive: Choosing the Right Loop
Loop TypeBest ForCan Modify Collection?Performance
for (index)Arrays, indexed access, known sizeYes (with index tracking)Fastest for arrays
whileUnknown iterations, condition-drivenYesSame as for
do-whileAt least one execution guaranteedYesSame as while
for-eachIterating all elements, readabilityNo (ConcurrentModificationException)Slightly slower (iterator overhead)
IteratorSafe removal during iterationYes (via remove())Same as for-each
ListIteratorBidirectional traversal, list modificationYes (add/set/remove)Same as Iterator
Gotcha: Modifying During Iteration

Never use for-each or standard for with index to remove elements from a collection — it causes ConcurrentModificationException or skips elements. Use Iterator.remove() or Java 8+ removeIf():

// WRONG: skips elements, causes CME
for (String s : list) { if (s.startsWith("tmp")) list.remove(s); }

// CORRECT: Iterator
Iterator<String> it = list.iterator();
while (it.hasNext()) { if (it.next().startsWith("tmp")) it.remove(); }

// MODERN: Java 8+ removeIf
list.removeIf(s -> s.startsWith("tmp"));
Analogy: The School Bus Route

A for loop is like a bus with numbered stops — you know exactly how many stops there are (1 to 10). A while loop is like a bus that keeps driving until the driver sees a "STOP" sign — you don't know when it will end. A do-while loop is like a bus that MUST visit at least one stop before checking if it should continue. A for-each loop is like a bus that visits every house on a street in order, but you're not allowed to knock down houses while riding! An Iterator is like having a demolition permit — you can remove a house, but only one at a time, and only through the proper city office (it.remove()).

Homework: Safe List Cleaner

Task: Create an ArrayList<String> with values: "tmp1", "keep", "tmp2", "important", "tmp3". Remove all strings starting with "tmp" using three different methods: Iterator, removeIf, and a manual index loop (backwards). Print the list after each method.

Expected output:

After Iterator: [keep, important]
After removeIf: [keep, important]
After backwards loop: [keep, important]

Edge case: What happens if you try to remove using a forward index loop (i from 0 to size-1)? Why does it skip elements?

final vs finally vs finalize()

Deep Dive: Three Completely Different Keywords
KeywordWhat It IsUsage ContextKey Behavior
finalAccess modifierVariables, methods, classesVariable: can't reassign. Method: can't override. Class: can't extend.
finallyBlock keywordAfter try-catchAlways executes (even with return/exception) — perfect for cleanup.
finalize()Object methodCalled by GC before destructionDeprecated in Java 9, marked for removal in Java 18 (JEP 421). Unreliable — never use.
 
final class ImmutableConfig {           // class cannot be extended
    final String apiKey;                 // field cannot be reassigned

    public void connect() {
        Connection conn = null;
        try {
            conn = DriverManager.getConnection(url);
            // ... use conn
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            log.error("Connection failed", e);
        } finally {                        // ALWAYS runs
            if (conn != null) {
                try { conn.close(); } catch (SQLException ignored) {}
            }
        }
    }

    @Override
    protected void finalize() throws Throwable {  // DEPRECATED — DO NOT USE
        super.finalize();  // Unreliable, single-threaded, can resurrect objects
    }
}
Analogy: The School Rules, Safety Net, and Ghost

final is like a school rule written in permanent marker — once the teacher writes "No running in the halls," that rule can never be changed. finally is like a safety net under a trapeze artist — no matter what happens (even if they fall mid-flip), the net ALWAYS catches them. It's where you put cleanup code that must run. finalize() is like a ghost that supposedly haunts the school after graduation — people say it will clean up your locker, but it's unreliable, might never show up, and the school officially banned it (Java 9+). Use a real janitor (try-with-resources or Cleaner API) instead!

Homework: The Unstoppable Cleanup

Task: Write a method that opens a file, reads the first line, and returns it. Use try-catch-finally to ensure the file reader is closed even if an exception occurs. Then rewrite the same method using try-with-resources.

Expected behavior: Even if you throw a RuntimeException after reading the line, the file must still close.

Edge case: What happens if the close() method in finally also throws an exception? Which exception does the caller see — the original one or the close exception?

Access Modifiers

Deep Dive: The Visibility Grid
ModifierSame ClassSame PackageSubclass (diff pkg)World
public✅✅✅✅
protected✅✅✅❌
package-private (no keyword)✅✅❌❌
private✅❌❌❌

Memory trick: Visibility decreases top-to-bottom: public → protected → package-private → private. Think of it as a castle: public is the drawbridge (anyone), protected is the inner gate (family + invited guests), package-private is the family wing, private is the king's chamber.

Note: "default" (package-private) means no keyword written — it is NOT the default keyword used in switch statements or interface methods.

Analogy: The Castle Security Levels

Imagine a medieval castle. public is the town square — anyone can enter, even strangers from far away. protected is the castle courtyard — villagers from the same town (same package) can enter, and invited knights from other towns (subclasses) can also enter, but random travelers cannot. package-private (no keyword) is the family dining hall — only family members living in the castle (same package) may enter; even cousins from other castles (subclasses in different packages) are turned away. private is the king's bedroom — only the king himself (the same class) can enter. No exceptions!

Homework: Build a Secure Bank Account

Task: Create a BankAccount class with 4 fields, each using a different access modifier: public String accountName, protected double balance, String accountType (package-private), and private String password. Create a second class in the same package and a third class in a different package that extends BankAccount. Try to access each field from each class and document which ones compile and which ones fail.

Expected result: The subclass in a different package can access public and protected fields, but NOT private or package-private fields.

Edge case: Can a nested inner class access private fields of its outer class? Test it!

Deep Dive: String Constant Pool Evolution

1.3 Object-Oriented Programming Deep Dive

The Four Pillars of OOP

Analogy: OOP is Like Building with LEGO

Inheritance is having a base "Vehicle" LEGO set and creating specialized "Car" and "Truck" sets that reuse the base wheels and chassis. Polymorphism is having one "move" instruction that works differently for Cars (drive) and Planes (fly) - same command, different behavior. Encapsulation is putting the complex engine inside an opaque LEGO block with only connection studs visible - you don't need to know how the engine works, just how to connect it. Abstraction is the instruction manual showing simplified step-by-step pictures instead of the complete engineering blueprint.

Polymorphism: Static vs. Dynamic

java 
public class PolymorphismDemo {
    // Static (Compile-time) Polymorphism: Method Overloading
    public int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }
    public double add(double a, double b) { return a + b; }
    public String add(String a, String b) { return a + b; }
    // Compiler picks the method based on argument types at compile time

    // Dynamic (Runtime) Polymorphism: Method Overriding
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Animal animal = new Dog(); // Reference type = Animal, Object type = Dog
        animal.makeSound();       // "Woof!" - resolved at runtime via vtable
        // JVM uses virtual method table (vtable) to dispatch to Dog's implementation
    }
}

class Animal {
    public void makeSound() { System.out.println("Some sound"); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    @Override
    public void makeSound() { System.out.println("Woof!"); }
}

SOLID Principles

SOLID Principles at a Glance
PrincipleDefinitionViolation Symptom
Single ResponsibilityA class should have one reason to changeMonster class with 500+ lines
Open/ClosedOpen for extension, closed for modificationAdding a feature breaks existing tests
Liskov SubstitutionSubtypes must be substitutable for base typesif (obj instanceof X) checks
Interface SegregationClients shouldn't depend on methods they don't useEmpty method implementations
Dependency InversionDepend on abstractions, not concretionsnew Service() in business logic
java 
// Dependency Inversion: Inject dependencies, don't create them
// BAD: Tight coupling
public class OrderService {
    private MySQLRepository repo = new MySQLRepository(); // Hardcoded!
    private SmtpEmailSender email = new SmtpEmailSender(); // Hardcoded!
}

// GOOD: Depend on abstractions, inject implementations
public class OrderService {
    private final OrderRepository repo;      // Interface
    private final NotificationService email; // Interface

    public OrderService(OrderRepository repo, NotificationService email) {
        this.repo = repo;
        this.email = email;
    }
    // Now you can swap MySQL for PostgreSQL, or SMTP for SendGrid
    // without touching OrderService code (Open/Closed Principle!)
}

Composition Over Inheritance

Deep Dive: Why Composition Wins

Inheritance creates a rigid "is-a" relationship that breaks when requirements change. If you model Employee extends Person, what happens when an Employee also needs to be a Customer? Java doesn't allow multiple inheritance of classes. Composition uses a "has-a" relationship instead: Employee has-a PersonDetails, has-a Department, has-a List<Role>. You can mix and match behaviors at runtime, swap implementations without breaking code, and test components in isolation. As the Gang of Four stated: "Favor object composition over class inheritance."

java 
// Composition: Flexible behavior delegation
public class SmartPhone {
    // Has-a relationships: can mix any combination
    private final Camera camera;        // Could be 12MP, 48MP, 108MP
    private final Processor processor;  // Could be Snapdragon, Apple Silicon
    private final Battery battery;      // Different capacities

    public SmartPhone(Camera c, Processor p, Battery b) {
        this.camera = c;
        this.processor = p;
        this.battery = b;
    }

    public Photo takePhoto() { return camera.capture(); }
    public void runApp(App app) { processor.execute(app); }
}

// Strategy Pattern: Swap algorithms at runtime
public interface PaymentStrategy {
    PaymentResult pay(BigDecimal amount);
}
public class CheckoutService {
    private PaymentStrategy strategy;  // Compose with any payment method
    public void setStrategy(PaymentStrategy s) { this.strategy = s; }
}
Production Gotcha: The Fragile Base Class Problem

When you inherit from a class, you are tightly coupled to its implementation details. If the parent class changes a method you override, your subclass may break silently. In Java, always use @Override to catch signature mismatches. Better yet, prefer final classes for value objects (records in Java 16+ are final by default), sealed classes (Java 17+) to control inheritance, and interfaces with default methods for shared behavior. Never call overridable methods from constructors - the subclass hasn't been constructed yet, leading to NPEs on uninitialized fields.

🧠
Phase 1 Knowledge Check
What is the primary advantage of Java's "Write Once, Run Anywhere" philosophy?
A Java code runs faster than C++ on all platforms
B The JVM translates bytecode to native machine code for any OS
C Java doesn't need compilation before execution
D Java source code can be read by any text editor

this vs super

Deep Dive: Referencing Current vs Parent Object
Contextthissuper
Refers toCurrent object instanceImmediate parent class object
Constructor callthis() — calls another constructor in same class. Must be first statement.super() — calls parent constructor. Implicit if not written. Must be first statement.
Method callthis.method() — calls method on current object (rarely needed explicitly)super.method() — calls parent version, bypassing override
Field accessthis.field — resolves shadowing (parameter with same name)super.field — accesses parent field hidden by subclass field
Cannot use whenIn static context (no instance)In static context; in Object class (no parent)
 
class Animal {
    String name = "Animal";
    Animal(String name) { this.name = name; }
    void speak() { System.out.println("Some sound"); }
}

class Dog extends Animal {
    String name = "Dog";  // shadows parent field

    Dog(String name) {
        super(name);      // calls Animal(String) — MUST be first
        this.name = name; // assigns to Dog's field
    }

    void speak() {
        super.speak();    // prints "Some sound"
        System.out.println(super.name);  // "Animal" — parent's field
        System.out.println(this.name);   // "Dog" — current field
    }
}
Analogy: Family Photo Albums

this is like looking at your own photo album — it shows pictures of YOU, your room, your toys. When you say "this toy," you mean the one in your hands right now. super is like looking at your parent's photo album — it shows pictures of THEM before you were born. When your dad has the same name as you (both called "Junior"), this.name means YOUR name, while super.name means your DAD's name. And when you build a new room in your house, super() is like calling your dad first to make sure the foundation is solid before you add your own decorations on top!

Homework: Three-Generation Family Tree

Task: Create a 3-class hierarchy: Animal → Dog → Puppy. Each class has a constructor that prints "[ClassName] constructor called" and calls super() first. Create a Puppy object and trace the output order.

Expected output:

Animal constructor called
Dog constructor called
Puppy constructor called

Edge case: What happens if you try to call this() and super() in the same constructor? Why does the compiler reject it?

Static vs Instance + Singleton Pattern

Deep Dive: Class-Level vs Object-Level
AspectstaticInstance (non-static)
Belongs toClass (one copy total)Object (each instance has its own)
Memory locationMetaspace (method area)Heap (inside object)
Access viaClassName.member (preferred) or obj.memberobj.member only
Can accessOnly static members directlyBoth static and instance members
this / superCannot use (no instance)Can use freely
LifecycleLoaded when class is first used, lives until class unloadedCreated with new, destroyed when GC'd

Static initializer blocks run once when the class is loaded, before any constructor:

public class DatabaseConfig {
    static final String URL;
    static final int MAX_CONNECTIONS;

    static {  // runs ONCE when class loads
        URL = System.getenv("DB_URL");
        MAX_CONNECTIONS = Integer.parseInt(System.getenv("DB_MAX_CONN"));
        System.out.println("DatabaseConfig initialized");
    }
}
Deep Dive: Singleton Pattern — From Naive to Correct

1. Naive (NOT thread-safe)

public class NaiveSingleton {
    private static NaiveSingleton instance;
    private NaiveSingleton() {}
    public static NaiveSingleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) instance = new NaiveSingleton();  // RACE CONDITION!
        return instance;
    }
}

2. Double-Checked Locking (CORRECT)

public class SafeSingleton {
    private static volatile SafeSingleton instance;  // volatile is CRITICAL
    private SafeSingleton() {}
    public static SafeSingleton getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {                          // first check (no lock)
            synchronized (SafeSingleton.class) {
                if (instance == null) {                  // second check (with lock)
                    instance = new SafeSingleton();
                }
            }
        }
        return instance;
    }
}

volatile prevents instruction reordering: without it, another thread could see a partially constructed object.

Gotcha: Never Call Overridable Methods from Constructors
class Parent {
    Parent() { initialize(); }  // DANGEROUS!
    void initialize() { /* parent logic */ }
}

class Child extends Parent {
    private final List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();

    @Override
    void initialize() {
        list.add("item");  // CRASH! list is still null during super()
    }
}

During construction, the subclass fields are not yet initialized when the parent constructor runs. Always call private or final methods from constructors.

Analogy: The Town Water Tower vs House Faucets

A static field is like the town water tower — there is exactly ONE tower for the entire town (class), and every house (object) draws from the same tower. If you add chlorine to the tower, every house gets chlorinated water. An instance field is like each house having its own water heater — one house can have hot water while another has cold water, and changing your heater doesn't affect your neighbor. A Singleton is like the town mayor's office — there can only be ONE mayor at a time. The naive version is like two people both running for mayor simultaneously and both winning (two instances!). The correct double-checked version is like having a locked ballot box: first you peek to see if there's already a winner (no lock needed), but if not, you lock the box, check again, and only then declare yourself mayor.

Homework: Thread-Safe Configuration Manager

Task: Implement a ConfigManager Singleton using double-checked locking with volatile. It should store a Map<String, String> of configuration values loaded from a properties file in the static initializer. Provide getProperty(String key) and setProperty(String key, String value) methods.

Expected behavior: Two threads calling getInstance() simultaneously must never create two separate instances.

Edge case: What happens if you remove volatile from the instance field? Research "instruction reordering" and explain why the second thread might see a non-null but incompletely constructed object.

Deep Dive: finalize() Deprecation & Modern Alternatives
Phase 2

Advanced Java & Enterprise Foundations

2.1 Concurrency & Multithreading

Deep Dive: Why Concurrency Matters

Modern CPUs have stopped getting faster in terms of clock speed. Instead, they gain more cores - 8, 16, 32, even 64 cores are common in servers. If your program uses only one thread, you are leaving 95% of your CPU's power unused. Concurrency is how we unlock that power: running multiple tasks simultaneously, either by switching rapidly between them on a single core (multitasking) or running them truly in parallel on multiple cores.

But concurrency introduces complexity. When two threads access the same data simultaneously, race conditions occur. When threads wait for each other, deadlocks happen. The Java Concurrency Utilities provide battle-tested tools to manage this complexity safely.

Thread Lifecycle

stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start()
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() completes
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait()
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify()
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep/join
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : time expires
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : synchronized
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired

    style NEW fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style RUNNABLE fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style TERMINATED fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ff7b72,color:#fff
    style WAITING fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff
    style TIMED_WAITING fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff
    style BLOCKED fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffa657,color:#fff
Analogy: The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a restaurant kitchen. A NEW thread is like a chef who just walked in and put on their apron — ready but not cooking yet. When the head chef (JVM) calls start(), the chef moves to RUNNABLE and grabs a stove (CPU core). If the chef needs to wait for a delivery (I/O), they step aside into WAITING or TIMED_WAITING. If another chef needs the same stove, our chef goes to BLOCKED. When the shift ends or the chef quits, they become TERMINATED. Just like a real kitchen, you can't restart a chef who already left — once terminated, always terminated!

Deep Insight: Thread States & JVM Internals
Homework: Thread Lifecycle Mastery

Task 1: Write a Java program that creates 3 threads. Make Thread A print numbers 1-5, Thread B print letters A-E, and Thread C print symbols @-#. Use Thread.sleep() with different durations to observe how the scheduler interleaves them. Print the thread state before and after start().

Task 2: Create a deadlock scenario intentionally. Two threads each lock two objects in opposite order. Use jstack or VisualVM to observe the BLOCKED state. Document the thread dump showing the circular wait.

Task 3: Implement a thread pool using raw Thread (not ExecutorService) that manages a fixed number of worker threads. Workers should pull tasks from a shared BlockingQueue. Measure throughput vs. creating a new thread per task.

Thread Synchronization

java 
// synchronized: Intrinsic lock (monitor) on object
public class BankAccount {
    private int balance = 0;

    // Method-level: lock on 'this' instance
    public synchronized void deposit(int amount) {
        balance += amount;  // Atomic: other threads block here
    }

    // Block-level: more granular control
    public void withdraw(int amount) {
        synchronized (this) {
            if (balance >= amount) {
                balance -= amount;  // Check-then-act must be atomic
            }
        }
    }
}

// volatile: ensures visibility, NOT atomicity
public class VolatileExample {
    // Without volatile: thread may cache flag locally, never seeing the update
    private volatile boolean running = true;

    public void shutdown() { running = false; }  // Visible to all threads immediately
    public void doWork() {
        while (running) {  // Reads from main memory, not CPU cache
            // Do work...
        }
    }
}
Analogy: The Single Bathroom

Imagine a house with one bathroom and 5 roommates. synchronized is like putting a lock on the bathroom door — only one person can use it at a time. The wait() method is like someone saying "I'll be back in 5 minutes" and stepping out so someone else can use it. notify() is knocking on the door to tell the next person it's free. notifyAll() is shouting "bathroom's free!" so everyone waiting knows. But if everyone shouts at once, it's chaos — just like spurious wakeups in threads! And ReentrantLock is like a smart lock with a keypad: you can check if it's occupied without blocking (tryLock), set a timeout (tryLock with time), or even make it fair so the person waiting longest goes first.

Deep Insight: synchronized vs ReentrantLock vs volatile
Homework: Synchronization Mastery

Task 1: Build a thread-safe bounded buffer (producer-consumer) using only synchronized, wait(), and notify(). No BlockingQueue allowed. Test with 5 producers and 3 consumers, each producing/consuming 1000 items. Measure throughput.

Task 2: Rewrite the same buffer using ReentrantLock with Condition variables (not wait()/notify()). Compare code clarity and performance. Implement a fair version and measure if fairness hurts throughput.

Task 3: Create a bank account class with transfer(Account other, int amount). Make it deadlock-free using lock ordering. Write a test that transfers money between 100 accounts randomly from 50 threads for 10 seconds. Verify no deadlocks and total money is conserved.

ExecutorService: Managed Thread Pools

java 
// Modern thread management: never create threads manually
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class ThreadPoolExample {
    // 1. Fixed thread pool: exactly N threads
    private final ExecutorService fixedPool =
        Executors.newFixedThreadPool(10);

    // 2. Cached thread pool: grows/shrinks dynamically
    private final ExecutorService cachedPool =
        Executors.newCachedThreadPool();

    // 3. BEST PRACTICE: Manual ThreadPoolExecutor with bounded queue
    private final ThreadPoolExecutor executor = new ThreadPoolExecutor(
        4,                      // core pool size
        8,                      // maximum pool size
        60L, TimeUnit.SECONDS,   // keep-alive time for idle threads
        new LinkedBlockingQueue<>(100),  // bounded queue: prevents OOM
        new ThreadPoolExecutor.CallerRunsPolicy()  // backpressure: caller runs task
    );

    // 4. Submit work and get result
    public CompletableFuture<String> processAsync(String input) {
        return CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            // Long-running computation
            return input.toUpperCase();
        }, executor);  // Use custom executor, not ForkJoinPool.commonPool()
    }
}
Analogy: The Call Center

Imagine a call center. newFixedThreadPool(10) is like having exactly 10 operators — no more, no less. If all 10 are busy, new calls wait in a queue. newCachedThreadPool() is like hiring temporary operators on demand — great for spikes, but expensive if you keep them around. newSingleThreadExecutor() is a one-person call center — everything happens in order, one at a time. ScheduledThreadPool is like operators who check their watch and make follow-up calls at exact times. ForkJoinPool is like a team that breaks big problems into tiny pieces, solves them in parallel, and puts the answers back together — perfect for divide-and-conquer tasks like sorting a massive list!

Deep Insight: Thread Pool Tuning & Production Pitfalls
Homework: ExecutorService Mastery

Task 1: Implement a custom ThreadPoolExecutor with a RejectedExecutionHandler that logs rejected tasks and retries them after 1 second. Test by submitting 10,000 tasks to a pool of size 2 with a queue of 100.

Task 2: Build a web crawler using ForkJoinPool. Given a starting URL, recursively crawl all links up to depth 3. Use RecursiveTask<List<String>> to parallelize. Compare crawl time vs. single-threaded version on a local test server.

Task 3: Create a ScheduledExecutorService that runs a health-check every 5 seconds. If the check fails 3 times in a row, trigger an alert. Implement graceful shutdown: stop accepting new checks, wait up to 30 seconds for running checks to finish, then force-stop.

Modern Concurrency: CompletableFuture

java 
// CompletableFuture: composing async operations
public CompletableFuture<Order> processOrder(String orderId) {
    return fetchOrder(orderId)                    // Step 1: fetch
        .thenApply(this::validateOrder)          // Step 2: validate
        .thenCompose(this::reserveInventory)   // Step 3: reserve (returns CF)
        .thenCombine(
            calculateShipping(orderId),       // Parallel: calc shipping
            Order::withShippingCost
        )
        .orTimeout(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS)       // Timeout protection
        .exceptionally(ex -> {                   // Error handling
            log.error("Order failed: {}", orderId, ex);
            return Order.failed(orderId);
        });
}
Analogy: The Pizza Delivery Chain

Imagine ordering pizza online. supplyAsync() is placing the order — it starts the work in the background. thenApply() is adding toppings after the dough is ready — transforms the result. thenAccept() is the delivery person handing you the pizza — consumes it without returning anything. thenCompose() is ordering dessert from a different restaurant using your pizza receipt — chains dependent async operations. thenCombine() is waiting for both pizza AND soda to arrive before eating — combines two independent futures. exceptionally() is the "sorry, we're out of pepperoni, here's a discount" fallback. And allOf() is a dinner party where you wait for ALL dishes before serving, while anyOf() is a race — whoever delivers first wins!

Deep Insight: Async Pipeline Patterns & Error Handling
Homework: CompletableFuture Mastery

Task 1: Build an async e-commerce checkout: validate cart (async) → check inventory (async) → calculate total (async) → process payment (async) → send confirmation email (async). Chain with thenCompose. Handle each step's failure with exceptionally and provide meaningful fallback. Use a custom Executor with a named thread pool.

Task 2: Implement a parallel data aggregation service. Given 5 different API endpoints, fetch all concurrently using CompletableFuture. Combine results with thenCombine or allOf. Add a 5-second timeout using orTimeout. If any API fails, log it but don't fail the whole request.

Task 3: Create a retry mechanism using CompletableFuture. A method should retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff (1s, 2s, 4s) on IOException. Use handle to decide whether to retry or propagate. Test with a mock service that fails randomly.

Virtual Threads (Project Loom) - Java 21

java 
// Virtual Threads: lightweight, auto-managed by JVM
// Millions possible vs. thousands of platform threads
public class VirtualThreadServer {

    // 1. Create virtual thread per task (structured concurrency)
    public void handleRequests(List<Request> requests) {
        try (var scope = new StructuredTaskScope.ShutdownOnFailure()) {
            List<StructuredTaskScope.Subtask<Response>> subtasks =
                requests.stream()
                    .map(req -> scope.fork(() -> process(req)))
                    .toList();

            scope.join();           // Wait for all to complete
            scope.throwIfFailed();  // If any failed, propagate

            return subtasks.stream()
                .map(StructuredTaskScope.Subtask::get)
                .toList();
        }
    }

    // 2. Spring Boot 3.2+ with virtual threads
    // application.properties:
    // spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true
    // Now @Async, @Scheduled, and Tomcat all use virtual threads automatically
}
Production Gotcha: Thread Pinning

Virtual threads can be pinned to platform threads when executing synchronized blocks or native methods. This negates their benefits - the carrier thread is blocked and cannot mount other virtual threads. In Java 21+, use java.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock instead of synchronized inside virtual threads. Monitor with -Djdk.tracePinnedThreads=full. Also, avoid thread-local variables with virtual threads - they don't work as expected since a virtual thread may run on different carriers over time. Use ScopedValue (Java 21+) instead.

Analogy: The Ghost Workers

Imagine a warehouse with 10 real workers (platform threads) but thousands of ghost workers (virtual threads). When a ghost worker needs to wait for a delivery truck, they vanish into thin air — freeing the real worker to help another ghost. When the truck arrives, the ghost reappears instantly. You can have a million ghost workers because they only exist when actually working. But if a ghost tries to lift a heavy box while holding a special "synchronized" badge, they get STUCK to the real worker — this is pinning, and it ruins the magic! The solution? Give ghosts ReentrantLock badges instead, which let them vanish even while holding the lock.

Deep Insight: Virtual Threads Production Tuning & Pinning
Homework: Virtual Threads Mastery

Task 1: Write a Spring Boot 3.2+ application with spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true. Create a REST endpoint that simulates I/O (Thread.sleep(100ms)). Load-test with 10,000 concurrent requests using k6 or JMeter. Compare throughput and memory vs. platform threads (set server.tomcat.threads.max=200).

Task 2: Demonstrate thread pinning. Create a virtual thread that enters a synchronized block and then performs a blocking HTTP call. Run with -Djdk.tracePinnedThreads=full and capture the pinning trace. Rewrite using ReentrantLock and show the pinning disappears.

Task 3: Implement a dual-executor configuration: one with virtual threads, one with platform threads. Use a feature flag to route requests between them. Write a JUnit test that validates both paths produce identical results. Document migration strategy from platform to virtual threads.

Functional Programming: Streams API

java 
// Streams: declarative data processing pipeline
public Map<Department, BigDecimal> calculatePayroll(List<Employee> employees) {
    return employees.stream()
        .filter(e -> e.isActive())                    // Intermediate: filter
        .flatMap(e -> e.getPayrollEntries().stream())  // Intermediate: flatten
        .filter(entry -> entry.getYear() == 2024)   // Intermediate: filter
        .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(            // Terminal: collect
            PayrollEntry::getDepartment,
            TreeMap::new,
            Collectors.reducing(BigDecimal.ZERO,
                PayrollEntry::getAmount,
                BigDecimal::add)
        ));
}

// Lazy evaluation: intermediates are only executed on terminal operation
// Stream fusion: multiple intermediates optimized into single pass
// Short-circuiting: findFirst(), anyMatch(), limit() can stop early
Stream Operations Reference
OperationTypePurpose
filter()IntermediateKeep elements matching predicate
map()IntermediateTransform each element
flatMap()IntermediateFlatten nested structures
sorted()Intermediate (stateful)Sort elements
distinct()Intermediate (stateful)Remove duplicates
collect()TerminalAggregate to collection
reduce()TerminalCombine elements to single value
forEach()TerminalSide effect on each element
findFirst()Terminal (short-circuit)Return first match
Analogy: The Assembly Line

Imagine a factory assembly line. A Stream is the conveyor belt — items move along it one by one. filter() is a quality inspector who removes bad items. map() is a robot that transforms each item (e.g., paints it). sorted() is a worker who rearranges items in order before they continue. collect() is the packing station at the end — it gathers everything into a box (List, Set, Map). reduce() is a machine that mashes all items into one (like summing prices). parallelStream() is adding MULTIPLE conveyor belts — faster, but only if the items don't need to stay in order and the workers don't fight over shared tools!

Deep Insight: Parallel Streams Pitfalls & Best Practices
Homework: Streams API Mastery

Task 1: Given a List<Employee> with fields (id, name, department, salary, joiningDate), write a single stream pipeline that: finds the top 3 highest-paid employees per department, handles null departments gracefully, and returns a Map<String, List<Employee>>. Use only stream operations — no loops.

Task 2: Demonstrate the parallel stream pitfall. Create a stream that uses a shared ArrayList<Integer> with forEach(list::add). Run with .parallel() and show missing/duplicate elements. Fix it using Collectors.toList() and explain why it works.

Task 3: Implement a custom Collector<T, ?, Map<K, List<T>>> that groups by a key AND maintains insertion order within each group. Test with 1 million elements. Compare performance vs. Collectors.groupingBy().

2.2 Web & Persistence Foundations

Servlet Lifecycle

Deep Dive: What is a Servlet?

A Servlet is Java's fundamental building block for web applications. Think of it as a "Java program that speaks HTTP." When a user types a URL in their browser, the HTTP request travels through the internet to a Servlet Container (like Tomcat or Jetty). The container looks up which Servlet handles that URL, creates a thread, and calls the Servlet's service() method. The Servlet reads the request, performs business logic, and writes an HTTP response. This request-response cycle is the foundation of every Java web framework, including Spring MVC.

sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Client Browser
    participant Container as Servlet Container<br/>(Tomcat/Jetty)
    participant Servlet as Servlet.service()

    Client->>Container: HTTP Request
    activate Container
    Container->>Container: Create Thread
    Container->>Servlet: service()
    activate Servlet
    Servlet->>Servlet: Business Logic
    Servlet-->>Container: HTTP Response
    deactivate Servlet
    Container-->>Client: HTTP Response
    deactivate Container
java 
@WebServlet("/hello")
public class HelloServlet extends HttpServlet {

    @Override
    public void init() throws ServletException {
        // Called once when Servlet is first loaded
        // Initialize database connections, caches, etc.
    }

    @Override
    protected void doGet(HttpServletRequest req, HttpServletResponse resp)
            throws ServletException, IOException {

        resp.setContentType("application/json");
        PrintWriter out = resp.getWriter();
        out.print("{\"message\": \"Hello, World!\"}");
    }

    @Override
    public void destroy() {
        // Called once when Servlet is unloaded
        // Clean up resources: close connections, flush caches
    }
}
Analogy: The Restaurant Waiter

Imagine a fancy restaurant. The Servlet Container (like Tomcat) is the restaurant manager. When the restaurant opens, the manager instantiates each waiter (servlet) and gives them training (init()) — this happens ONCE. When a customer arrives, the manager assigns a waiter to their table. The waiter takes the order (service()), brings food, and handles the bill. The same waiter serves many customers, one at a time — but if too many customers arrive, the manager hires more waiters (creates more servlet instances or uses a thread pool). When the restaurant closes, the manager fires all waiters gracefully (destroy()), giving them time to finish serving current tables. The key insight: init() and destroy() happen once per servlet lifetime, but service() happens for EVERY request!

Deep Insight: Servlet Container Internals & Thread Safety
Homework: Servlet Lifecycle Mastery

Task 1: Create a custom HttpServlet that logs every lifecycle event (constructor, init, service, destroy) with timestamps. Deploy to Tomcat. Send 100 concurrent requests using Apache Bench. Observe: how many times does each method run? Document the thread-safety implications of instance variables.

Task 2: Implement a ServletContextListener that initializes a database connection pool on startup and closes it on shutdown. Use @WebListener annotation. Verify the pool is available to all servlets via ServletContext attributes.

Task 3: Build a simple MVC framework from scratch using only Servlets and JSP (no Spring). Implement: Front Controller pattern, URL routing via servlet mapping, request attribute passing to JSP, and a basic template engine. Support at least 3 routes with different controllers.

Hibernate: Object-Relational Mapping

java 
// Entity: Java class mapped to database table
@Entity
@Table(name = "users")
public class User {
    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    private Long id;

    @Column(nullable = false, length = 100)
    private String username;

    @Column(name = "email_addr", unique = true)
    private String email;

    // Entity Lifecycle States:
    // TRANSIENT: new object, not associated with Session
    // MANAGED: associated with Session, changes auto-flushed
    // DETACHED: was managed, Session closed, changes not tracked
    // REMOVED: scheduled for deletion on commit
}

// Core Hibernate API usage
public class UserRepository {
    private final SessionFactory sessionFactory;

    public User findById(Long id) {
        try (Session session = sessionFactory.openSession()) {
            // get(): returns null if not found
            // load(): returns proxy, throws if not found
            return session.get(User.class, id);
        }
    }

    public Long save(User user) {
        try (Session session = sessionFactory.openSession()) {
            Transaction tx = session.beginTransaction();
            try {
                Long id = (Long) session.save(user);
                tx.commit();  // Flush changes to database
                return id;
            } catch (Exception e) {
                tx.rollback();
                throw e;
            }
        }
    }
}
Analogy: The Universal Translator

Imagine you're a diplomat who only speaks Java, but you need to talk to a database that only understands SQL. Hibernate is your universal translator! You write Java objects (entities), and Hibernate translates them into SQL tables, rows, and columns. @Entity says "this Java class represents a database table." @Id says "this field is the primary key." @OneToMany is like saying "one country has many cities." @ManyToOne is "many employees work in one department." The Session is your conversation with the database — you open it, say things (save, update, delete), and then close it. But be careful: if you try to access a city's details AFTER closing the conversation, the translator has left — that's a LazyInitializationException!

Deep Insight: Hibernate Session & Transaction Internals
Homework: Hibernate ORM Mastery

Task 1: Design a domain model for a Library system: Book, Author, Member, Loan. Use all JPA relationship types (@OneToOne, @OneToMany, @ManyToMany). Implement with proper cascade and orphan removal. Write integration tests using an in-memory H2 database.

Task 2: Demonstrate the difference between merge() and saveOrUpdate(). Create a detached entity, modify it, and show how merge() creates a copy while saveOrUpdate() reattaches. Use @Version for optimistic locking and show a conflict scenario.

Task 3: Implement a generic DAO layer with BaseRepository<T, ID> using Spring Data JPA's JpaRepository. Add custom methods using @Query with JPQL and native SQL. Implement pagination and sorting. Write a specification-based dynamic query using JpaSpecificationExecutor.

Advanced Hibernate: Caching & Fetching

Hibernate Caching Strategy Comparison
Cache LevelScopeConcurrencyUse Case
First-LevelSession-boundThread-safe (per session)Entity lookaside within transaction
Second-LevelSessionFactory-boundNeeds provider (EhCache, Caffeine)Reference data (countries, configs)
Query CacheSessionFactory-boundCaches result sets by SQLRead-heavy, rarely-changing queries
Production Gotcha: The N+1 Select Problem

The N+1 problem is the most common Hibernate performance killer. When you load N entities and lazily access a relationship, Hibernate executes 1 initial query + N additional queries. For 100 orders with customer relationships, that's 101 queries instead of 1. Solutions: (1) Use JOIN FETCH in JPQL: SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.customer, (2) Use @EntityGraph to declare fetch plans, (3) Enable batch fetching: @BatchSize(size = 50), (4) Use a DTO projection for read-only queries. Always check SQL logs in development - if you see a query repeating in a loop, you have N+1.

java 
// Solving N+1 with Entity Graph
@NamedEntityGraph(name = "Order.withCustomer",
    attributeNodes = @NamedAttributeNode("customer"))
@Entity
public class Order { /* ... */ }

// Usage:
EntityGraph<?> graph = session.getEntityGraph("Order.withCustomer");
Map<String, Object> hints = Map.of("jakarta.persistence.loadgraph", graph);
Order order = session.find(Order.class, orderId, hints);
// Now customer is fetched in a single JOIN query
Analogy: The Library Cache System

Imagine a huge library. The 1st Level Cache is your personal backpack — when you check out a book, you keep it with you during your visit. If you ask for the same book again, it's right there! The 2nd Level Cache is the library's public display shelf — books stay there even after you leave, so the next person finds them faster. Query Cache is like a photocopy machine — if someone already looked up "books about cats," the librarian remembers the list instead of searching again. But the N+1 problem is like asking the librarian for 100 books one by one instead of giving them a list — each request is fast, but 100 requests kill performance! The fix? Give the librarian the whole list at once (JOIN FETCH) or ask them to batch the lookups (@BatchSize).

Deep Insight: N+1 Problem & Caching Strategies
Homework: Advanced Hibernate Mastery

Task 1: Create a reproducible N+1 scenario with 3 entities: Department → Employee → Project. Use FetchType.LAZY and iterate over departments, accessing employees and their projects. Enable SQL logging. Measure query count. Fix with JOIN FETCH, @BatchSize, and @EntityGraph. Compare query counts and result set sizes.

Task 2: Configure Ehcache as 2nd Level Cache. Enable query caching. Write a test that executes the same query twice and verify the 2nd execution produces zero SQL (cache hit). Test cache eviction with @CacheEvict. Measure memory usage with 10,000 cached entities.

Task 3: Implement a read-only query optimization scenario. Use @Immutable on an entity, QueryHints.HINT_READONLY, and StatelessSession for bulk inserts. Compare performance: regular session vs. stateless session for inserting 50,000 rows. Document when each approach is appropriate.

Phase 3

The Spring Ecosystem Mastery

3.1 How Spring Works - Core Internals

Deep Dive: Inversion of Control

Traditional programming follows the Hollywood Principle's opposite: "We call, they answer." Your code creates objects, configures them, and manages their lifecycle. Inversion of Control (IoC) flips this: "Don't call us, we'll call you." The Spring Container acts as a sophisticated factory - you declare what you need (via annotations or configuration), and Spring handles when and how objects are created, wired together, and destroyed. This decouples your components from their dependencies' construction details, enabling testability, modularity, and AOP.

IoC Container: BeanFactory vs ApplicationContext

Spring IoC Container Comparison
FeatureBeanFactoryApplicationContext
Bean InstantiationLazy (on demand)Eager (at startup, unless @Lazy)
InternationalizationNoYes (MessageSource)
Event PropagationNoYes (ApplicationEvent)
Declarative Parent ContextsNoYes (WebApplicationContext)
AOP IntegrationManualBuilt-in
Use CaseResource-constrainedEnterprise applications (always use this)
java 
// Spring Core: Dependency Injection patterns
@Configuration
public class AppConfig {

    // @Bean: explicit factory method (use for third-party classes)
    @Bean
    public DataSource dataSource() {
        HikariConfig config = new HikariConfig();
        config.setJdbcUrl("jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb");
        config.setMaximumPoolSize(10);
        return new HikariDataSource(config);
    }

    // Constructor injection: RECOMMENDED (immutable, testable)
    @Bean
    public UserService userService(UserRepository repo, EmailService email) {
        return new UserService(repo, email);
    }
}

// Field injection: AVOID (hidden dependencies, hard to test)
@Service
public class BadService {
    @Autowired private UserRepository repo;  // DON'T DO THIS
}

// Constructor injection: CORRECT (Spring 4.3+ auto-wires single constructor)
@Service
public class GoodService {
    private final UserRepository repo;
    private final EmailService email;

    public GoodService(UserRepository repo, EmailService email) {
        this.repo = repo;
        this.email = email;
    }
}

// Record-based DTOs + immutable service (Java 16+)
@Service
public record ModernService(UserRepository repo, EmailService email) {
    // Compact constructor, immutable fields, auto-generated
}
P3S1-T2

Bean Scopes: Who Shares What?

Spring manages beans in different "scopes" — think of them as different sharing policies for objects.

ScopeWhen CreatedShared?Use Case
singletonContainer startupYes — one per containerStateless services, repositories
prototypeEvery injection/requestNo — new instance each timeStateful objects, per-request helpers
requestPer HTTP requestYes — within one requestRequest-scoped DTOs, user context
sessionPer HTTP sessionYes — within one sessionShopping cart, user preferences
applicationPer ServletContextYes — across all sessionsApp-wide configuration cache
Java
@Scope("prototype")
@Component
public class ReportGenerator {
    // New instance for every injection
}

@Scope(value = WebApplicationContext.SCOPE_REQUEST, proxyMode = ScopedProxyMode.TARGET_CLASS)
@Component
public class RequestContext {
    // Lives for one HTTP request
    private String requestId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();
}

// Injecting request-scoped bean into singleton
@Service
public class OrderService {
    @Autowired
    private RequestContext requestContext; // Spring creates a CGLIB proxy
}
Production Gotcha: @Async + @Scope("request") = IllegalStateException! When an async method runs in a different thread, the request scope is gone. Use RequestContextHolder to pass request attributes, or switch to @Scope("prototype").
Analogy for a 10-Year-Old
Imagine a school library:

Singleton = The shared encyclopedia set. Everyone uses the SAME books. If someone writes in one, everyone sees it.

Prototype = Borrowing a blank notebook. Each student gets their OWN fresh notebook. What you write in yours doesn't affect anyone else's.

Request = A hall pass for ONE bathroom trip. Valid only for that trip. When you come back, it's gone.

Session = Your locker. Yours for the whole school day. You can put things in, take things out — but the next student who uses that locker number gets a fresh empty one.

Application = The school bell schedule. Everyone sees the same schedule all year long.
Practice Homework
  1. Create a @Scope("prototype") bean that tracks how many times it's been used. Inject it into a singleton service and call it 5 times. Print the usage count each time — what do you observe?
  2. Build a request-scoped UserContext bean with a userId field. Create a controller that sets the userId from a header, then call an async service method that tries to read it. Handle the scope mismatch gracefully using RequestContextHolder.
  3. Explain why Spring uses CGLIB proxies for scoped beans injected into singletons. What would happen without the proxy?

Spring Bean Lifecycle

flowchart LR
    A[1. Instantiate] --> B[2. Populate<br/>Properties]
    B --> C[3. BeanNameAware<br/>setBeanName]
    C --> D[4. BeanFactoryAware<br/>setBeanFactory]
    D --> E[5. BPP<br/>postProcessBefore]
    E --> F[6. @PostConstruct<br/>InitializingBean]
    F --> G[7. BPP<br/>postProcessAfter]
    G --> H[8. Bean Ready]
    H --> I[9. @PreDestroy<br/>DisposableBean]
    I --> J[Destroyed]

    style A fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style B fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style C fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style D fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style E fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff
    style F fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff
    style G fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff
    style H fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style I fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ff7b72,color:#fff
    style J fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#484f58,color:#fff
java 
// Full bean lifecycle hooks
@Component
public class LifecycleBean implements
        BeanNameAware, InitializingBean, DisposableBean {

    private String beanName;

    @Override
    public void setBeanName(String name) {
        this.beanName = name;  // Step 3: Know your own bean name
    }

    @PostConstruct
    public void init() {
        // Step 6: Initialize resources (db connections, caches)
        System.out.println(beanName + " initialized");
    }

    @Override
    public void afterPropertiesSet() throws Exception {
        // Alternative to @PostConstruct (Spring-specific, less preferred)
    }

    @PreDestroy
    public void cleanup() {
        // Step 9: Release resources
        System.out.println(beanName + " destroyed");
    }

    @Override
    public void destroy() throws Exception {
        // Alternative to @PreDestroy
    }
}
P3S1-T3

Bean Lifecycle: From Class to Ready Object

Spring doesn't just call new — it orchestrates a full lifecycle with hooks for customization.

PhaseWhat HappensHook / Interface
1. InstantiationConstructor called (via reflection or CGLIB)—
2. Populate PropertiesDependencies injected (@Autowired, constructor)—
3. BeanNameAwareBean receives its namesetBeanName()
4. BeanFactoryAwareBean receives BeanFactory referencesetBeanFactory()
5. ApplicationContextAwareBean receives context referencesetApplicationContext()
6. @PostConstructCustom initialization logic@PostConstruct method
7. InitializingBeanAfter properties setafterPropertiesSet()
8. Custom initXML/JavaConfig init method@Bean(initMethod)
9. BeanPostProcessorWrap/modify bean before usepostProcessBefore/AfterInitialization
10. ReadyBean is in service—
11. @PreDestroyCleanup before shutdown@PreDestroy method
12. DisposableBeanFinal cleanupdestroy()
Java
@Component
public class PaymentGateway implements InitializingBean, DisposableBean,
        ApplicationContextAware, BeanNameAware {

    @Autowired private RestTemplate restTemplate;
    private ApplicationContext context;
    private String beanName;

    @PostConstruct
    public void warmUpConnectionPool() {
        // Called AFTER dependencies injected, BEFORE bean is used
        restTemplate.getForObject("https://api.stripe.com/health", String.class);
    }

    @Override
    public void afterPropertiesSet() {
        // Called after @PostConstruct
        System.out.println("PaymentGateway ready: " + beanName);
    }

    @Override
    public void setApplicationContext(ApplicationContext ctx) {
        this.context = ctx; // Can look up other beans programmatically
    }

    @Override
    public void setBeanName(String name) { this.beanName = name; }

    @PreDestroy
    public void closeConnections() {
        // Cleanup: close pools, flush caches
    }

    @Override
    public void destroy() { /* final cleanup */ }
}
Production Gotcha: @PostConstruct runs during context refresh — if it throws, the ENTIRE application fails to start. Use @Lazy or move heavy initialization to a background thread. Also, InitializingBean couples your code to Spring — prefer @PostConstruct for portability.
Analogy for a 10-Year-Old
Imagine building a LEGO robot:

1. Instantiation = Opening the box and dumping pieces on the table.

2. Populate Properties = The instruction manual tells you which motors (dependencies) connect to which ports.

3-5. Aware callbacks = The robot learns its name, which factory built it, and which room it's in.

6. @PostConstruct = You run a quick test — spin the wheels, check sensors — BEFORE the competition starts.

9. BeanPostProcessor = A quality inspector wraps your robot in protective foam (AOP proxy) before shipping.

11-12. @PreDestroy / destroy() = After the competition, you carefully disassemble the robot and put pieces back in the box.
Practice Homework
  1. Implement a BeanPostProcessor that logs the creation time of every bean. Print the bean name and its class when postProcessAfterInitialization is called.
  2. Create a bean with both @PostConstruct and InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet(). Add print statements to both. Which runs first? Verify with output.
  3. Write a custom DestructionAwareBeanPostProcessor that tracks bean destruction order during application shutdown. What pattern do you notice?

Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP)

Analogy: AOP is Like Airport Security

Imagine you run an airport with 50 gates. You need to add security checks to every gate. You could modify each gate's code (cross-cutting concern) - but that's error-prone. Instead, you install security checkpoints that intercept passengers before they reach any gate. AOP works the same way: you define Advices (security checks) at Pointcuts (which gates) without modifying the actual gate code. Logging, transaction management, and security are classic cross-cutting concerns.

java 
// Centralized logging aspect
@Aspect
@Component
public class LoggingAspect {

    // Pointcut: matches all methods in service package
    @Pointcut("execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))")
    public void serviceMethods() {}

    @Around("serviceMethods()")
    public Object logAround(ProceedingJoinPoint joinPoint) throws Throwable {
        long start = System.currentTimeMillis();
        log.info("Entering: {}", joinPoint.getSignature().getName());

        try {
            Object result = joinPoint.proceed();  // Execute the actual method
            long duration = System.currentTimeMillis() - start;
            log.info("Exiting: {} ({}ms)", joinPoint.getSignature().getName(), duration);
            return result;
        } catch (Exception e) {
            log.error("Exception in {}: {}", joinPoint.getSignature(), e.getMessage());
            throw e;
        }
    }
}

// Transaction aspect (Spring's @Transactional is implemented this way)
@Aspect
@Component
public class TransactionAspect {
    @Around("@annotation(transactional)")
    public Object manageTransaction(ProceedingJoinPoint jp, Transactional transactional)
            throws Throwable {
        TransactionStatus status = txManager.getTransaction(
            new DefaultTransactionDefinition());
        try {
            Object result = jp.proceed();
            txManager.commit(status);
            return result;
        } catch (RuntimeException e) {
            txManager.rollback(status);  // Rollback on unchecked exceptions
