            throw e;
        }
    }
}
AOP Advice Types
AdviceAnnotationWhen It Runs
Before@BeforeBefore method execution
After@AfterAfter method completes (success or exception)
AfterReturning@AfterReturningAfter successful return
AfterThrowing@AfterThrowingAfter exception thrown
Around@AroundSurrounds method execution (most powerful)
java 
// Proxy mechanism: Spring creates a proxy around your bean
// JDK Dynamic Proxy: used when bean implements an interface
// CGLIB: used for concrete classes (subclass-based proxy)

// Force CGLIB (when you need to proxy class methods)
@EnableAspectJAutoProxy(proxyTargetClass = true)

// Common pitfall: self-invocation bypasses proxy
@Service
public class OrderService {
    public void processOrder() {
        validateOrder();  // @Transactional IGNORED here! No proxy involved.
    }

    @Transactional  // This won't work on self-invocation!
    public void validateOrder() { }
}
Production Gotcha: Self-Invocation Problem

Spring AOP uses proxies. When processOrder() calls validateOrder() internally, it calls this.validateOrder() - the raw bean, not the proxy. The @Transactional advice on validateOrder() is completely bypassed. Solutions: (1) Inject yourself via @Autowired (ugly), (2) Use AspectJ weaving instead of Spring proxies, (3) Restructure to avoid self-invocation, or (4) Use @Transactional at the class level or entry method. For logging aspects, always use @Around on public methods called from outside the class.

P3S2-T1

Spring Events: Decoupled Communication

Spring's event system lets beans talk to each other without knowing about each other — perfect for loose coupling.

FeatureSync EventsAsync EventsTransactional Events
Annotation@EventListener@EventListener + @Async@TransactionalEventListener
ThreadSame thread as publisherSeparate thread poolSame TX, but after commit/rollback
ExceptionPropagates to publisherLogged, not propagatedOnly if TX commits
Use CaseImmediate side effectsNotifications, emailsAudit logs, cache invalidation
Java
// 1. Define the event
public class OrderPlacedEvent extends ApplicationEvent {
    private final Long orderId;
    private final BigDecimal amount;
    public OrderPlacedEvent(Object source, Long orderId, BigDecimal amount) {
        super(source); this.orderId = orderId; this.amount = amount;
    }
    // getters...
}

// 2. Publish the event
@Service
public class OrderService {
    @Autowired private ApplicationEventPublisher publisher;

    @Transactional
    public Order createOrder(OrderRequest req) {
        Order order = orderRepository.save(new Order(req));
        // Event is published NOW, but transactional listeners wait for commit
        publisher.publishEvent(new OrderPlacedEvent(this, order.getId(), order.getTotal()));
        return order;
    }
}

// 3. Listen synchronously (same thread)
@Component
public class InventoryListener {
    @EventListener
    public void onOrderPlaced(OrderPlacedEvent event) {
        inventoryService.reserveStock(event.getOrderId());
    }
}

// 4. Listen asynchronously (different thread)
@Component
public class EmailListener {
    @Async
    @EventListener
    public void sendConfirmationEmail(OrderPlacedEvent event) {
        emailService.sendOrderConfirmation(event.getOrderId());
    }
}

// 5. Listen AFTER transaction commits (most reliable for side effects)
@Component
public class AuditListener {
    @TransactionalEventListener(phase = TransactionPhase.AFTER_COMMIT)
    public void auditOrder(OrderPlacedEvent event) {
        auditLogService.record("ORDER_PLACED", event.getOrderId());
    }
    @TransactionalEventListener(phase = TransactionPhase.AFTER_ROLLBACK)
    public void auditFailure(OrderPlacedEvent event) {
        auditLogService.record("ORDER_FAILED", event.getOrderId());
    }
}
Production Gotcha: Without @Async, a slow listener blocks the publisher. With @Async, exceptions are swallowed by default — configure an AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler. For @TransactionalEventListener, the event object must be serializable if you use a persistent event store (like Spring Modulith).
Analogy for a 10-Year-Old
Imagine a school announcement system:

Synchronous Event = The principal makes an announcement over the PA system. Everyone in each classroom stops what they're doing, listens, then continues. If the announcement is long, EVERYONE waits.

Asynchronous Event = The principal sends a text message to all teachers. Teachers read it when they have a free moment. The principal doesn't wait — they move on to their next meeting.

Transactional Event = The principal says "If the field trip payment goes through, THEN send permission slips home." If the payment fails (transaction rolls back), NO permission slips are sent — even though the announcement was already made.

This is why @TransactionalEventListener(AFTER_COMMIT) is so powerful — side effects only happen when you're SURE the main action succeeded.
Practice Homework
  1. Create an OrderPlacedEvent and publish it from a service. Build TWO listeners: one sync (updates inventory) and one async (sends email). Verify they run on different threads using Thread.currentThread().getName().
  2. Implement a @TransactionalEventListener(AFTER_COMMIT) that writes to an audit table. Throw a RuntimeException in the main transaction and verify the audit record is NOT created.
  3. Configure a custom ThreadPoolTaskExecutor for async events. Set core pool size to 2, max to 10, and queue capacity to 100. Test what happens when you publish 150 events rapidly.

3.2 Important Concepts & Modules

Spring MVC Architecture

flowchart LR
    Client -->|HTTP Request| Dispatcher[DispatcherServlet]
    Dispatcher -->|HandlerMapping| Controller[Controller]
    Controller -->|Model + View/JSON| Service[Service Layer]
    Service -->|Result| Repository[Repository]
    Repository -->|Data| Service
    Service -->|Result| Controller
    Controller -->|HTTP Response| Client

    style Client fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style Dispatcher fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style Controller fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style Service fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff
    style Repository fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffa657,color:#fff
java 
// Spring MVC Controller (Spring Boot 3.x)
@RestController  // @Controller + @ResponseBody (returns JSON, not views)
@RequestMapping("/api/users")
@Validated       // Enable @Valid method parameter validation
public class UserController {

    private final UserService service;

    @GetMapping("/{id}")
    public ResponseEntity<UserDto> getUser(@PathVariable Long id) {
        return service.findById(id)
            .map(u -> ResponseEntity.ok(UserMapper.toDto(u)))
            .orElse(ResponseEntity.notFound().build());
    }

    @PostMapping
    public ResponseEntity<UserDto> createUser(
            @RequestBody @Valid CreateUserRequest request,
            UriComponentsBuilder uriBuilder) {
        UserDto created = service.create(request);
        URI location = uriBuilder.path("/api/users/{id}").build(created.id());
        return ResponseEntity.created(location).body(created);
    }

    @GetMapping
    public Page<UserDto> listUsers(
            @RequestParam(defaultValue = "0") int page,
            @RequestParam(defaultValue = "20") int size,
            @RequestParam(defaultValue = "id,asc") String sort) {
        return service.findAll(PageRequest.of(page, size, Sort.by(sort)));
    }
}
P3S2-T2

Spring MVC: Request Journey from Browser to Database

Every HTTP request passes through a chain of components. Understanding this chain is key to debugging and optimizing web applications.

ComponentRoleCommon Customizations
DispatcherServletFront controller — receives ALL requestsCustom servlet registration, async support
HandlerMappingFinds the right controller methodCustom path matching, interceptors
HandlerAdapterInvokes the controller methodCustom argument resolvers
ControllerBusiness logic + response building@RestController, @ControllerAdvice
HandlerExceptionResolverCatches and converts exceptions@ExceptionHandler, @ControllerAdvice
ViewResolverRenders the response (JSON/XML/HTML)Custom message converters
Java
// Custom HandlerInterceptor — runs before/after controller
@Component
public class RequestTimingInterceptor implements HandlerInterceptor {
    private static final Logger log = LoggerFactory.getLogger(RequestTimingInterceptor.class);

    @Override
    public boolean preHandle(HttpServletRequest req, HttpServletResponse res, Object handler) {
        req.setAttribute("startTime", System.currentTimeMillis());
        return true; // false = abort request
    }

    @Override
    public void afterCompletion(HttpServletRequest req, HttpServletResponse res,
                                  Object handler, Exception ex) {
        long start = (Long) req.getAttribute("startTime");
        long duration = System.currentTimeMillis() - start;
        log.info("{} {} took {}ms", req.getMethod(), req.getRequestURI(), duration);
    }
}

// Register interceptor
@Configuration
public class WebConfig implements WebMvcConfigurer {
    @Autowired private RequestTimingInterceptor interceptor;

    @Override
    public void addInterceptors(InterceptorRegistry registry) {
        registry.addInterceptor(interceptor)
                .addPathPatterns("/api/**")
                .excludePathPatterns("/api/health");
    }
}

// Global exception handling
@RestControllerAdvice
public class GlobalExceptionHandler {
    @ExceptionHandler(MethodArgumentNotValidException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<Map<String, Object>> handleValidationErrors(MethodArgumentNotValidException ex) {
        Map<String, String> errors = ex.getBindingResult().getFieldErrors().stream()
            .collect(Collectors.toMap(
                FieldError::getField,
                FieldError::getDefaultMessage
            ));
        return ResponseEntity.badRequest().body(Map.of("errors", errors));
    }
}
Production Gotcha: @ControllerAdvice without @RestControllerAdvice tries to resolve views for error responses — you'll get 404s for JSON APIs. Always use @RestControllerAdvice for REST APIs. Also, exception handlers in a @ControllerAdvice only handle exceptions from controllers in the same package or sub-packages unless you specify @ControllerAdvice(basePackages).
Analogy for a 10-Year-Old
Imagine ordering food at a drive-thru:

DispatcherServlet = The drive-thru entrance. ALL cars must enter here. No exceptions.

HandlerMapping = The menu board with numbers. "Order #3 goes to the burger station, Order #7 goes to the drink station."

HandlerInterceptor = The person at the window who checks your ID before taking your order. They can say "no" and send you away.

Controller = The cook who actually makes your food. They follow the recipe (business logic) and hand you the meal (response).

HandlerExceptionResolver = The manager who steps in when the cook burns the food. Instead of crashing the whole restaurant, they say "Sorry, here's a refund" (graceful error response).

ViewResolver = The packaging. Do you want a bag, a box, or a tray? For APIs, this is JSON packaging.
Practice Homework
  1. Create a custom HandlerInterceptor that logs request method, URI, and duration. Register it for all /api/** paths except /api/public/**.
  2. Build a @RestControllerAdvice that handles EntityNotFoundException with HTTP 404 and IllegalArgumentException with HTTP 400. Include a consistent error response format with timestamp, status, error, and message fields.
  3. Implement a custom HandlerMethodArgumentResolver that resolves a @CurrentUser annotation into the authenticated user object from a JWT token in the Authorization header.

Spring Security: Stateless JWT Authentication

java 
// Spring Security 6.x Configuration (Java DSL, no WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter)
@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
public class SecurityConfig {

    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http,
                                           JwtAuthFilter jwtFilter) throws Exception {
        http
            .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())  // Stateless: CSRF not needed
            .sessionManagement(session ->
                session.sessionCreationPolicy(SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS))
            .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
                .requestMatchers("/api/auth/**").permitAll()
                .requestMatchers("/api/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")
                .anyRequest().authenticated()
            )
            .addFilterBefore(jwtFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class);
        return http.build();
    }
}

// JWT Filter: validates token on every request
@Component
public class JwtAuthFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter {

    @Override
    protected void doFilterInternal(HttpServletRequest req,
                                    HttpServletResponse res,
                                    FilterChain chain) throws ServletException, IOException {
        String token = extractToken(req);
        if (token != null && jwtUtil.isValid(token)) {
            String username = jwtUtil.extractUsername(token);
            UserDetails user = userDetailsService.loadUserByUsername(username);

            UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken auth =
                new UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken(
                    user, null, user.getAuthorities());
            auth.setDetails(new WebAuthenticationDetailsSource().buildDetails(req));

            // Set authentication in SecurityContext for this request
            SecurityContextHolder.getContext().setAuthentication(auth);
        }
        chain.doFilter(req, res);  // Continue filter chain
    }
}

Spring Data JPA

java 
// Repository: Spring auto-generates queries from method names
public interface UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Long> {

    // Derived query: Spring generates SELECT ... WHERE username = ? AND active = true
    Optional<User> findByUsernameAndActiveTrue(String username);

    // Custom JPQL query
    @Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.lastLogin < :date AND u.active = true")
    List<User> findInactiveSince(@Param("date") LocalDateTime date);

    // Native SQL for complex queries
    @Query(value = "SELECT u.*, COUNT(o.id) as order_count " +
                  "FROM users u LEFT JOIN orders o ON u.id = o.user_id " +
                  "GROUP BY u.id HAVING order_count > :minOrders", nativeQuery = true)
    List<UserWithOrderCount> findPowerUsers(@Param("minOrders") int minOrders);

    // Modifying query with clear persistence context
    @Modifying(clearAutomatically = true, flushAutomatically = true)
    @Query("UPDATE User u SET u.lastLogin = CURRENT_TIMESTAMP WHERE u.id = :id")
    int updateLastLogin(@Param("id") Long id);
}
P3S2-T3

@Transactional Propagation: The Transaction Matrix

Propagation defines what happens when a @Transactional method calls another @Transactional method. Seven levels, seven behaviors.

PropagationExisting TX?BehaviorWhen to Use
REQUIRED (default)YesJoin existing TXAlmost everything
REQUIREDNoCreate new TX—
REQUIRES_NEWYesSuspend existing, create NEWAudit logs, notifications
REQUIRES_NEWNoCreate new TX—
NESTEDYesCreate savepointPartial rollback scenarios
SUPPORTSYesJoin existing TXRead-only queries
SUPPORTSNoExecute non-transactionallyOptional transactional behavior
MANDATORYYesJoin existing TXInternal service methods
MANDATORYNoThrow exceptionEnforce caller has TX
NOT_SUPPORTEDYesSuspend TX, run withoutReporting queries
NEVERYesThrow exceptionCache operations
Java
@Service
public class OrderService {
    @Autowired private PaymentService paymentService;
    @Autowired private AuditService auditService;
    @Autowired private OrderRepository orderRepo;

    @Transactional
    public void placeOrder(OrderRequest req) {
        // In TX #1
        Order order = orderRepo.save(new Order(req));

        // paymentService uses REQUIRED — joins TX #1
        // If payment fails, order is ALSO rolled back
        paymentService.processPayment(order.getId(), req.getAmount());

        // auditService uses REQUIRES_NEW — creates TX #2
        // Even if TX #1 rolls back, audit record is COMMITTED
        auditService.logOrderCreated(order.getId());
    }
}

@Service
public class AuditService {
    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void logOrderCreated(Long orderId) {
        auditRepo.save(new AuditLog("ORDER_CREATED", orderId));
    }
}

@Service
public class ReportService {
    // Run without TX — better for long-running read queries
    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.NOT_SUPPORTED)
    public List<OrderReport> generateMonthlyReport(YearMonth month) {
        return orderRepo.findByMonth(month); // No TX overhead
    }
}
Production Gotcha: REQUIRES_NEW creates a PHYSICAL database connection — it doesn't reuse the same connection. In high-concurrency systems, this can exhaust your connection pool. Also, NESTED (savepoints) only works with JDBC 3.0+ drivers and is NOT supported by all JPA providers (Hibernate supports it, EclipseLink doesn't). Test your specific database + JPA combo.
Analogy for a 10-Year-Old
Imagine a bank with safety deposit boxes (transactions):

REQUIRED = "Use my box if there's one open, otherwise get a new one." You and your friend share the same box. If either of you messes up, BOTH items are removed.

REQUIRES_NEW = "I ALWAYS need my own private box." Even if you already have a box, you PAUSE using it, open a NEW one, do your thing, close it, then resume your original box. Your new box is independent.

NESTED = "Put a divider in my current box." If the section behind the divider goes wrong, only THAT section is thrown away. The rest of the box stays.

MANDATORY = "I refuse to work unless you already have a box open." If you try to use this service without a box, they kick you out (throw exception).

NEVER = "I refuse to work if there's ANY box open." Perfect for things that shouldn't interfere with banking — like checking the weather.
Practice Homework
  1. Create a service with @Transactional (REQUIRED) that calls two methods: one with REQUIRED and one with REQUIRES_NEW. Throw an exception after the REQUIRES_NEW call. Verify: is the REQUIRES_NEW data committed or rolled back?
  2. Implement a MANDATORY method that updates inventory. Call it both WITH and WITHOUT a surrounding @Transactional. What exceptions do you get?
  3. Write a test that demonstrates NESTED behavior: insert 3 records, throw after the 2nd, catch the exception. How many records remain? Compare with REQUIRED behavior.

3.3 Spring Boot Revolution

Spring vs. Spring Boot: The Configuration Evolution

Deep Dive: Convention Over Configuration

Before Spring Boot (pre-2014), a Spring developer spent hours wiring XML configurations or JavaConfig classes. Need a database connection? Write 50 lines of DataSource configuration. Need a web server? Download Tomcat, configure it separately, and deploy a WAR file. Spring Boot flipped this paradigm with auto-configuration: it detects what's on your classpath and configures sensible defaults automatically. Have spring-boot-starter-data-jpa on the classpath? Spring Boot auto-creates a DataSource, EntityManagerFactory, and TransactionManager. It's opinionated but customizable - every auto-configuration can be overridden with explicit beans or application.properties.

java 
// Before Spring Boot: 50+ lines of JavaConfig
@Configuration
public class LegacyDataConfig {
    @Bean public DataSource dataSource() { /* 20 lines */ }
    @Bean public LocalContainerEntityManagerFactoryBean emf() { /* 15 lines */ }
    @Bean public PlatformTransactionManager txManager() { /* 10 lines */ }
}

// With Spring Boot: Just add dependencies, ZERO config needed
// application.properties:
spring.datasource.url=jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb
spring.datasource.username=admin
spring.datasource.password=secret
spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=20

// Embedded server: run as java -jar, no external Tomcat needed
// The JAR contains an embedded Tomcat that starts automatically
Key Spring Boot Auto-Configuration Starters
StarterWhat It Auto-ConfiguresKey Properties
spring-boot-starter-webTomcat, Spring MVC, Jacksonserver.port, server.servlet.context-path
spring-boot-starter-data-jpaHikariCP, Hibernate, TransactionManagerspring.datasource.*, spring.jpa.*
spring-boot-starter-securitySecurityFilterChain, PasswordEncoderspring.security.*
spring-boot-starter-testJUnit 5, Mockito, TestRestTemplatespring.test.*
spring-boot-starter-actuatorHealth, Metrics, Info endpointsmanagement.endpoints.*

Spring Boot Actuator: Production Observability

sql 
// application.yml - Actuator configuration
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: health,info,metrics,prometheus,env,configprops
      base-path: /admin  # Don't expose on public URL!
  endpoint:
    health:
      show-details: when_authorized
      show-components: always
  metrics:
    export:
      prometheus:
        enabled: true  # Scraped by Prometheus for Grafana dashboards

// Custom health indicator
@Component
public class DatabaseHealthIndicator implements HealthIndicator {
    private final DataSource dataSource;

    @Override
    public Health health() {
        try (Connection conn = dataSource.getConnection()) {
            if (conn.isValid(5)) {
                return Health.up()
                    .withDetail("database", "reachable")
                    .withDetail("validationQuery", "SELECT 1")
                    .build();
            }
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            return Health.down()
                .withException(e)
                .build();
        }
        return Health.down().build();
    }
}
P3S3-T1

Spring Boot Actuator: Production Observability

Actuator provides production-ready endpoints for monitoring, metrics, and health checks. Micrometer is the metrics facade that powers them.

EndpointWhat It ShowsProduction Risk
/actuator/healthApp status + component checksLow — expose with details=when_authorized
/actuator/metricsJVM, HTTP, datasource metricsMedium — can reveal internal state
/actuator/prometheusMetrics in Prometheus formatMedium — secure with network policies
/actuator/envEnvironment properties, some maskedHIGH — can leak secrets
/actuator/heapdumpJVM memory snapshotCRITICAL — contains ALL in-memory data
/actuator/threaddumpAll thread stack tracesMedium — reveals code structure
/actuator/loggersLog levels + ability to change themMedium — can enable debug logging
YAML
# application.yml — PRODUCTION HARDENING
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: health,info,metrics,prometheus  # NEVER expose env, heapdump
      base-path: /admin                          # Hide behind non-standard path
  endpoint:
    health:
      show-details: when_authorized              # Only show details to authenticated users
      show-components: when_authorized
    prometheus:
      enabled: true
  metrics:
    tags:
      application: ${spring.application.name}
      environment: ${ENVIRONMENT:production}
    distribution:
      percentiles-histogram:
        http.server.requests: true               # Enable histogram for latency percentiles
      slo:
        http.server.requests: 50ms,100ms,200ms,500ms,1s,5s

# Custom health indicator
@Component
public class DatabaseHealthIndicator implements HealthIndicator {
    @Autowired private DataSource dataSource;

    @Override
    public Health health() {
        try (Connection conn = dataSource.getConnection()) {
            if (conn.isValid(1)) {
                return Health.up()
                    .withDetail("validationQuery", "SELECT 1")
                    .withDetail("responseTime", "1s")
                    .build();
            }
        } catch (SQLException e) {
            return Health.down()
                .withException(e)
                .build();
        }
        return Health.down().build();
    }
}

# Custom Micrometer metric
@Component
public class OrderMetrics {
    private final Counter orderCounter;
    private final Timer orderTimer;

    public OrderMetrics(MeterRegistry registry) {
        this.orderCounter = Counter.builder("orders.placed")
            .description("Total orders placed")
            .tag("currency", "USD")
            .register(registry);
        this.orderTimer = Timer.builder("orders.processing.time")
            .description("Order processing duration")
            .publishPercentiles(0.5, 0.95, 0.99)
            .register(registry);
    }

    public void recordOrderPlaced() { orderCounter.increment(); }
    public void recordProcessingTime(long millis) { orderTimer.record(millis, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS); }
}
Production Gotcha: In 2024, Volkswagen leaked 9TB of GPS data because an /actuator/heapdump endpoint was exposed to the internet without authentication. ALWAYS: (1) Restrict exposed endpoints to health+metrics+prometheus only, (2) Put Actuator behind a separate port or network segment, (3) Use Spring Security to require authentication, (4) Never expose heapdump or env externally. Use Kubernetes NetworkPolicies to restrict which pods can reach Actuator.
Analogy for a 10-Year-Old
Imagine your body has a control panel on your back:

/health = A simple green/yellow/red light. "Are you alive?" Safe to show anyone.

/metrics = Your heart rate, body temperature, and how fast you're running. Useful for doctors (DevOps), but a bit personal.

/env = Your home address, parent's phone numbers, and locker combination. DANGEROUS if strangers see it.

/heapdump = A complete photocopy of your brain. EVERY thought, memory, and secret password is in there. This is the MOST dangerous endpoint — it's how Volkswagen leaked 9TB of data!

Rule: Only let your doctor (monitoring system) see the health light and basic metrics. Lock everything else in a safe.
Practice Homework
  1. Configure Actuator to expose ONLY health, info, and prometheus endpoints. Change the base path to /sys. Verify that /actuator/env returns 404.
  2. Create a custom HealthIndicator that checks connectivity to Redis. Return Health.up() with response time details if connected, Health.down() with the exception if not.
  3. Implement a Micrometer Timer that measures payment processing time. Record 100 simulated payments and query /actuator/metrics/orders.processing.time to see p50, p95, and p99 percentiles.

Advanced: Custom Auto-Configuration

java 
// Creating your own Spring Boot Starter library
// 1. Create @Configuration with @ConditionalOnClass
@Configuration
@ConditionalOnClass(MyLibraryClient.class)
@EnableConfigurationProperties(MyLibraryProperties.class)
public class MyLibraryAutoConfiguration {

    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnMissingBean  // Only if user didn't define their own
    public MyLibraryClient myLibraryClient(MyLibraryProperties props) {
        return MyLibraryClient.builder()
            .apiKey(props.getApiKey())
            .timeout(props.getTimeout())
            .build();
    }
}

// 2. Configuration properties with IDE autocomplete support
@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "my.library")
public record MyLibraryProperties(
    @NotBlank String apiKey,
    @Min(1000) int timeoutMillis
) {}

// 3. META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports
// com.mylibrary.autoconfigure.MyLibraryAutoConfiguration

// Usage in applications:
// my.library.api-key=sk-abc123
// my.library.timeout-millis=5000
P3S3-T2

Building Custom Spring Boot Starters

Spring Boot's auto-configuration lets you create reusable libraries that "just work" when added to a project. Understanding this unlocks library authoring.

ComponentPurposeKey Annotation
Auto-Configuration ClassDefines beans conditionally@AutoConfiguration
Properties ClassType-safe configuration binding@ConfigurationProperties
Imports FileRegisters auto-config with Spring BootMETA-INF/spring/...AutoConfiguration.imports
Starter POMPulls in autoconfigure + dependenciesMaven dependency
Java
// 1. Configuration Properties — type-safe config
@ConfigurationProperties(prefix = "app.http-client")
public class HttpClientProperties {
    private boolean enabled = true;
    private Duration connectTimeout = Duration.ofSeconds(5);
    private Duration readTimeout = Duration.ofSeconds(30);
    private int maxRetries = 3;
    // getters/setters...
}

// 2. Auto-Configuration — conditional bean creation
@AutoConfiguration
@ConditionalOnClass(RestTemplate.class)
@ConditionalOnProperty(prefix = "app.http-client", name = "enabled", havingValue = "true", matchIfMissing = true)
@EnableConfigurationProperties(HttpClientProperties.class)
@AutoConfigureAfter(RestTemplateAutoConfiguration.class)
public class HttpClientAutoConfiguration {

    @Bean
    @ConditionalOnMissingBean  // Don't override user's bean
    public RestTemplate resilientRestTemplate(
            RestTemplateBuilder builder,
            HttpClientProperties props) {
        return builder
            .setConnectTimeout(props.getConnectTimeout())
            .setReadTimeout(props.getReadTimeout())
            .additionalInterceptors(new RetryInterceptor(props.getMaxRetries()))
            .build();
    }
}

// 3. Register in META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports
// com.example.autoconfigure.HttpClientAutoConfiguration

// 4. Starter POM — minimal, just pulls in dependencies
/*
<dependency>
    <groupId>com.example</groupId>
    <artifactId>my-http-client-spring-boot-starter</artifactId>
    <version>1.0.0</version>
</dependency>
*/

// Conditional annotations reference:
// @ConditionalOnClass — class exists on classpath
// @ConditionalOnMissingBean — no bean of this type yet
// @ConditionalOnProperty — property has specific value
// @ConditionalOnWebApplication — running in web context
// @ConditionalOnExpression — SpEL expression evaluates to true
Production Gotcha: Auto-configuration classes MUST NOT be in a package scanned by component scanning (typically the main application package or below). Put them in a separate package like com.yourcompany.yourlibrary.autoconfigure. If scanned, Spring may create beans twice or in the wrong order. Also, Spring Boot 2.7+ uses META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports — the old spring.factories still works but is deprecated.
Analogy for a 10-Year-Old
Imagine you're selling a "Smart Desk Lamp" kit:

Properties Class = The instruction manual's settings page: "Brightness: 1-10, Color: warm/cool, Timer: 0-120 minutes." Users fill this out to customize their lamp.

Auto-Configuration = The lamp's brain. When someone plugs it in, it checks: "Is there a power outlet?" (@ConditionalOnClass) → Yes. "Did the user already install a different bulb?" (@ConditionalOnMissingBean) → No. "Is the switch turned on?" (@ConditionalOnProperty) → Yes. OK, I'll turn on the default warm light at brightness 5.

Imports File = The product barcode. When someone buys your lamp, the store scans the barcode so the inventory system knows "this product exists and should be activated when purchased."

Starter POM = The box. It contains the lamp, the manual, AND the right power cable. One box, everything needed.

Key Rule: The lamp's brain shouldn't be in the same drawer as the user's other electronics — it might get confused with their existing smart home hub!
Practice Homework
  1. Create a custom starter with @ConfigurationProperties for a feature flag system. Support enabled, defaultValue, and refreshInterval properties.
  2. Build an auto-configuration class that creates a FeatureFlagService bean ONLY when app.features.enabled=true AND no existing FeatureFlagService bean exists. Register it in the new AutoConfiguration.imports file.
  3. Test your starter: create a sample Spring Boot app, add your starter as a dependency, and verify the bean is created (or not) based on properties. Use @SpringBootTest with different property profiles.
Production Gotcha: Auto-Configuration Ordering

Auto-configurations have a specific order defined by @AutoConfigureBefore, @AutoConfigureAfter, and @AutoConfigureOrder. If your custom starter's beans aren't being created, check that: (1) The META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports file exists with the correct path, (2) Your starter JAR is on the classpath, (3) The @ConditionalOnClass requirement is satisfied, (4) No @ConditionalOnMissingBean is preventing creation. Use --debug or set debug=true in properties to see a detailed auto-configuration report showing which configurations matched and which didn't.

GraalVM Native Images

java 
// Native Image compilation for near-instant startup and low memory
// 1. Add GraalVM Native plugin to pom.xml
// 2. Mark classes for reflection with @RegisterForReflection

@RegisterForReflection({User.class, Order.class})  // Needed for serialization frameworks
@SpringBootApplication
public class NativeApplication {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(NativeApplication.class, args);
    }
}

// Build native executable:
// ./mvnw native:compile -Pnative
//
// Results:
// - JVM app: ~3 second startup, ~200MB RAM
// - Native:  ~50ms startup, ~60MB RAM
// - Binary size: ~80MB (includes everything needed)

// Runtime hints for dynamic features
public class RuntimeHintsRegistrar implements org.springframework.aot.hint.RuntimeHintsRegistrar {
    @Override
    public void registerHints(RuntimeHints hints, ClassLoader classLoader) {
        hints.resources().registerPattern("*.xml");  // Resource files
    }
}
P3S3-T3

GraalVM Native Images: Spring Boot at Lightspeed

GraalVM compiles Spring Boot apps to native binaries — startup in milliseconds, tiny memory footprint. But it requires AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compilation and explicit reflection configuration.

AspectJVM ModeNative Image
Startup Time3-10 seconds50-200 milliseconds
Memory Footprint200-500MB20-80MB
ReflectionDynamic, at runtimeMust be declared at build time
Proxy ClassesGenerated at runtimeMust be pre-generated
Build TimeFast (seconds)Slow (minutes — full static analysis)
DebuggingFull JVM debuggingLimited — no JVMTI agents
Best ForLong-running servicesServerless, CLI tools, edge computing
Java
// 1. Add GraalVM plugin to pom.xml
/*
<plugin>
    <groupId>org.graalvm.buildtools</groupId>
    <artifactId>native-maven-plugin</artifactId>
</plugin>
*/

// 2. Register reflection hints for classes used dynamically
public class MyRuntimeHints implements RuntimeHintsRegistrar {
    @Override
    public void registerHints(RuntimeHints hints, ClassLoader classLoader) {
        // Register for reflection
        hints.reflection().registerType(MyDto.class,
            MemberCategory.INVOKE_PUBLIC_CONSTRUCTORS,
            MemberCategory.INVOKE_PUBLIC_METHODS);

        // Register resource files
        hints.resources().registerPattern("messages/*.properties");

        // Register serialization
        hints.serialization().registerType(MySerializable.class);

        // Register proxy interfaces
        hints.proxies().registerJdkProxy(MyInterface.class);
    }
}

// 3. Import the hints registrar
@Configuration
@ImportRuntimeHints(MyRuntimeHints.class)
public class AppConfig { }

// 4. Build native image
// mvn -Pnative native:compile
// ./target/myapp — starts in ~100ms!

// Spring Boot 3.x AOT processing happens automatically during build:
// - Analyzes @Component classes at build time
// - Generates proxy classes ahead of time
// - Creates reflection config for Jackson, JPA, etc.
// - But YOU must hint custom dynamic code
Production Gotcha: Native images CANNOT use runtime bytecode generation (CGLIB proxies, Hibernate lazy loading without hints, some AOP). Spring Boot 3.x handles most framework classes automatically, but YOUR custom dynamic code needs explicit hints. Also, native images don't support Java Flight Recorder, JVMTI agents, or some JVM-specific monitoring. Profile your app in JVM mode first, then port to native. Use --trace-class-initialization to debug build-time issues.
Analogy for a 10-Year-Old
Imagine two ways to prepare for a spelling bee:

JVM Mode = Bringing a dictionary to the competition. You can look up ANY word on the spot — very flexible! But it takes time to flip through pages, and the dictionary is heavy to carry.

Native Image = Memorizing ONLY the words you'll be tested on before the competition. You can't look up new words, but you answer INSTANTLY and you don't need to carry anything heavy.

Runtime Hints = Your study list. You must tell the teacher (GraalVM compiler) EXACTLY which words to memorize. If you forget to add "onomatopoeia" to your list, you won't know it at the competition — even if it's in the dictionary.

Trade-off: The study session (build) takes hours, but the competition (runtime) is blazing fast. Perfect for quick-fire rounds (serverless), but overkill for a 3-hour marathon (long-running server).
Practice Homework
  1. Build a simple Spring Boot 3.x app with one REST endpoint. Add the GraalVM native plugin and compile to native image. Measure startup time and memory usage vs JVM mode.
  2. Create a custom DTO with Jackson annotations. Build a native image WITHOUT RuntimeHints. What error do you get? Now add the hints and rebuild — does it work?
  3. Research: What Spring Boot features DON'T work in native images? List 5 limitations and their workarounds (e.g., dynamic property loading, JSP, some AOP patterns).
🧠
Spring IoC Quiz
Which injection type is RECOMMENDED in Spring Boot and why?
A Field injection - easiest to write with @Autowired
B Setter injection - allows optional dependencies
C Constructor injection - immutable, testable, and required dependencies
D Method injection - most flexible for runtime changes
Phase 4

Next-Gen Java: Spring AI

4.1 Generative AI Foundations & ChatClient API

Deep Dive: Why Spring AI Exists

Large Language Models (LLMs) like GPT-4, Claude, and Llama have become commoditized infrastructure - similar to databases or message queues. Every enterprise application will soon have AI features. But integrating AI is messy: each provider has different APIs, authentication schemes, and response formats. Spring AI is the Spring team's answer: a portable abstraction layer that lets you swap OpenAI for Anthropic or local Ollama models with a single configuration change. It brings Spring's philosophy of "portable service abstractions" to the AI world, just like Spring Data unified database access.

Subtopic 1: ChatClient Fluent API

The ChatClient is the front door to every Spring AI interaction. It exposes a fluent builder API that mirrors WebClient and RestClient - you chain .prompt(), attach a system message (the model's standing instructions, set once per client or per call) and a user message (the per-request question), inject dynamic parameters with {placeholder} templating, and terminate the chain with .call() for a synchronous response or .stream() for a reactive Flux<ChatResponse> that emits tokens as they arrive. Because the entire client is built from a provider-agnostic ChatModel, you can register multiple ChatClient.Builder beans - one per provider profile - and swap between OpenAI, Anthropic, and Ollama using Spring's standard @Qualifier injection or configuration profiles, with zero changes to business logic.

java 
// Spring AI 1.0+: ChatClient.Builder beans, one per provider profile
@Configuration
public class AiClientConfig {

    @Bean @Qualifier("openAiClient")
    public ChatClient openAiChatClient(OpenAiChatModel model) {
        return ChatClient.builder(model)
            .defaultSystem("You are a precise enterprise assistant. Cite sources when possible.")
            .defaultOptions(OpenAiChatOptions.builder().temperature(0.4).build())
            .build();
    }

    @Bean @Qualifier("anthropicClient")
    public ChatClient anthropicChatClient(AnthropicChatModel model) {
        return ChatClient.builder(model)
            .defaultSystem("You are a precise enterprise assistant. Cite sources when possible.")
            .build();
    }
}

@Service
public class AiService {

    private final ChatClient chatClient;

    public AiService(@Qualifier("anthropicClient") ChatClient chatClient) {
        this.chatClient = chatClient;   // Swap the qualifier, nothing else changes
    }

    // Synchronous call with dynamic parameters
    public String summarize(String document, String tone) {
        return chatClient.prompt()
            .system(s -> s.text("Respond in a {tone} tone.").param("tone", tone))
            .user("Summarize in 3 bullet points:\n\n" + document)
            .call()
            .content();
    }

    // Reactive streaming: token-by-token Flux<ChatResponse> with metadata
    public Flux<String> streamSummary(String document) {
        return chatClient.prompt()
            .user(document)
            .stream()
            .chatResponse()                      // Flux<ChatResponse> - full metadata per chunk
            .map(resp -> resp.getResult().getOutput().getText());
    }
}
Analogy: The Embassy Translator

Think of ChatClient as a professional translator stationed inside an international embassy. You never speak the foreign government's native bureaucratic dialect (OpenAI's raw REST schema, Anthropic's message blocks, Ollama's local protocol) - you just hand the translator your request in plain, fluent language: "tell them this, using this tone, with these details filled in." The translator converts it into whatever dialect the embassy on duty speaks, walks it through the door, and brings the response back in your language. Swap which embassy is on duty (OpenAI today, Anthropic tomorrow) and your instructions to the translator never change - only the @Qualifier you hand them at the door.

Deep Insight: ChatClient Internals & Multi-Provider Architecture
Homework: Build a Provider-Agnostic Chat Gateway

Build: A @RestController exposing POST /api/chat that accepts { "provider": "openai"|"anthropic", "message": "...", "stream": boolean }. Wire two ChatClient beans behind qualifiers, select one at runtime via a Map<String, ChatClient> injected by Spring (bean name as key), and return either a JSON body (non-streaming) or a text/event-stream (SSE) response (streaming) using Flux<String> as the return type.

Correct output: A non-streaming request returns {"provider":"anthropic","content":"...","tokensUsed":142} in under your configured latency budget (e.g. p99 < 4s for a 200-token response). A streaming request via curl -N shows tokens arriving incrementally, not all at once.

Edge case: Simulate a provider returning a malformed/empty ChatResponse (mock the ChatModel to throw NonTransientAiException). Your gateway must catch it, fall back to the secondary provider automatically, and log which provider actually answered - without the caller ever seeing a stack trace.

Subtopic 2: Structured Output Converters

Most enterprise use cases don't want prose back - they want a typed Java object. Spring AI's structured output converters solve this without any custom parsing code. When you call .entity(MyRecord.class), a BeanOutputConverter reflects over the target type, generates a JSON Schema describing its exact shape, and silently appends format instructions to your prompt ("Respond only with JSON matching this schema: {...}") before the request ever leaves your application. When the response comes back, the same converter parses the raw text into your POJO using Jackson, throwing a structured exception if the model violated the schema. MapOutputConverter and ListOutputConverter do the same thing for looser, untyped collections when you don't want to define a record just for one call.

java 
public record TicketTriage(String category, Integer priority, List<String> suggestedTags) {}

@Service
public class TriageService {

    private final ChatClient chatClient;

    // BeanOutputConverter does the schema injection + parsing for you
    public TicketTriage triage(String ticketBody) {
        return chatClient.prompt()
            .system("You triage support tickets. Be strict about the schema.")
            .user(ticketBody)
            .call()
            .entity(TicketTriage.class);   // auto-injects JSON schema + auto-parses
    }

    // Manual converter use - useful when you need the raw schema string for logging/debugging
    public List<String> extractKeywords(String text) {
        var converter = new ListOutputConverter(new DefaultConversionService());
        return chatClient.prompt()
            .user(u -> u.text("Extract 5 keywords from: {text}\n{format}")
                .param("text", text)
                .param("format", converter.getFormat()))   // schema instructions, explicit
            .call()
            .content() == null ? List.of() : converter.convert(chatClient.prompt().user(text).call().content());
    }
}
Analogy: The Legal Clerk's Intake Form

A legal clerk never accepts a rambling verbal story at the courthouse window - they hand you a numbered intake form and say "fill in exactly these boxes, nothing else." BeanOutputConverter is that clerk: before your question ever reaches the judge (the LLM), it staples a blank form to it describing precisely which boxes must be filled (your record's fields) and in what format. When the answer comes back, the clerk doesn't read it aloud - they file it directly into the matching folder (your Java object). If the response skips a box or scribbles outside the lines, the clerk rejects the whole filing rather than guessing what you meant.

Deep Insight: Schema Injection & Token Cost
Homework: Resilient Structured Triage Endpoint

Build: A TriageController wrapping the TicketTriage service above, behind POST /api/triage. Add a @Retryable(retryFor = StructuredOutputParsingException.class, maxAttempts = 2) around the call, where the retry re-prompts with: "Your last response did not match the schema. Return ONLY valid JSON."

Correct output: {"category":"billing","priority":2,"suggestedTags":["refund","invoice"]} - always valid against the record's shape, never wrapped in markdown fences.

Edge case: Force the model (via a low-quality prompt or a mocked response) to return JSON wrapped in ```json ... ``` fences or with trailing prose after the object. Your retry/cleanup logic must strip the fences and parse successfully on attempt 2, and emit a structured log warning (not a crash) on the first failed attempt.

Subtopic 3: Chat Memory & Conversation Context

LLMs are stateless by default - every call is a blank slate unless you re-supply the conversation. Spring AI's ChatMemory abstraction makes conversation history a pluggable concern, the same way Repository abstracts persistence. MessageWindowChatMemory (the in-memory implementation) keeps a sliding window of the last N messages per conversationId, evicting the oldest once the window fills - cheap, but lost on restart. For production, you swap in a JdbcChatMemoryRepository or Redis-backed repository so history survives restarts and scales across instances, while still respecting the same windowing/eviction contract so your token budget stays predictable regardless of backend.

java 
@Configuration
public class MemoryConfig {

    // Dev: in-memory sliding window, 20 messages per conversation
    @Bean @Profile("dev")
    public ChatMemory inMemoryChatMemory() {
        return MessageWindowChatMemory.builder().maxMessages(20).build();
    }

    // Prod: JDBC-backed, survives restarts, shared across instances
    @Bean @Profile("prod")
    public ChatMemory jdbcChatMemory(JdbcChatMemoryRepository repository) {
        return MessageWindowChatMemory.builder()
            .chatMemoryRepository(repository)
            .maxMessages(20)
            .build();
    }
}

@Service
public class ConversationalAgent {

    private final ChatClient chatClient;
    private final ChatMemory chatMemory;

    public String chat(String conversationId, String userMessage) {
        chatMemory.add(conversationId, new UserMessage(userMessage));

        String response = chatClient.prompt()
            .system("You are a concise customer support agent for Acme Corp.")
            .messages(chatMemory.get(conversationId))   // full windowed history
            .call()
            .chatResponse();

        chatMemory.add(conversationId, new AssistantMessage(response));
        return response;
    }

    // Explicit eviction - e.g. user clicks "clear conversation"
    public void clear(String conversationId) {
        chatMemory.clear(conversationId);
    }
}
Analogy: The Shipping Dock Manifest

A busy shipping dock doesn't keep every crate that ever passed through forever - it keeps a rolling manifest of the last 20 shipments clipped to the wall, and when crate #21 arrives, the oldest one falls off the bottom of the clipboard. That clipboard is MessageWindowChatMemory. Each conversationId is a different loading dock with its own manifest, so Truck A's history never mixes with Truck B's. An in-memory manifest is a literal paper clipboard - gone the moment the dock closes for the night (app restart). A JDBC-backed manifest is the same clipboard photocopied into a filing cabinet downtown, so any dock worker on any shift, at any of your company's branches (app instances), can pick up exactly where the last one left off.

Deep Insight: Memory Windowing & Context Window Budgets
Homework: Multi-Tenant Conversation Memory

Build: Extend ConversationalAgent so conversationId is derived from {tenantId}:{userId}:{sessionId}. Add a scheduled @Scheduled job that evicts any JDBC-backed conversation untouched for >24 hours, logging a count of evicted rows.

Correct output: Two different tenants sending the literal same sessionId string never see each other's history; GET /api/conversations/{id}/messages returns exactly the windowed message list, oldest-first, capped at your configured max.

Edge case: A client sends 50 rapid-fire messages in under a second before the assistant reply for message #1 has been persisted (a race on the same conversationId). Your eviction/windowing logic must not corrupt ordering or silently drop a user message - demonstrate this with a concurrent test that asserts message order and count after the burst.

4.2 RAG Pipelines & Vector Databases

Analogy: RAG is Like an Open-Book Exam

Imagine taking a test about your company's policies. Without RAG, the AI is like a student taking a closed-book exam about a company they don't work for - it will guess or hallucinate. With RAG, you first load all company documents into a Vector Database (like a searchable index). When a user asks a question, you search for the most relevant document chunks, add them to the prompt as context, and then ask the AI to answer. The AI now has the exact information it needs - like an open-book exam with a perfectly organized reference library. The three subtopics below are the three stations of that library: how books get shelved (ETL), how you find the right page (vector search), and who's allowed to hand you a book at all (advisors).

Subtopic 4: ETL Ingestion Pipelines

Before anything can be retrieved, it has to be ingested. Spring AI models this as a classic ETL pipeline: a DocumentReader extracts raw text from a source (PDF, Markdown, JSON, a JDBC result set), a TokenTextSplitter breaks that text into overlapping chunks sized to fit comfortably inside a model's context window, a transformation step enriches each chunk with metadata (source filename, page number, department, access-control tags), and finally an EmbeddingModel converts each chunk's text into a dense numeric vector before it's written to a VectorStore. Get the chunk size or overlap wrong and retrieval quality silently degrades - chunks that are too large dilute relevance, chunks with no overlap can split a sentence's meaning in half.

java 
@Service
public class DocumentIngestionService {

    private final VectorStore vectorStore;

    public void ingest(Resource pdfResource, String department) {
        // 1. Extract: read the PDF into Document objects (one per page by default)
        DocumentReader reader = new PagePdfDocumentReader(pdfResource);
        List<Document> rawDocs = reader.get();

        // 2. Transform: split into ~500-token chunks with 50-token overlap
        TokenTextSplitter splitter = TokenTextSplitter.builder()
            .withChunkSize(500)
            .withMinChunkSizeChars(100)
            .build();
        List<Document> chunks = splitter.apply(rawDocs);

        // 3. Enrich: stamp every chunk with access-control metadata
        chunks.forEach(doc -> doc.getMetadata().putAll(Map.of(
            "department", department,
            "ingestedAt", Instant.now().toString()
        )));

        // 4. Load: embed + persist (EmbeddingModel is called internally by add())
        vectorStore.add(chunks);
    }
}
Analogy: The Corporate Research Team

Picture a corporate research team processing a stack of incoming reports. A junior analyst (DocumentReader) first photocopies and reads each report cover to cover. A second analyst (TokenTextSplitter) takes scissors to each report and cuts it into index-card-sized excerpts, deliberately overlapping the cut points slightly so no idea gets sliced cleanly in half. A third analyst stamps each card with a department code and a date (metadata enrichment) before filing it. Finally, a fourth analyst - the EmbeddingModel - doesn't file the card by alphabet or date, but by meaning: cards about "refund policy" end up in the same drawer-region as cards about "money-back guarantee," even though the words are completely different.

Deep Insight: Chunking Strategy & Embedding Cost
Homework: Batch Ingestion CLI

Build: A CommandLineRunner that walks a directory of mixed .pdf and .md files, picks the correct DocumentReader per extension, runs them through a shared TokenTextSplitter, tags each chunk with its source filename, and reports ingestion progress every 100 documents.

Correct output: Terminal shows [ingest] 100/437 chunks embedded (department=legal, avgChunkTokens=412) style progress lines, ending in a summary: total documents, total chunks, total wall-clock time.

Edge case: One PDF in the directory is corrupted (truncated mid-file) and another is a 0-byte placeholder. Ingestion must skip and log both, continue processing the remaining files, and exit with a non-zero status code summarizing how many files failed - without losing or duplicating chunks from files that succeeded before the failure.

Subtopic 5: Vector Stores & Similarity Search

The VectorStore interface is Spring AI's portable abstraction over fundamentally different storage engines: PGVector (a Postgres extension - great if you already run Postgres), Milvus (a purpose-built distributed engine for billion-scale collections), and Redis Vector Store (great when you already have Redis and want low-latency lookups alongside caching). All of them are queried the same way through SearchRequest, including Spring AI's portable metadata filter syntax - a small SQL-like expression language (department == 'legal' AND year >= 2023) that gets translated under the hood into each store's native filter dialect, so your filtering logic doesn't change when you migrate vector stores.

java 
@Service
public class SemanticSearchService {

    private final VectorStore vectorStore;   // PGVectorStore, MilvusVectorStore, or RedisVectorStore

    public List<Document> search(String query, String department) {
        FilterExpressionBuilder b = new FilterExpressionBuilder();

        return vectorStore.similaritySearch(SearchRequest.builder()
            .query(query)
            .topK(5)
            .similarityThreshold(0.75)               // drop weak matches outright
            .filterExpression(b.eq("department", department).build())  // portable SQL-like filter
            .build());
    }
}
Vector Store Providers
ProviderTypeBest For
PgvectorPostgreSQL extensionExisting Postgres infrastructure
MilvusDistributedLarge-scale, billions of vectors
Redis Vector StoreIn-memory + persistenceLow-latency lookups alongside caching
ChromaOpen sourceLocal development, on-premise
Neo4jGraph + vectorGraph relationships + semantic search
Analogy: The Legal Clerk's Filtered Card Catalog

A legal clerk searching a vast card catalog doesn't pull every card vaguely related to "contracts" - they first narrow the drawer by jurisdiction and year (the metadata filter), then walk their finger down the remaining cards by topical closeness (the similarity search). Whether that catalog lives in a single filing room (PGVector bolted onto Postgres), a sprawling distributed warehouse (Milvus), or a clerk's desk drawer they can reach without leaving their chair (Redis, fast but smaller), the clerk asks the question the exact same way - "jurisdiction = 'NY' AND year >= 2023, then closest 5 matches." The warehouse's internal organization is invisible to the request.

Deep Insight: ANN Indexes & Filter Pushdown
Homework: Multi-Tenant Filtered Retrieval

Build: A GET /api/search?q=...&dept=legal&minYear=2023 endpoint that constructs a compound FilterExpressionBuilder (AND of department and a numeric range on year), runs similaritySearch, and returns ranked results with their similarity score.

Correct output: JSON array sorted descending by score, each entry {"content":"...","score":0.91,"metadata":{"department":"legal","year":2024}} - never a result whose metadata violates the filter, even if it scored higher than everything else.

Edge case: Query a department with zero ingested documents. The endpoint must return an empty array with HTTP 200 (not a 500), and must not silently fall back to an unfiltered search just because the filtered result set was empty - that would leak data across tenants.

Subtopic 6: The Advisor Architecture

Advisors are Spring AI's interceptor chain for ChatClient calls - conceptually identical to servlet filters or AOP around-advice, but operating on prompts and responses instead of HTTP requests. The built-in QuestionAnsweringAdvisor wraps the entire "retrieve relevant chunks, inject them into the prompt, then call the model" RAG workflow into a single line of configuration. SafeGuardAdvisor screens prompts and responses against a blocklist before they ever reach the model or the caller. Because advisors form an ordered chain, you can write your own (implementing CallAroundAdvisor / StreamAroundAdvisor) to log every request/response pair, redact PII, inject tenant-specific context, or short-circuit a call entirely - all without touching the business logic that calls ChatClient.

java 
// Custom advisor: logs latency and redacts emails from every response
public class AuditAdvisor implements CallAroundAdvisor {

    @Override
    public AdvisedResponse aroundCall(AdvisedRequest request, CallAroundAdvisorChain chain) {
        long start = System.nanoTime();
        AdvisedResponse response = chain.nextAroundCall(request);   // continue the chain
        long elapsedMs = (System.nanoTime() - start) / 1_000_000;
        log.info("AI call took {}ms, {} tokens", elapsedMs,
            response.response().getMetadata().getUsage().getTotalTokens());
        return response;
    }

    @Override public String getName() { return "AuditAdvisor"; }
    @Override public int getOrder() { return 0; }   // lower runs first
}

@Service
public class GuardedRagService {

    public GuardedRagService(ChatClient.Builder builder, VectorStore vectorStore) {
        this.chatClient = builder
            .defaultAdvisors(
                new AuditAdvisor(),
                QuestionAnsweringAdvisor.builder(vectorStore).build(),   // out-of-the-box RAG
                SafeGuardAdvisor.builder().sensitiveWords(List.of("confidential-leak-term")).build()
            )
            .build();
    }
    private final ChatClient chatClient;
}
Analogy: Customs Inspectors at the Shipping Dock

Every crate (prompt) leaving your warehouse for the LLM passes through a line of customs inspectors before it reaches the border, and every crate coming back passes through the same line in reverse. One inspector (AuditAdvisor) just stamps a timestamp and weighs the crate. The next (QuestionAnsweringAdvisor) opens it, runs to the warehouse shelves, grabs the most relevant reference documents, and tucks them inside before resealing it. A third (SafeGuardAdvisor) refuses to let certain banned cargo cross the border at all, in either direction. The order of inspectors matters - you wouldn't want the cargo-injector running after the inspector who already sealed the crate - which is exactly why each advisor declares an explicit getOrder().

Deep Insight: Advisor Ordering & Chain Short-Circuiting
Homework: Build a PII-Redacting Advisor

Build: A custom CallAroundAdvisor named PiiRedactionAdvisor with getOrder() lower than your QuestionAnsweringAdvisor, that regex-scans the outgoing user message for email addresses and SSN-like patterns and replaces them with [REDACTED] before the request continues down the chain. Register it alongside QuestionAnsweringAdvisor and SafeGuardAdvisor in that explicit order.

Correct output: A user message "my email is jane@acme.com, what's my refund status?" arrives at the model as "my email is [REDACTED], what's my refund status?" - verify via a logging advisor placed last in the chain that the model never sees the raw email.

Edge case: The user's message contains only a PII string with nothing else (e.g. just an SSN, no question). After redaction the message becomes effectively empty/meaningless. Your advisor must detect this and short-circuit the chain with a canned clarification response ("Could you rephrase your question?") rather than sending an empty prompt to the model.

4.3 Advanced Agentic Orchestration

Subtopic 7: Function Calling / Tool Calling

Tool calling is what turns a chatbot into an agent - it lets the model decide, mid-conversation, that it needs to call one of your real Java methods to answer accurately (check live inventory, look up an order, run a calculation) instead of guessing. You expose a method as a tool with the @Tool annotation (or by registering a FunctionCallback programmatically); Spring AI introspects its parameters and @ToolParam descriptions to generate a JSON Schema, sends that schema to the model alongside the prompt, and when the model's response contains a tool-call request, Spring AI automatically invokes your Java method, feeds the result back to the model in a follow-up round-trip, and only returns the model's final, tool-informed answer to your code. The whole asynchronous request → tool-call → execute → re-prompt → final-answer loop happens transparently inside a single .call().

java 
@Component
public class OrderTools {

    private final OrderRepository orderRepository;

    @Tool(description = "Look up the current shipping status of an order by its order ID")
    public String getOrderStatus(
            @ToolParam(description = "The numeric order ID, e.g. 48213") Long orderId) {
        return orderRepository.findById(orderId)
            .map(o -> "Order " + orderId + " is " + o.getStatus())
            .orElse("No order found with that ID");
    }

    @Tool(description = "Issue a refund for an order; requires manager approval flag")
    public String issueRefund(Long orderId, Boolean approved) {
        if (!Boolean.TRUE.equals(approved)) return "Refund requires explicit approval";
        orderRepository.refund(orderId);
        return "Refund issued for order " + orderId;
    }
}

@Service
public class SupportAgent {
    public SupportAgent(ChatClient.Builder builder, OrderTools tools) {
        this.chatClient = builder.defaultTools(tools).build();   // register every @Tool method on this bean
    }
    private final ChatClient chatClient;

    public String handle(String userMessage) {
        // Model may silently call getOrderStatus() 0, 1, or N times before answering
        return chatClient.prompt().user(userMessage).call().content();
    }
}
Analogy: The Smart Vending Machine

An old vending machine only ever does one thing: dispense whatever's behind the button you press. A smart vending machine is wired into the backend inventory and payment systems - press "B4" and it doesn't just drop a snack, it calls a real function: check stock, charge the card, update inventory, then dispense. Tool calling turns your LLM into that smart machine's brain: the model doesn't know how to check an order's status, but it knows there's a button labeled getOrderStatus with a slot for an order ID, and it knows pressing it gets a real, factual answer back instead of a guess. Crucially, the machine - not the customer - decides which button to press based on what was asked.

Deep Insight: Tool-Call Round-Trips & Latency Budgets
Homework: Multi-Tool Agent with Guardrails

Build: Add a third tool, cancelOrder(Long orderId, String reason), that throws a checked-style runtime exception if the order is already shipped. Wire all three tools onto SupportAgent and add a system prompt instructing the model to always confirm with the user before calling issueRefund or cancelOrder.

Correct output: Asking "what's the status of order 4821?" triggers exactly one tool call to getOrderStatus and returns a natural-language answer incorporating the real status - verify via a logging wrapper around the tool method that it was invoked exactly once with the correct argument.

Edge case: The model calls cancelOrder on an order that's already shipped, and your tool method throws. Spring AI must surface that failure back to the model as a tool-error result (not crash the whole request) so the model can apologize and explain it can't cancel a shipped order - assert this end-to-end without an unhandled exception reaching the controller.

Subtopic 8: Model Context Protocol (MCP)

Tool calling above wires your own Java methods into a model. MCP generalizes the same idea across process and network boundaries: an MCP server exposes tools, resources, and prompts over a standard protocol, and any MCP client - written in Java, Python, or anything else - can discover and call them without custom integration code. Spring AI's spring-ai-mcp-core module gives you both sides: a Spring Boot app can be an MCP client consuming tools from an external server (a filesystem server, a database server, a third-party SaaS server) over STDIO (spawned local subprocess, fastest, no network) or SSE (Server-Sent Events over HTTP, for remote/networked servers); or it can be an MCP server itself, exposing its own @Tool-annotated beans to any MCP-compatible client - including other Claude or GPT agents - dynamically.

java 
// --- MCP CLIENT side: consume tools from an external MCP server over STDIO ---
@Configuration
public class McpClientConfig {

    @Bean
    public McpSyncClient filesystemMcpClient() {
        var transport = StdioClientTransport.builder()
            .command("npx", "-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/data")
            .build();
        McpSyncClient client = McpClient.sync(transport).build();
        client.initialize();   // handshake: negotiate protocol version + capabilities
        return client;
    }

    @Bean
    public ToolCallbackProvider mcpTools(McpSyncClient filesystemMcpClient) {
        return McpToolCallbackProvider.builder().mcpClients(filesystemMcpClient).build();
    }
}

// --- MCP SERVER side: expose your own Spring beans as MCP tools over SSE ---
@Component
public class InventoryMcpTools {
    @Tool(description = "Check live stock level for a SKU")
    public int checkStock(String sku) { return inventoryService.stockOf(sku); }
}
// application.yml: spring.ai.mcp.server.transport=SSE  enables this automatically
Analogy: The International Embassy

A national embassy doesn't care what passport you're holding - American, French, Japanese - it offers the same standardized visa application process to anyone who walks in speaking the embassy's protocol. An MCP server is that embassy: it publishes a fixed set of "visas" (tools/resources) it's willing to issue, and any MCP client, regardless of what country (programming language or AI vendor) it's from, requests them the same standardized way. A STDIO transport is a private back-room meeting - fast, but only works if you're already inside the same building (process). An SSE transport is the embassy's public front desk over the internet - anyone, anywhere, can walk up and request service, at the cost of a network round-trip.

Deep Insight: STDIO vs SSE Transport Trade-offs
Homework: Dual-Role MCP Service

Build: A Spring Boot app that is both an MCP client (consuming a filesystem MCP server over STDIO to read product spec files) and an MCP server (exposing InventoryMcpTools over SSE to any external agent). Wire a ChatClient with defaultToolCallbacks from the client-side ToolCallbackProvider so a single agent can both read specs and check live stock.

Correct output: A request like "what's the stock level for the SKU described in spec.txt?" results in two distinct tool calls (filesystem read, then stock check) visible in your audit logs, in that order, before the final answer.

Edge case: The STDIO subprocess for the filesystem MCP server crashes mid-session (kill it manually during a request). Your client must detect the broken transport, surface a clean error to the agent rather than hanging indefinitely, and a health-check endpoint must report that MCP client as DOWN without taking the whole application down.

Subtopic 9: AI Observability & Evaluation

Once AI features are in production, "it works on my machine" isn't good enough - you need the same operational rigor you'd demand of any other service. Spring AI integrates with Micrometer and OpenTelemetry through the standard ObservationRegistry, automatically emitting spans and metrics for every ChatClient call (latency, token counts, model name) that flow straight into Actuator's /actuator/metrics and any tracing backend (Zipkin, Jaeger, Datadog) you've already wired up. That tells you the model responded - it doesn't tell you the answer was good. For that, Spring AI ships evaluators like RelevancyEvaluator (does the response actually address the retrieved context and the question?) and FactCheckingEvaluator (is the response logically consistent with the source documents?) - both implemented as a second, independent LLM call that grades the first, letting you automatically flag and score hallucinations in CI or in a sampled slice of live traffic.

java 
// application.yml: tracing is wired automatically once micrometer-tracing is on the classpath
// management.tracing.sampling.probability: 1.0   (sample 100% in this example)

@Service
public class EvaluatedRagService {

    private final ChatClient chatClient;
    private final RelevancyEvaluator relevancyEvaluator;
    private final FactCheckingEvaluator factCheckingEvaluator;

    public EvaluationReport answerAndGrade(String question, List<Document> retrievedContext) {
        ChatResponse response = chatClient.prompt().user(question).call().chatResponse();

        EvaluationRequest evalRequest = new EvaluationRequest(question, retrievedContext, response.getResult().getOutput().getText());

        EvaluationResponse relevancy = relevancyEvaluator.evaluate(evalRequest);
        EvaluationResponse factCheck = factCheckingEvaluator.evaluate(evalRequest);

        if (!factCheck.isPass()) {
            log.warn("Potential hallucination detected: {}", factCheck.getFeedback());
        }

        return new EvaluationReport(response.getResult().getOutput().getText(),
            relevancy.isPass(), factCheck.isPass());
    }
}
Analogy: The Research Team's Fact-Checking Editor

A junior analyst hands in a report (the model's response). A separate, senior editor on the same research team - who never wrote the report and has no stake in defending it - reads it against the original source binders (the retrieved context) and signs off only if every claim actually traces back to a real page. That editor is the FactCheckingEvaluator: a second, independent model call whose only job is grading the first one, exactly the way a fact-checking desk at a newspaper operates separately from the reporters. Meanwhile, a stopwatch on the wall (Micrometer/OTel spans) timed how long the analyst took to write the report in the first place - useful for staffing decisions, but it tells you nothing about whether the report was actually true.

Deep Insight: Evaluator Cost & Sampling Strategy
Homework: Production Hallucination Dashboard

Build: Wrap EvaluatedRagService in a sampling layer that runs the full evaluator pair on only 10% of live traffic (configurable via a property), publishing the pass/fail result as a custom Micrometer Counter tagged evaluator=factCheck,result=pass|fail, visible at /actuator/metrics/ai.evaluation.result.

Correct output: Over 1,000 simulated requests with sampling at 10%, the counter totals land close to 100 evaluated calls (±statistical noise), and a failing fact-check produces a log line including the original question, the failed claim, and the source documents it contradicted.

Edge case: The evaluator LLM call itself times out or errors (it's still an LLM call, with its own failure modes). This must not count as a factual failure or pollute your pass/fail metric - it must be recorded under a distinct result=evaluator_error tag, and the original user-facing response must still be returned promptly rather than blocked on the evaluator.

Production Gotcha: Hallucination and Prompt Injection

LLMs will confidently make up information (hallucination) and can be manipulated by malicious user input (prompt injection). Always use RAG with explicit grounding - instruct the AI to only answer from provided context. Implement output validation using Spring AI's structured output with Bean Validation constraints. For sensitive operations, never let AI directly execute code or database queries - have it generate recommendations that a human reviews. Use @Retryable for transient API failures, implement circuit breakers for AI provider outages, and always log AI interactions for audit trails. Monitor token usage and costs - unbounded context windows can lead to surprise bills. Treat any tool or MCP server connected to a model as part of your attack surface: a malicious or compromised document, RAG chunk, or MCP resource can attempt to inject instructions into the model just as much as a malicious user message can.

Phase 5

Critical Enterprise Dependencies & Integrations

5.1 Spring Security & Stateless Authentication

Deep Dive: Token-Based vs Session-Based Authentication

Traditional session-based authentication stores user state on the server. When you log in, the server creates a session (stored in memory or Redis) and sends you a cookie with a session ID. Every subsequent request includes this cookie, and the server looks up your session. This works but has problems: sessions consume server memory, don't scale horizontally without sticky sessions or shared Redis, and are vulnerable to CSRF attacks.

Token-based (JWT) authentication is stateless. The server stores nothing. After login, the server issues a signed JWT token containing user claims (username, roles, expiry). The client sends this token in every request header. The server validates the signature and extracts claims - no database lookup needed for authentication. This scales infinitely and works naturally with microservices and SPAs.

JWT Implementation Architecture

sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as Client
    participant Auth as AuthController
    participant User as UserDetailsService
    participant Jwt as JwtUtil
    participant Controller as Controller
    participant Security as SecurityContext

    Client->>Auth: 1. POST /login
    Auth->>User: 2. Validate credentials
    User-->>Auth: Valid user
    Auth->>Jwt: 3. Generate JWT
    Jwt-->>Auth: Signed token
    Auth-->>Client: 4. Return JWT

    Client->>Controller: 5. GET /api/data<br/>Authorization: Bearer #60;jwt#62;
    Controller->>Security: 6. Validate token
    Security->>Security: 7. Set Authentication
    Security-->>Controller: 8. Proceed
    Controller-->>Client: Response
java 
// Maven dependencies
// <dependency>
//     <groupId>io.jsonwebtoken</groupId>
//     <artifactId>jjwt-api</artifactId>
//     <version>0.12.3</version>
// </dependency>

// JWT Utility: Create and validate tokens
@Component
public class JwtUtil {

    private final SecretKey key = Jwts.SIG.HS256.key().build();
    private final long expirationMs = 86400000; // 24 hours

    public String generateToken(UserDetails user) {
        return Jwts.builder()
            .subject(user.getUsername())
            .claim("roles", user.getAuthorities().stream()
                .map(GrantedAuthority::getAuthority)
                .toList())
            .issuedAt(new Date())
            .expiration(new Date(System.currentTimeMillis() + expirationMs))
            .signWith(key)
            .compact();
    }

    public Claims parseToken(String token) {
        return Jwts.parser()
            .verifyWith(key)
            .build()
            .parseSignedClaims(token)
            .getPayload();
    }

    public boolean isValid(String token) {
        try {
            parseToken(token);
            return true;
        } catch (JwtException | IllegalArgumentException e) {
            return false;
        }
    }
}
java 
// Complete SecurityFilterChain for stateless JWT
@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
@EnableMethodSecurity  // Enables @PreAuthorize, @PostAuthorize
public class JwtSecurityConfig {

    @Bean
    public PasswordEncoder passwordEncoder() {
        return new BCryptPasswordEncoder(12);  // Strength 12 (cost factor)
    }

    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http,
                                           JwtAuthenticationFilter jwtFilter) throws Exception {
        http
            .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())
            .sessionManagement(s -> s.sessionCreationPolicy(SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS))
            .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
                .requestMatchers("/api/auth/**", "/actuator/health").permitAll()
                .requestMatchers("/api/admin/**").hasAuthority("ROLE_ADMIN")
                .anyRequest().authenticated()
            )
            .addFilterBefore(jwtFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class);
        return http.build();
    }
}

// Method-level security
@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/orders")
public class OrderController {

    @GetMapping("/my-orders")
    @PreAuthorize("hasRole('CUSTOMER')")
    public List<OrderDto> getMyOrders(@AuthenticationPrincipal UserDetails user) {
        return orderService.findByCustomer(user.getUsername());
    }

    @DeleteMapping("/{id}")
    @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') or @orderService.isOwner(#id, authentication.name)")
    public ResponseEntity<Void> deleteOrder(@PathVariable Long id) {
        orderService.delete(id);
        return ResponseEntity.noContent().build();
    }
}
Production Gotcha: JWT Security Checklist

Never store JWTs in localStorage - vulnerable to XSS attacks. Use httpOnly cookies instead. Always validate all JWT claims: check exp (expiration), iss (issuer), aud (audience), and verify the signature algorithm isn't "none". Store the signing key in a secrets manager (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault), never in source code. Implement token refresh: short-lived access tokens (15 min) + long-lived refresh tokens (7 days) stored securely. For logout, maintain a token blocklist in Redis (with TTL matching token expiry). Rotate signing keys periodically using kid (key ID) header to identify which key signed the token.

Sub-Concept 1: Security Filter Chain & Authentication Lifecycle
