Spring Security's SecurityFilterChain is an ordered chain of javax.servlet.Filter implementations that every HTTP request passes through before reaching your controller. At startup, Spring Security assembles this chain from your SecurityFilterChain bean configuration. The most critical filter is UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter for form login, but in stateless JWT APIs you replace it with a custom OncePerRequestFilter. The AuthenticationManager is the dispatcher — it delegates to one or more AuthenticationProvider implementations (e.g., DaoAuthenticationProvider which calls your UserDetailsService). On success, the resulting Authentication object is stored in the SecurityContextHolder (a ThreadLocal-backed holder), making the principal available anywhere downstream via SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication().

Custom filters are injected with addFilterBefore(myFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class) or addFilterAfter(). The order matters: a JWT filter must run before the authentication filter so it can pre-populate the SecurityContext from the token, preventing re-authentication. Every filter must call filterChain.doFilter(request, response) to continue the chain or short-circuit by writing directly to the response.

java 
// Spring Security 6.x — Modern lambda DSL (no deprecated .and())
@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
@EnableMethodSecurity
public class SecurityConfig {

    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http,
                                               JwtAuthFilter jwtFilter,
                                               AuthenticationProvider authProvider) throws Exception {
        return http
            .authenticationProvider(authProvider)
            .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())
            .sessionManagement(s -> s.sessionCreationPolicy(SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS))
            .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
                .requestMatchers("/api/auth/**", "/actuator/health").permitAll()
                .requestMatchers(HttpMethod.GET, "/api/public/**").permitAll()
                .requestMatchers("/api/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")
                .anyRequest().authenticated())
            .addFilterBefore(jwtFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class)
            .exceptionHandling(ex -> ex
                .authenticationEntryPoint((req, res, e) -> res.sendError(401, "Unauthorized"))
                .accessDeniedHandler((req, res, e) -> res.sendError(403, "Forbidden")))
            .build();
    }

    @Bean
    public AuthenticationProvider authenticationProvider(UserDetailsService uds,
                                                              PasswordEncoder encoder) {
        var provider = new DaoAuthenticationProvider();
        provider.setUserDetailsService(uds);
        provider.setPasswordEncoder(encoder);
        return provider;
    }

    @Bean
    public AuthenticationManager authenticationManager(AuthenticationConfiguration config)
            throws Exception {
        return config.getAuthenticationManager();
    }
}

// Custom JWT OncePerRequestFilter
@Component
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class JwtAuthFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter {
    private final JwtUtil jwtUtil;
    private final UserDetailsService userDetailsService;

    @Override
    protected void doFilterInternal(HttpServletRequest request,
                                        HttpServletResponse response,
                                        FilterChain chain) throws ServletException, IOException {
        final String header = request.getHeader("Authorization");
        if (header == null || !header.startsWith("Bearer ")) {
            chain.doFilter(request, response);
            return;
        }
        final String token = header.substring(7);
        final String username = jwtUtil.extractUsername(token);
        if (username != null && SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication() == null) {
            UserDetails userDetails = userDetailsService.loadUserByUsername(username);
            if (jwtUtil.isValid(token, userDetails)) {
                var authToken = new UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken(
                    userDetails, null, userDetails.getAuthorities());
                authToken.setDetails(new WebAuthenticationDetailsSource().buildDetails(request));
                SecurityContextHolder.getContext().setAuthentication(authToken);
            }
        }
        chain.doFilter(request, response);
    }
}
Analogy: The Military Base Security Checkpoint

Imagine a military base with a main gate (the SecurityFilterChain). Every vehicle must pass checkpoints in strict order: the outer perimeter guard checks the pre-approved visitor list (permit-all rules), then the badge scanner verifies your military ID (the JwtAuthFilter), then the duty officer checks your clearance level against the wing you want to enter (@PreAuthorize). If you pass all checkpoints you're recorded in the base access log (SecurityContextHolder) and escorted to your destination. Try to enter out of order or skip a checkpoint — the gate physically blocks you. The AuthenticationManager is the duty officer's supervisor who knows which specialist (AuthenticationProvider) to call for each ID type.

Deep Insight: Filter Chain Internals & Authentication Architecture
Homework: Custom Multi-Tenant Security Filter Chain

Build: Two distinct SecurityFilterChain beans ordered with @Order(1) and @Order(2): one for /api/admin/** requiring ROLE_ADMIN plus an IP-allowlist filter that blocks non-whitelisted IPs with HTTP 403; one for /api/public/** permitting all but rate-limiting by IP via an @ApplicationScoped counter bean.

Correct terminal output: curl -X GET http://localhost:8080/api/admin/dashboard -H "Authorization: Bearer <admin_jwt>" → 200 from whitelisted IP, 403 from any other IP even with valid JWT. Public endpoint returns 200 until 11th request/minute, then 429.

Edge case: SecurityContextHolder is ThreadLocal-backed. In a thread-pooled servlet container, demonstrate context leaking between requests reusing the same thread — then fix it by adding SecurityContextHolder.clearContext() in a finally block and write a test proving the leak is gone.

Sub-Concept 2: Stateless JWT Architecture (RS256 & Refresh Tokens)

The basic HS256 JWT uses a symmetric key — the same secret signs and verifies. For microservices, this is a liability: every service that validates tokens must hold the signing secret. Asymmetric RS256 solves this: a private key signs tokens (only the auth server holds it), and a public key verifies them (freely distributable). This is the JWKS model used by Auth0, Keycloak, and Okta.

Refresh Token Architecture: Access tokens should be short-lived (5–15 minutes). When they expire, the client presents a long-lived refresh token (7–30 days) stored in an HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict cookie — invisible to JavaScript, immune to XSS. The refresh endpoint validates against a Redis denylist, issues a new access token, and rotates the refresh token for one-time-use security.

java 
// RS256 Asymmetric JWT: openssl genrsa -out private.pem 2048
@Component
public class Rs256JwtUtil {
    private final RSAPrivateKey privateKey;
    private final RSAPublicKey  publicKey;

    public String generateAccessToken(UserDetails user) {
        return JWT.create()
            .withSubject(user.getUsername())
            .withIssuer("https://auth.myapp.com")
            .withAudience("https://api.myapp.com")
            .withIssuedAt(Instant.now())
            .withExpiresAt(Instant.now().plusSeconds(900))
            .withClaim("roles", user.getAuthorities().stream()
                .map(GrantedAuthority::getAuthority).toList())
            .sign(Algorithm.RSA256(publicKey, privateKey));
    }

    public DecodedJWT verify(String token) {
        return JWT.require(Algorithm.RSA256(publicKey, null))
            .withIssuer("https://auth.myapp.com")
            .withAudience("https://api.myapp.com")
            .acceptLeeway(30)  // 30-second clock skew tolerance
            .build().verify(token);
    }
}

// Refresh token endpoint — validates, rotates, returns new access token
@PostMapping("/api/auth/refresh")
public ResponseEntity<TokenResponse> refresh(HttpServletRequest req, HttpServletResponse res) {
    String refreshToken = extractRefreshFromCookie(req);
    if (refreshToken == null || tokenDenylist.isRevoked(refreshToken))
        return ResponseEntity.status(401).build();

    DecodedJWT decoded = jwtUtil.verify(refreshToken);
    UserDetails user = userDetailsService.loadUserByUsername(decoded.getSubject());

    tokenDenylist.revoke(refreshToken, decoded.getExpiresAtAsInstant());
    String newRefresh = jwtUtil.generateRefreshToken(user);
    String newAccess  = jwtUtil.generateAccessToken(user);

    ResponseCookie cookie = ResponseCookie.from("refreshToken", newRefresh)
        .httpOnly(true).secure(true).sameSite("Strict")
        .maxAge(Duration.ofDays(7)).path("/api/auth/refresh").build();
    res.addHeader(HttpHeaders.SET_COOKIE, cookie.toString());
    return ResponseEntity.ok(new TokenResponse(newAccess));
}
Analogy: The Central Passport Agency (RS256 Model)

Symmetric JWT (HS256) is like every hotel in a chain holding the master key to verify guest badges — one reception desk breach exposes all badges. RS256 is like a Central Passport Agency: only government HQ (auth server with the private key) can stamp passports. Hotels (microservices) hold a public notary seal to verify the stamp without holding the stamping machinery. A refresh token is your long-term residency permit kept in a vault at home (HttpOnly cookie, inaccessible from XSS), while your tourist visa (access token) is the short-term pass you carry daily. When the visa expires, you return to the consulate with your residency permit, it's checked against a revocation list, then swapped for a freshly stamped visa.

Deep Insight: Asymmetric JWT Trade-offs & Token Revocation at Scale
Homework: RS256 JWT with Refresh Token Rotation

Build: POST /api/auth/login returns a 15-minute RS256 access token in JSON and sets an HttpOnly SameSite=Strict refresh token cookie (7-day TTL). POST /api/auth/refresh reads the cookie, validates against a Redis denylist, rotates it, and returns a new access token. POST /api/auth/logout adds the refresh token to the denylist.

Correct output: curl -c cookies.txt -X POST /api/auth/login -d '{"username":"alice","password":"secret"}' → {"accessToken":"eyJhbGci..."}. curl -b cookies.txt -X POST /api/auth/refresh → {"accessToken":"eyJhbGci..."} (new token, rotated cookie).

Edge case: Auth server and resource server clocks are 5 seconds apart. A token with iat=now arrives before the resource server's clock advances, causing IssuedAt cannot be in the future. Fix: configure 30-second acceptLeeway(30) in the JWT verifier. Write a test proving a 29-second-future token is accepted and a 31-second-future token is rejected.

Sub-Concept 3: Method-Level Security & RBAC/ABAC

@EnableMethodSecurity (replacing the deprecated @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity in Spring Security 6.x) activates a proxy-based AOP mechanism that intercepts method calls and evaluates access control expressions before or after execution. @PreAuthorize fires before the method — ideal for access gates. @PostAuthorize fires after — used to filter return values based on the result. @Secured is simpler, supporting only role names without SpEL expressions.

RBAC vs ABAC: Role-Based Access Control grants permissions based on roles (ROLE_ADMIN). Attribute-Based Access Control grants permissions based on attributes of the subject, resource, action, and environment — e.g., "a user can edit a document only if they are the owner AND the document is in DRAFT status." ABAC is implemented via custom AuthorizationManager beans or SpEL expressions calling service beans.

java 
@Service
public class DocumentService {

    // RBAC: Only admins or editors can publish
    @PreAuthorize("hasAnyRole('ADMIN', 'EDITOR')")
    public void publish(Long documentId) { /* ... */ }

    // ABAC: delegate full policy check to a service bean
    @PreAuthorize("@docSecurityService.canEdit(#documentId, authentication)")
    public Document edit(Long documentId, DocumentRequest req) { /* ... */ }

    // @PostAuthorize: hide other users' docs — returnObject available in SpEL
    @PostAuthorize("returnObject.ownerId == authentication.principal.id or hasRole('ADMIN')")
    public Document findById(Long id) { return repository.findById(id).orElseThrow(); }

    // @PostFilter: strip list to caller's own items
    @PostFilter("filterObject.ownerId == authentication.principal.id")
    public List<Document> findAll() { return repository.findAll(); }
}

// ABAC Policy Service — complex multi-attribute authorization
@Service("docSecurityService")
public class DocumentSecurityService {
    public boolean canEdit(Long documentId, Authentication auth) {
        Document doc = repository.findById(documentId).orElseThrow();
        CustomUserPrincipal user = (CustomUserPrincipal) auth.getPrincipal();
        boolean isOwner = doc.getOwnerId().equals(user.getId());
        boolean isAdmin = auth.getAuthorities().stream()
            .anyMatch(a -> a.getAuthority().equals("ROLE_ADMIN"));
        boolean isDraft = doc.getStatus() == DocumentStatus.DRAFT;
        return (isOwner || isAdmin) && isDraft;
    }
}
Analogy: VIP Guest List (RBAC) vs Classified Facility Policy (ABAC)

RBAC is the nightclub's guest list: you're either on the VIP list (ADMIN), press list (EDITOR), or general admission (USER). Your tier determines which areas you access — simple, fast, easy to manage. ABAC is the security policy of a classified government facility: entry isn't just about clearance level — you also need a specific purpose code (attribute), your appointment must be active (resource state), and your supervisor must have pre-authorized the visit. @PreAuthorize("@docSecurityService.canEdit(#id, authentication)") is that full policy check running invisibly at the entrance, combining all attributes in one decision before any door opens.

Deep Insight: Method Security Internals & ABAC Authorization Managers
Homework: ABAC Document Management System

Build: A DocumentController where PUT /api/documents/{id} is protected by an ABAC rule: caller is the document owner OR an admin, document status is DRAFT, and content length is under 10,000 characters. Implement as a @Service("docPolicy") bean called from @PreAuthorize("@docPolicy.canUpdate(#id, #req, authentication)").

Correct output: Owner token + DRAFT doc → 200 OK. Non-owner token → 403. Owner token but PUBLISHED doc → 403.

Edge case: @PostAuthorize evaluates after the method executes. If the method has side effects (writes, emails), a @PostAuthorize failure still rolls back a @Transactional method body, but the email already sent. Design a test demonstrating this failure mode and prove why @PreAuthorize is always preferred for write operations.

5.2 Federation, OAuth2 & OIDC

Sub-Concept 4: OAuth2 Resource Server

An OAuth2 Resource Server exposes protected resources and validates Bearer tokens issued by an external Identity Provider (IdP). Instead of managing its own user authentication, it delegates token validation to the IdP's JWKS endpoint. Spring Boot's spring-boot-starter-oauth2-resource-server auto-configures this with one property: spring.security.oauth2.resourceserver.jwt.issuer-uri. Spring fetches JWKS keys from <issuer>/.well-known/openid-configuration at startup and caches them, rotating automatically when the IdP rotates keys.

Custom claim mapping is done via a JwtAuthenticationConverter: you extract custom scopes or roles from token claims and convert them into Spring GrantedAuthority objects. For Keycloak, roles live under resource_access.<client>.roles; for Auth0, under a custom namespace claim. Without this converter, Spring only reads scope claims with the SCOPE_ prefix.

java 
# application.yml — Resource Server pointing to Keycloak
spring:
  security:
    oauth2:
      resourceserver:
        jwt:
          issuer-uri: https://keycloak.mycompany.com/realms/myrealm

// SecurityConfig — Resource Server with Keycloak role mapping
@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
@EnableMethodSecurity
public class ResourceServerConfig {

    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        return http
            .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
                .requestMatchers("/actuator/health").permitAll()
                .anyRequest().authenticated())
            .oauth2ResourceServer(oauth2 -> oauth2
                .jwt(jwt -> jwt.jwtAuthenticationConverter(keycloakConverter())))
            .sessionManagement(s -> s.sessionCreationPolicy(SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS))
            .build();
    }

    private JwtAuthenticationConverter keycloakConverter() {
        Converter<Jwt, Collection<GrantedAuthority>> keycloakRoles = jwt -> {
            Map<String, Object> resourceAccess = jwt.getClaim("resource_access");
            if (resourceAccess == null) return List.of();
            Map<String, Object> clientAccess =
                (Map<String, Object>) resourceAccess.get("my-spring-client");
            if (clientAccess == null) return List.of();
            return ((List<String>) clientAccess.get("roles")).stream()
                .map(r -> new SimpleGrantedAuthority("ROLE_" + r.toUpperCase()))
                .collect(Collectors.toList());
        };
        JwtGrantedAuthoritiesConverter scopeConverter = new JwtGrantedAuthoritiesConverter();
        JwtAuthenticationConverter converter = new JwtAuthenticationConverter();
        converter.setJwtGrantedAuthoritiesConverter(jwt -> {
            List<GrantedAuthority> combined = new ArrayList<>(scopeConverter.convert(jwt));
            combined.addAll(keycloakRoles.convert(jwt));
            return combined;
        });
        return converter;
    }
}
Analogy: The Automated Customs Shipping Manifest

An OAuth2 Resource Server is like a port authority customs scanner. It doesn't issue shipping manifests (tokens) — that's the shipping company's (IdP's) job. When a container arrives, the scanner downloads the latest authorized manifest template from the shipping company's central registry (JWKS endpoint), verifies the barcode (JWT signature) matches exactly, then reads the declared contents (claims) to decide if goods are cleared. Your JwtAuthenticationConverter is the customs clerk who translates the manifest's product codes (Keycloak roles) into domestic tariff classifications (Spring GrantedAuthority) that your domestic trade rules (@PreAuthorize) can act on.

Deep Insight: JWKS Caching, Key Rotation & Multi-IdP Configurations
Homework: Keycloak-Backed Resource Server

Build: Spring Boot 3.4+ app as an OAuth2 Resource Server against a local Keycloak Docker instance. Map resource_access.<client>.roles to ROLE_* GrantedAuthorities. Protect GET /api/admin/report with @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN')") and GET /api/me with @PreAuthorize("isAuthenticated()").

Correct output: curl /api/me -H "Authorization: Bearer <valid_token>" → {"sub":"alice","roles":["USER"]}. curl /api/admin/report -H "Authorization: Bearer <user_token>" → 403. Admin token → 200.

Edge case: Keycloak rotates signing keys periodically. Spring caches JWKS for 5 minutes. Simulate key rotation by revoking Keycloak's active key and issuing a new token signed with the new key. Without configuration, your server rejects the new token for up to 5 minutes. Fix: configure NimbusJwtDecoder with jwkSetUri and a shorter cache TTL, or catch BadJwtException and trigger a JWKS refresh on first failure.

Sub-Concept 5: OAuth2 Client & Authorization Code Flow with PKCE

The Authorization Code Flow is the secure OAuth2 flow for user-facing applications. The client never handles the user's credentials — instead, it redirects the user to the IdP's login page, which issues a short-lived authorization code. The code is exchanged server-side for tokens, so tokens never appear in the browser URL. PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) extends this for public clients: the client generates a random code_verifier, sends its SHA256 hash (code_challenge) during authorization, then proves ownership at the token exchange step — preventing authorization code interception attacks.

java 
# application.yml — OAuth2 Client (Authorization Code + PKCE via Keycloak)
spring:
  security:
    oauth2:
      client:
        registration:
          keycloak:
            client-id: my-spring-app
            client-secret: ${KEYCLOAK_CLIENT_SECRET}
            authorization-grant-type: authorization_code
            redirect-uri: "{baseUrl}/login/oauth2/code/{registrationId}"
            scope: openid, profile, email
        provider:
          keycloak:
            issuer-uri: https://keycloak.mycompany.com/realms/myrealm

// SecurityConfig — OAuth2 Login
@Bean
public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    return http
        .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
            .requestMatchers("/", "/login").permitAll()
            .anyRequest().authenticated())
        .oauth2Login(oauth2 -> oauth2
            .loginPage("/login")
            .defaultSuccessUrl("/dashboard", true)
            .userInfoEndpoint(ui -> ui.oidcUserService(customOidcUserService())))
        .oauth2Client(Customizer.withDefaults())
        .logout(logout -> logout
            .logoutSuccessUrl("/login?logout")
            .invalidateHttpSession(true)
            .deleteCookies("JSESSIONID"))
        .build();
}

// Propagating the access token to a downstream API
@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class ApiGatewayService {
    private final OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager clientManager;

    public String callDownstream(Authentication auth) {
        OAuth2AuthorizeRequest req = OAuth2AuthorizeRequest
            .withClientRegistrationId("keycloak").principal(auth).build();
        String token = clientManager.authorize(req).getAccessToken().getTokenValue();
        return WebClient.create("https://api.mycompany.com")
            .get().uri("/api/data")
            .headers(h -> h.setBearerAuth(token))
            .retrieve().bodyToMono(String.class).block();
    }
}
Analogy: The Concert Venue Wristband + PKCE Secret Handshake

Old insecure flow (Implicit): the venue handed you your wristband in the public lobby — anyone could grab it. Authorization Code Flow: the venue gives you a claim ticket at the door, and you exchange it in a private back room. PKCE adds: before entering, you write a secret phrase, lock it in a safe at the booth (code_challenge = SHA256 of your secret), then at exchange time you prove you're the same person by unlocking the safe with your original phrase (code_verifier). Even if someone steals your claim ticket, they can't exchange it — they don't know your phrase. The state parameter is your stamped parking ticket: without showing it matches when you return, the booth assumes you're an impersonator.

Deep Insight: Authorization Code Flow Internals & PKCE Attack Prevention
Homework: Full OAuth2 Login Flow with Token Propagation

Build: A Spring MVC app using oauth2Login() against Keycloak. After login, display user's name and email at GET /dashboard using @AuthenticationPrincipal OidcUser user. From the same controller, call a downstream REST API passing the stored access token from OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager.

Correct output: Browser → /dashboard → Keycloak login → redirect back → page shows Welcome, Alice (alice@example.com) and downstream API response. Inspect HTTP traffic: the redirect URL must contain code_challenge and code_challenge_method=S256 parameters.

Edge case: The OAuth2 state parameter prevents CSRF on the redirect callback. Forge a callback: GET /login/oauth2/code/keycloak?code=abc&state=forged. Spring must return 401 and log OAuth2AuthenticationException: [authorization_request_not_found]. Write a MockMvc test verifying this exact rejection path.

5.3 Enterprise Cross-Cutting Concerns & Production Security

Sub-Concept 6: CORS & CSRF Defenses

CORS is enforced by browsers. When a browser makes a cross-origin request, it sends a preflight OPTIONS first. Your server must respond with correct Access-Control-Allow-* headers or the browser blocks the actual request. In Spring Security apps, CORS must be configured in the SecurityFilterChain via http.cors() — otherwise security filters block preflight requests before CORS headers are applied.

CSRF tricks an authenticated user's browser into making unauthorized requests. The defense: the server embeds a secret CSRF token in each response that browsers cannot read cross-origin, and requires it in every state-changing request. For SPAs, CookieCsrfTokenRepository.withHttpOnlyFalse() stores the token in a readable JavaScript cookie (XSRF-TOKEN) which the SPA reads and sends as the X-XSRF-TOKEN header — readable only by same-origin JavaScript.

java 
// Spring Security 6.x — CORS + SPA-compatible CSRF
@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
public class WebSecurityConfig {

    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        return http
            .cors(cors -> cors.configurationSource(corsConfigurationSource()))
            .csrf(csrf -> csrf
                .csrfTokenRepository(CookieCsrfTokenRepository.withHttpOnlyFalse())
                .csrfTokenRequestHandler(new XorCsrfTokenRequestAttributeHandler()))
            .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth.anyRequest().authenticated())
            .build();
    }

    @Bean
    public CorsConfigurationSource corsConfigurationSource() {
        CorsConfiguration cfg = new CorsConfiguration();
        cfg.setAllowedOrigins(List.of("https://app.mycompany.com"));  // never use *
        cfg.setAllowedMethods(List.of("GET", "POST", "PUT", "DELETE", "OPTIONS"));
        cfg.setAllowedHeaders(List.of("Authorization", "Content-Type", "X-XSRF-TOKEN"));
        cfg.setAllowCredentials(true);   // required for CSRF cookie to work cross-origin
        cfg.setMaxAge(3600L);
        UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource src = new UrlBasedCorsConfigurationSource();
        src.registerCorsConfiguration("/**", cfg);
        return src;
    }
}

// WebMvcConfigurer (for non-security filter CORS — e.g. @RestController methods)
@Configuration
public class WebMvcConfig implements WebMvcConfigurer {
    @Override
    public void addCorsMappings(CorsRegistry registry) {
        registry.addMapping("/api/**")
            .allowedOrigins("https://app.mycompany.com")
            .allowedMethods("GET", "POST", "PUT")
            .allowCredentials(true).maxAge(3600);
    }
}
Analogy: The Bank Counter Partition (CORS) & Secret Handshake (CSRF)

CORS is the bank's counter window — it defines exactly which customers (origins) can approach which teller (endpoint) and what documents (headers) they can hand through. A customer from a rival bank (cross-origin) is turned away by the bouncer (browser enforcement) unless the teller posted their name on the approved visitor list. CSRF is the bank's secret handshake for wire transfers: when you call to authorize a transaction, they read back a one-time code you generated earlier (the CSRF token). If someone tricks you into calling the bank on their behalf, they can make the call but can't reproduce the code — transaction rejected. CookieCsrfTokenRepository.withHttpOnlyFalse() is the modern version: your React app reads the code from a cookie and whispers it back in X-XSRF-TOKEN on every state-changing request.

Deep Insight: CORS Preflight Failures & Spring Security 6 CSRF Deferred Token Model
Homework: SPA-Compatible CSRF + CORS Security

Build: A Spring Boot API on port 8080 and a minimal SPA served via Python's http.server on port 3000. The SPA fetches with credentials: 'include'. On first load it calls GET /api/csrf to read the XSRF-TOKEN cookie, then includes X-XSRF-TOKEN: <value> in all POST requests.

Correct output: curl -c cookies.txt http://localhost:8080/api/csrf sets XSRF-TOKEN. curl -b cookies.txt -X POST /api/orders -H "X-XSRF-TOKEN: <value>" → 201 Created. Same POST without header → 403 Forbidden.

Edge case: In Spring Security 6, CSRF token handling is deferred — the cookie may be absent from a GET response if the token hasn't been committed. Fix by writing a filter that forces token commitment on every GET to /api/csrf: CsrfToken token = (CsrfToken) request.getAttribute(CsrfToken.class.getName()); token.getToken(); — this forces the token to be written to the response cookie.

Sub-Concept 7: Distributed Caching (Redis/Hazelcast)

Spring Cache abstracts your backend behind annotations: @Cacheable checks the cache first and only executes the method on a miss, @CachePut always executes and updates the cache, @CacheEvict removes entries on mutations. The abstraction works identically whether backed by an in-memory ConcurrentMapCacheManager, Redis, or Hazelcast.

Three classic distributed cache failure modes: Cache Avalanche — many entries expire simultaneously causing a DB spike (fix: TTL jitter). Cache Stampede / Thundering Herd — a popular key expires and hundreds of threads simultaneously miss and query the DB (fix: distributed lock via Redisson). Cache Penetration — invalid keys that never exist in the DB bypass the cache on every request (fix: cache null results or use a Bloom filter).

java 
// RedisCacheManager with per-cache TTL + jitter (anti-avalanche)
@Configuration
@EnableCaching
public class CacheConfig {

    @Bean
    public RedisCacheManager cacheManager(RedisConnectionFactory factory) {
        RedisCacheConfiguration defaults = RedisCacheConfiguration.defaultCacheConfig()
            .entryTtl(Duration.ofMinutes(10))
            .serializeValuesWith(RedisSerializationContext.SerializationPair
                .fromSerializer(new GenericJackson2JsonRedisSerializer()));

        Map<String, RedisCacheConfiguration> configs = Map.of(
            "products", defaults.entryTtl(Duration.ofMinutes(jittered(30))),
            "users",    defaults.entryTtl(Duration.ofMinutes(jittered(15))),
            "config",   defaults.entryTtl(Duration.ofHours(1))
        );
        return RedisCacheManager.builder(factory)
            .cacheDefaults(defaults).withInitialCacheConfigurations(configs).build();
    }

    private long jittered(long base) {
        return base + ThreadLocalRandom.current().nextLong(0, 5);
    }
}

// Spring Cache annotations on service layer
@Service
public class ProductService {

    @Cacheable(value = "products", key = "#id", unless = "#result == null")
    public ProductDto findById(Long id) {
        return repository.findById(id).map(mapper::toDto).orElse(null);
    }

    @CachePut(value = "products", key = "#result.id")
    public ProductDto update(ProductRequest req) {
        return mapper.toDto(repository.save(mapper.toEntity(req)));
    }

    @CacheEvict(value = "products", key = "#id")
    public void delete(Long id) { repository.deleteById(id); }

    // Thundering Herd mitigation via Redisson distributed lock
    public ProductDto findByIdWithLock(Long id, RedissonClient redisson) {
        RLock lock = redisson.getLock("lock:product:" + id);
        try {
            if (lock.tryLock(500, 3000, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS)) {
                ProductDto cached = cacheManager.getCache("products").get(id, ProductDto.class);
                if (cached != null) return cached;  // another thread already populated it
                return findById(id);               // only one thread hits DB
            }
        } finally {
            if (lock.isHeldByCurrentThread()) lock.unlock();
        }
        return findById(id);
    }
}
Analogy: The Bank Safety Deposit Vault Express Window

A cache is the bank's express teller window pre-stocked with the 50 most-requested documents from the vault (database). Cache Avalanche: all teller documents expire at exactly 5pm Friday — every customer simultaneously hammers the vault, which collapses. Fix: stagger document expiry times (TTL jitter). Cache Stampede: 200 customers all request the same rare document that just expired, all 200 march into the vault at once. Fix: post a sign — "One person at a time, take a number" (distributed lock) — the first person photocopies the document for everyone else. Cache Penetration: someone continuously requests documents that have never existed, bypassing the express window every time. Fix: keep a "known non-existent" ledger at the express window (cache null results) or a pre-check list at the door (Bloom filter).

Deep Insight: Cache Failure Modes & Distributed Lock Patterns
Homework: Production Redis Cache with Stampede Protection

Build: A ProductService backed by Redis with per-cache TTL jitter (±5 minutes on a 30-minute base). Add GET /api/products/{id}. Simulate 500 concurrent requests to an expired key using ab -c 500 -n 500. Verify only one DB query fires by counting log lines from the repository method.

Correct output: Under load: CACHE MISS → DB QUERY for id=42 appears exactly once; subsequent 499 requests show CACHE HIT for id=42. redis-cli TTL products::42 returns 1500–1800 seconds (jitter applied).

Edge case: Cache penetration — call GET /api/products/99999 (non-existent) 1000 times. Without mitigation: 1000 DB hits. Fix: change unless = "#result == null" to allow null caching with a short-TTL sentinel cache config. Verify DB is hit only once per unique non-existent key per TTL window.

Sub-Concept 8: Enterprise Scheduling & Async Execution

@Scheduled runs tasks on a fixed schedule using a TaskScheduler backed by a thread pool. The default scheduler uses a single thread — if a task overruns its interval, subsequent executions delay and pile up. Always configure a ThreadPoolTaskScheduler with 2–4 threads in production. @Async runs methods on a separate thread pool, returning immediately. Critical rule: @Async only works when called from outside the bean (Spring proxy intercepts the call) — calling from within the same class executes synchronously.

In a clustered environment, @Scheduled runs on every instance — a nightly report job fires N times. ShedLock solves this: each instance tries to acquire a named lock in the database (or Redis) before executing; only one succeeds, the rest skip. The lock auto-releases after a maximum duration if the winning instance crashes.

java 
// ThreadPoolTaskScheduler + ThreadPoolTaskExecutor for production
@Configuration
@EnableScheduling
@EnableAsync
public class TaskConfig {

    @Bean
    public TaskScheduler taskScheduler() {
        ThreadPoolTaskScheduler s = new ThreadPoolTaskScheduler();
        s.setPoolSize(4);
        s.setThreadNamePrefix("scheduled-");
        s.setErrorHandler(t -> log.error("Task failed: {}", t.getMessage(), t));
        s.setWaitForTasksToCompleteOnShutdown(true);
        s.setAwaitTerminationSeconds(30);
        return s;
    }

    @Bean
    public Executor asyncExecutor() {
        ThreadPoolTaskExecutor e = new ThreadPoolTaskExecutor();
        e.setCorePoolSize(5);
        e.setMaxPoolSize(20);
        e.setQueueCapacity(100);
        e.setThreadNamePrefix("async-");
        e.setRejectedExecutionHandler(new ThreadPoolExecutor.CallerRunsPolicy());
        e.initialize();
        return e;
    }
}

// ShedLock — only ONE cluster node executes the job per trigger
@EnableSchedulerLock(defaultLockAtMostFor = "PT30M")
@SpringBootApplication
public class Application {}

@Component
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class ReportScheduler {

    @Scheduled(cron = "0 0 2 * * *")
    @SchedulerLock(name = "dailyReport",
                   lockAtLeastFor = "PT5M",  // hold minimum 5 min (prevent near-miss re-runs)
                   lockAtMostFor  = "PT20M") // auto-release after 20 min if node crashes
    public void generateDailyReport() {
        log.info("Daily report on instance: {}", instanceId);
        reportService.generate();
    }
}

// @Async: correct external-call pattern — self-invocation skips the proxy!
@Service
public class EmailNotificationService {
    @Async("asyncExecutor")
    public CompletableFuture<Void> sendWelcomeEmail(String email) {
        log.info("Sending on thread: {}", Thread.currentThread().getName());
        mailClient.send(email);
        return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(null);
    }
}

// OrderService calls EmailNotificationService via injected Spring proxy — @Async works
@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class OrderService {
    private final EmailNotificationService emailService;  // Spring proxy

    public Order placeOrder(OrderRequest req) {
        Order order = repository.save(buildOrder(req));
        emailService.sendWelcomeEmail(req.getCustomerEmail());  // non-blocking
        return order;
    }
}
Analogy: The Night Shift Sign-Out Sheet (ShedLock)

Imagine a hospital with three night shift nurses, all receiving the same "generate morning report" alarm at 2 AM. Without coordination, all three start generating the same report simultaneously. ShedLock is the sign-out sheet at the nursing station: only the nurse who signs it first (acquires the DB lock row) does the work; the others see it's already signed and return to rounds. The sheet has a maximum hold time (lockAtMostFor) — if the first nurse has an emergency and can't cross it off, the sheet auto-resets after 20 minutes. @Async is the practice of dropping a task in the outbox tray for a mail runner (asyncExecutor pool) instead of delivering it yourself — you write the letter, drop it in the tray, and continue with patients immediately.

Deep Insight: Thread Pool Sizing, @Async Proxy Pitfalls & ShedLock Under Node Failure
Homework: Clustered Scheduler with ShedLock & Async Email

Build: A Spring Boot app with @Scheduled(fixedRate = 10000) protected by @SchedulerLock(name = "heartbeat", lockAtMostFor = "PT1M"). Run two instances on ports 8080 and 8081 sharing the same Postgres/H2. Add @Async email in UserRegistrationService.register().

Correct output: Heartbeat logs appear on only one instance at a time; the other logs Lock already held — skipping. Kill the active instance mid-lock; within lockAtMostFor seconds the second instance acquires the lock. Email logs show thread name async-1.

Edge case: @Async silently fails when called from within the same bean (this.sendEmail()). Write a unit test proving this: call emailService.sendWelcomeEmail() directly on this via reflection and verify it runs on the calling thread. Then call it through the injected Spring proxy and verify thread name matches async-*.

5.4 Communications, Validation & Resilience

Spring Boot Mail with Templates

java 
// Maven: spring-boot-starter-mail + spring-boot-starter-thymeleaf

@Service
public class EmailService {

    private final JavaMailSender mailSender;
    private final TemplateEngine templateEngine;

    @Async("emailExecutor")  // Don't block HTTP response thread
    public void sendOrderConfirmation(Order order) {
        try {
            Context ctx = new Context();
            ctx.setVariable("order", order);
            ctx.setVariable("customerName", order.getCustomer().getName());

            String html = templateEngine.process("order-confirmation", ctx);

            MimeMessage message = mailSender.createMimeMessage();
            MimeMessageHelper helper = new MimeMessageHelper(message, true, "UTF-8");
            helper.setTo(order.getCustomer().getEmail());
            helper.setSubject("Order #" + order.getId() + " Confirmed");
            helper.setText(html, true);  // true = HTML content

            // Add invoice PDF attachment
            helper.addAttachment("invoice.pdf", new ByteArrayResource(invoicePdf));

            mailSender.send(message);
        } catch (MessagingException e) {
            log.error("Failed to send email for order {}", order.getId(), e);
            // Queue for retry via @Retryable or message queue
        }
    }
}

// Async executor configuration
@Configuration
public class AsyncConfig {
    @Bean("emailExecutor")
    public Executor emailExecutor() {
        ThreadPoolTaskExecutor executor = new ThreadPoolTaskExecutor();
        executor.setCorePoolSize(2);
        executor.setMaxPoolSize(5);
        executor.setQueueCapacity(500);
        executor.setThreadNamePrefix("email-");
        executor.initialize();
        return executor;
    }
}

Data Validation with Bean Validation (JSR-380)

java 
// DTO with validation constraints
public record CreateUserRequest(
    @NotBlank(message = "Username is required")
    @Size(min = 3, max = 50, message = "Username must be 3-50 characters")
    @Pattern(regexp = "^[a-zA-Z0-9_]+$", message = "Only alphanumeric and underscore")
    String username,

    @NotBlank
    @Email(message = "Must be a valid email address")
    String email,

    @NotBlank
    @Size(min = 8)
    @Pattern(regexp = ".*[A-Z].*", message = "Must contain uppercase letter")
    @Pattern(regexp = ".*[0-9].*", message = "Must contain digit")
    String password,

    @Min(18) @Max(120)
    int age
) {}

// Controller with validation
@PostMapping
public ResponseEntity<UserDto> createUser(@RequestBody @Valid CreateUserRequest request) {
    // If validation fails, MethodArgumentNotValidException is thrown
    // Global exception handler returns 400 with field errors
    return ResponseEntity.ok(userService.create(request));
}

// Global exception handler
@RestControllerAdvice
public class ValidationExceptionHandler {
    @ExceptionHandler(MethodArgumentNotValidException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<Map<String, String>> handleValidation(MethodArgumentNotValidException ex) {
        Map<String, String> errors = new HashMap<>();
        ex.getBindingResult().getFieldErrors().forEach(error ->
            errors.put(error.getField(), error.getDefaultMessage())
        );
        return ResponseEntity.badRequest().body(errors);
    }
}

Object Mapping with MapStruct

java 
// MapStruct: Compile-time code generation (no runtime reflection)
// Much faster than ModelMapper or Orika

@Mapper(componentModel = "spring", unmappedTargetPolicy = ReportingPolicy.IGNORE)
public interface UserMapper {

    UserMapper INSTANCE = Mappers.getMapper(UserMapper.class);

    // Entity -> DTO
    UserDto toDto(User entity);

    // DTO -> Entity
    User toEntity(CreateUserRequest dto);

    // Custom mapping logic
    @Mapping(target = "fullName", expression = "java(entity.getFirstName() + \" \" + entity.getLastName())")
    @Mapping(target = "memberSince", source = "createdAt", dateFormat = "yyyy-MM-dd")
    UserProfileDto toProfileDto(User entity);

    // List mapping
    List<UserDto> toDtoList(List<User> entities);
}

// Generated code at compile time (no reflection!):
// @Component
// public class UserMapperImpl implements UserMapper {
//     @Override
//     public UserDto toDto(User entity) {
//         if (entity == null) return null;
//         return new UserDto(entity.getId(), entity.getUsername(), ...);
//     }
// }

Resilience4j: Circuit Breaker, Retry & Rate Limiting

java 
// Resilience4j annotations for microservice fault tolerance
@Service
public class PaymentGatewayClient {

    // Circuit Breaker: Open after 50% failure rate, wait 30s before retry
    @CircuitBreaker(name = "payment", fallbackMethod = "processPaymentFallback")
    @Retry(name = "payment")       // Retry 3 times with exponential backoff
    @TimeLimiter(name = "payment")   // Timeout after 5 seconds
    public CompletableFuture<PaymentResult> processPayment(PaymentRequest request) {
        return CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> {
            // Call external payment gateway
            return restClient.post()
                .uri("/api/v1/payments")
                .body(request)
                .retrieve()
                .body(PaymentResult.class);
        });
    }

    // Fallback: called when circuit is open or all retries exhausted
    private CompletableFuture<PaymentResult> processPaymentFallback(
            PaymentRequest request, Exception ex) {
        log.warn("Payment failed, queueing for retry: {}", request.getId());
        // Queue to dead-letter queue for later processing
        messageQueue.send("payment-dlq", request);
        return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(
            PaymentResult.queued(request.getId()));
    }
}

// Rate limiting: prevent API abuse
@RateLimiter(name = "publicApi", fallbackMethod = "rateLimitFallback")
@GetMapping("/public/data")
public List<DataDto> getPublicData() {
    return dataService.findAll();
}

// application.yml configuration:
resilience4j:
  circuitbreaker:
    instances:
      payment:
        slidingWindowSize: 10
        failureRateThreshold: 50
        waitDurationInOpenState: 30s
        permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState: 3
  retry:
    instances:
      payment:
        maxAttempts: 3
        waitDuration: 1s
        exponentialBackoffMultiplier: 2
  ratelimiter:
    instances:
      publicApi:
        limitForPeriod: 100
        limitRefreshPeriod: 1m
        timeoutDuration: 0
Production Gotcha: Circuit Breaker Misconfiguration

The most common Circuit Breaker mistake is setting the sliding window too small. With slidingWindowSize: 5, a single failure gives 20% failure rate - too sensitive for transient network blips. Use at least 100 for production. Also, always provide fallback methods - without them, the CB throws CallNotPermittedException to the caller. Never combine CB with synchronous HTTP calls inside a loop - the CB state is per-instance, so it may not trigger correctly. Use @TimeLimiter with CompletableFuture to prevent hanging threads. Monitor CB metrics via Actuator + Micrometer for early warning.

Phase 6

Enterprise Messaging & Microservices

6.1 Message Queues [RabbitMQ / Kafka]

Deep Dive: Why Asynchronous Messaging Exists

Synchronous REST calls couple a caller's lifetime to a callee's lifetime: if the downstream service is slow, down, or overwhelmed, the caller blocks or fails. Message brokers decouple temporally (the consumer doesn't need to be running when the producer sends) and spatially (the producer doesn't need to know which service, or how many instances, will consume the message). RabbitMQ and Kafka solve this problem with fundamentally different storage models — RabbitMQ is a smart broker with dumb consumers (it actively pushes and tracks per-message state), while Kafka is a dumb broker with smart consumers (it's an append-only log, and consumers track their own position). Choosing between them is an architectural decision, not a popularity contest.

RabbitMQ Exchange Types & Spring AMQP

RabbitMQ implements AMQP 0-9-1. A publisher never sends directly to a queue — it always sends to an exchange, which routes the message to zero or more bound queues based on the exchange type and routing key.

AMQP Exchange Types & Routing Behavior
Exchange TypeRouting LogicTypical Use Case
directExact match between routing key and binding keyTask queues — route order.created to exactly one queue
topicWildcard match: * matches one word, # matches zero or more wordsSelective broadcast — *.error catches payment.error and shipping.error
fanoutIgnores routing key entirely; broadcasts to every bound queueCache invalidation events, audit logging fan-out
headersMatches on message header attributes instead of routing keyMulti-attribute routing (region + priority) without key string parsing
 
graph LR
    P[Producer] -->|publish| EX{Topic Exchange
orders.#}
    EX -->|orders.created| Q1[Queue: New Orders]
    EX -->|orders.cancelled| Q2[Queue: Cancellations]
    EX -->|orders.#| Q3[Queue: Audit Log]
    Q1 --> C1[Consumer A]
    Q2 --> C2[Consumer B]
    Q3 --> C3[Consumer C]

    style EX fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style Q1 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style Q2 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#ffa657,color:#fff
    style Q3 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff

Spring AMQP wraps the AMQP client with @RabbitListener for consumption and RabbitTemplate for publishing. Below, a topic exchange routes order events, with a Dead Letter Exchange (DLX) catching messages that exhaust retries — the standard defense against poison pills (malformed messages that crash a consumer repeatedly and block the queue head).

java 
// Declarative topology: exchange, queue, DLX, and binding
@Configuration
public class OrderQueueConfig {

    public static final String EXCHANGE = "orders.exchange";
    public static final String DLX = "orders.dlx";

    @Bean
    TopicExchange ordersExchange() { return new TopicExchange(EXCHANGE); }

    @Bean
    Queue newOrdersQueue() {
        return QueueBuilder.durable("orders.new")
            .withArgument("x-dead-letter-exchange", DLX)
            .withArgument("x-dead-letter-routing-key", "orders.failed")
            .withArgument("x-message-ttl", 30000) // 30s before DLX retry
            .build();
    }

    @Bean
    Binding newOrdersBinding(Queue newOrdersQueue, TopicExchange ordersExchange) {
        return BindingBuilder.bind(newOrdersQueue).to(ordersExchange).with("orders.created");
    }
}

// Consumer with manual ack and exponential backoff retry via RetryTemplate
@Component
public class OrderListener {

    @RabbitListener(queues = "orders.new", ackMode = "MANUAL")
    public void handleOrder(OrderCreatedEvent event, Channel channel,
                            @Header(AmqpHeaders.DELIVERY_TAG) long tag) throws IOException {
        try {
            orderProcessor.process(event);
            channel.basicAck(tag, false);
        } catch (PoisonPillException ex) {
            channel.basicNack(tag, false, false); // requeue=false -> routes to DLX
        } catch (TransientException ex) {
            channel.basicNack(tag, false, true);  // requeue=true -> retry immediately
        }
    }
}
Analogy: The Postal Sorting Hub

A RabbitMQ exchange is a postal sorting hub, never a mailbox. You never hand a letter directly to a recipient — you hand it to the hub with an address label (routing key). A direct exchange is a hub clerk who matches the exact zip code to one sorting bin. A topic exchange is a clerk who reads partial addresses with wildcards — "anything in the 90*** error district" — and routes to multiple bins that match the pattern. A fanout exchange is the hub making a photocopy of every letter and dropping a copy in every single bin regardless of address, like an "all residents" mailer. A message that keeps getting returned as undeliverable after several attempts eventually gets pulled aside to a special "dead letter" bin (the DLX) so it doesn't jam the regular sorting line for everyone else.

Deep Insight: DLX Retry Storms & Poison Pill Architecture
Homework: Resilient Order Pipeline with DLX Retry

Build: A Spring Boot service with a topic exchange orders.exchange, a primary queue orders.new bound on orders.created, a DLX orders.dlx, and a retry queue with a TTL that re-routes failed messages back to the primary queue up to 3 times before landing in a terminal orders.parked queue.

Expected output: Publishing a malformed JSON payload via rabbitTemplate.convertAndSend("orders.exchange", "orders.created", badPayload) should produce three retry attempts visible in your logs (~30s apart), then a final message in orders.parked. Verify via the RabbitMQ Management UI (http://localhost:15672) that the parked queue's message count increments exactly once — not three times.

Edge case: What happens if your consumer crashes (OOM, pod restart) after processing the order but before calling channel.basicAck()? Explain why RabbitMQ will redeliver the message to another consumer, and why your processOrder() logic must be idempotent (e.g., via an orders_processed dedupe table keyed on order ID) to avoid double-charging a customer.

Apache Kafka Architecture & Spring Kafka

Kafka is not a message queue in the traditional sense — it's a distributed, append-only commit log. A topic is split into partitions, each an ordered, immutable sequence of records identified by a monotonically increasing offset. Each partition is replicated across brokers (controlled by replication.factor); one replica is the leader handling all reads/writes, the rest are followers that replicate for durability and failover.

graph TB
    subgraph Topic: payments [replication.factor=3]
        P0[Partition 0
Leader: Broker-1]
        P1[Partition 1
Leader: Broker-2]
        P2[Partition 2
Leader: Broker-3]
    end
    Prod[Producer] --> P0
    Prod --> P1
    Prod --> P2
    P0 --> CG1A[Consumer A
group: billing-svc]
    P1 --> CG1B[Consumer B
group: billing-svc]
    P2 --> CG1C[Consumer C
group: billing-svc]

    style P0 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style P1 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#58a6ff,color:#fff

Within a consumer group, each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer instance — this is how Kafka achieves parallelism while preserving per-partition ordering. Add a 4th consumer to a 3-partition topic and it sits idle; remove a consumer and Kafka triggers a rebalance, reassigning that consumer's partitions to the survivors.

yaml
spring:
  kafka:
    bootstrap-servers: localhost:9092
    producer:
      acks: all                          # wait for all in-sync replicas
      properties:
        enable.idempotence: true          # dedupes producer retries server-side
        max.in.flight.requests.per.connection: 5
    consumer:
      group-id: billing-svc
      auto-offset-reset: earliest
      enable-auto-commit: false            # manual ack -> commit only after DB write succeeds
      isolation-level: read_committed     # hide uncommitted transactional records
java
// Idempotent, transactional producer + manual-ack consumer
@Service
public class PaymentEventProducer {
    private final KafkaTemplate<String, PaymentEvent> kafkaTemplate;

    public void publish(PaymentEvent event) {
        kafkaTemplate.send("payments", event.orderId(), event)
            .whenComplete((result, ex) -> {
                if (ex != null) log.error("Publish failed for {}", event.orderId(), ex);
            });
    }
}

@Component
public class PaymentEventConsumer {

    @KafkaListener(topics = "payments", groupId = "billing-svc",
                    containerFactory = "manualAckContainerFactory")
    public void consume(ConsumerRecord<String, PaymentEvent> record, Acknowledgment ack) {
        if (ledgerRepository.existsByEventId(record.value().eventId())) {
            ack.acknowledge(); // already processed -> skip but still commit offset
            return;
        }
        ledgerService.applyPayment(record.value());
        ack.acknowledge(); // commit offset ONLY after the DB write succeeds
    }
}
Analogy: The Stock Exchange Order Book Tape

A Kafka topic is the exchange's ticker tape — every trade is appended in order and never erased; trade #4,521 always stays trade #4,521 (the offset) no matter how many times you re-read the tape. A partition is one ticker tape lane out of several running in parallel, each handling a slice of the alphabet of stock symbols so multiple clerks (consumers) can work simultaneously without bumping elbows. A consumer group is a single trading desk: assign each clerk on the desk to their own lane, and if a clerk goes home sick (instance dies), the desk supervisor (the group coordinator) reassigns that clerk's lane to someone else still on shift — that reassignment pause is the rebalance, and trades briefly stop being read on that lane while the handoff happens.

Deep Insight: Offset Management, Rebalancing & Exactly-Once Cost
Homework: Manual-Offset Consumer with Rebalance Logging

Build: A 3-partition topic shipments with a Spring Kafka consumer group running 2 instances. Implement ConsumerAwareRebalanceListener to log onPartitionsRevoked and onPartitionsAssigned events. Use manual AckMode.MANUAL_IMMEDIATE and commit offsets only after writing to a local Postgres table.

Expected output: Starting a 3rd consumer instance should print rebalance logs on all 3 instances showing the partition reassignment, e.g. Partitions revoked: [shipments-2] followed by Partitions assigned: [shipments-2] on the new instance — with zero gaps or duplicate processing visible in the Postgres table's primary key constraint.

Edge case: Kill (kill -9) a consumer instance mid-batch, after it has called your business logic for records 1–7 of a 10-record poll but before committing any offsets. Document what happens to records 1–7 when the rebalanced partition lands on a surviving consumer — they will be reprocessed from the last committed offset. Explain why this proves Kafka only guarantees at-least-once delivery by default, and what code change (idempotent upsert vs. transactional outbox commit) closes that gap.

Schema Evolution with Avro & Confluent Schema Registry

Once two or more services share an event format, that format becomes a contract. Avro pairs each message with a compact binary encoding and a schema (the Confluent Schema Registry stores schemas by topic and serves them by ID, so payloads don't need to embed the full schema). Compatibility rules govern whether a schema change breaks existing producers or consumers:

Avro Schema Compatibility Modes
ModeRuleSafe Change Example
BACKWARDNew schema can read data written with the previous schemaAdd a field with a default value; remove a field
FORWARDOld schema can read data written with the new schemaRemove a field with a default; add a field is rejected unless deletable
FULLBoth backward and forward holdAdd or remove fields, but only ones that carry a default value
yaml
spring:
  kafka:
    producer:
      properties:
        schema.registry.url: http://schema-registry:8081
        value.serializer: io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroSerializer
        auto.register.schemas: false  # register via CI pipeline, never at runtime in prod
    consumer:
      properties:
        schema.registry.url: http://schema-registry:8081
        value.deserializer: io.confluent.kafka.serializers.KafkaAvroDeserializer
        specific.avro.reader: true

The safest production rollout order under BACKWARD compatibility is consumer-first: deploy consumers that can tolerate the new field before any producer starts emitting it. Adding a field is only safe if it has a "default" value in the .avsc definition — without a default, old records lacking that field can't be deserialized by readers expecting it.

Analogy: A Newspaper Publishing Syndicate's House Style Guide

The Schema Registry is the syndicate's central style guide office that every affiliated newspaper (consumer) must check before typesetting a wire story (event). If the wire service (producer) wants to add a new field to its story template — say, a "photo credit" line — it can only do so by giving every paper a sensible default caption to use if the field is blank, otherwise some papers' printing presses (deserializers) jam trying to lay out a column that doesn't exist in their template. Backward compatibility means a newspaper using this week's template can still typeset last week's wire stories; forward compatibility means a newspaper still running last week's template can typeset this week's stories without crashing.

Deep Insight: Zero-Downtime Schema Migration Strategy
Homework: Backward-Compatible Field Addition Under Live Traffic

Build: An Avro schema OrderEvent.avsc with fields orderId, amount. Register it under BACKWARD compatibility. Deploy a consumer, then evolve the schema to add currencyCode with "default": "USD", re-register, and deploy a new producer that starts emitting the field.

Expected output: The Schema Registry's POST /compatibility/subjects/{subject}/versions/latest check should return {"is_compatible": true} for the addition. Old consumer instances still on v1 should keep consuming v2 messages without error (ignoring the new field); new consumers should see currencyCode="USD" on any message produced before the rollout.

Edge case: Attempt to register a schema that removes amount without a default value under BACKWARD mode. Document the registry's 409 Conflict rejection and explain, in terms of old consumers reading new data, exactly which deserialization step would fail if the registry didn't block it.

6.2 Event-Driven Architectures

Deep Dive: The Dual-Write Problem

A service that writes to its own database and then publishes a message in two separate steps has a correctness bug, not an edge case. If the process crashes between the DB commit and the broker publish — or the broker is briefly unreachable — the event is silently lost, and downstream services never learn the write happened. Conversely, if you publish first and the DB write then fails, you've announced something that never happened. There is no way to make a relational database commit and a network call to a broker atomic with a plain two-step approach. Every pattern in this section exists to close that gap.

Outbox Pattern & Transactional Event Publishing

sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application
    participant DB as Database (single TX)
    participant Poller as Outbox Poller
    participant Broker as Kafka

    App->>DB: INSERT orders row
    App->>DB: INSERT outbox row (same TX)
    DB-->>App: COMMIT (atomic)
    Poller->>DB: SELECT unsent outbox rows
    Poller->>Broker: publish event
    Broker-->>Poller: ack
    Poller->>DB: mark outbox row sent

The Transactional Outbox Pattern writes the domain change and a serialized event into an outbox table inside the same local database transaction. Because both inserts share one ACID transaction, they either both succeed or both roll back — there is no window where one happened without the other. A separate poller (or a CDC tool like Debezium tailing the database's write-ahead log) then reads unsent rows and publishes them to the broker, marking them sent only after a broker ack.

java
// Single local transaction: domain write + outbox write
@Service
public class OrderService {

    @Transactional
    public void createOrder(CreateOrderCommand cmd) {
        Order order = orderRepository.save(Order.from(cmd));

        OutboxEvent event = new OutboxEvent(
            UUID.randomUUID(), "OrderCreated",
            objectMapper.writeValueAsString(new OrderCreatedEvent(order.getId(), order.getTotal())),
            OutboxStatus.PENDING, Instant.now()
        );
        outboxRepository.save(event); // SAME transaction as the order insert
    }
}

// Polling publisher — runs every 500ms, batches and marks sent
@Component
public class OutboxPoller {

    @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 500)
    @Transactional
    public void publishPending() {
        List<OutboxEvent> batch = outboxRepository.findTop100ByStatusOrderByCreatedAtAsc(OutboxStatus.PENDING);
        for (OutboxEvent evt : batch) {
            kafkaTemplate.send("order-events", evt.getAggregateId(), evt.getPayload())
                .thenRun(() -> evt.markSent());
        }
        outboxRepository.saveAll(batch);
    }
}
Analogy: The Relay Race Baton Handoff

In a relay race, the rule is simple: a runner cannot let go of the baton until the next runner's hand is closed around it inside the changeover box. That changeover box is the database transaction — the domain write and the outbox write happen "inside the box" together, so the baton (the event) is never dropped mid-handoff and never picked up before it's actually been passed. The poller is the race official walking the track afterward to confirm every baton that entered a changeover box made it to the next leg, re-handing any baton it finds still sitting in the box.

Deep Insight: Outbox Transaction Boundaries & Poller Race Conditions
Homework: Outbox Poller with Idempotent Downstream Consumer

Build: An orders table and outbox table written in one @Transactional method. A separate @Scheduled poller publishing unsent rows to Kafka. A downstream consumer that writes to a processed_events table keyed by the outbox event's UUID before applying business logic.

Expected output: Creating 50 orders concurrently should result in exactly 50 rows in outbox with status='SENT', 50 Kafka messages, and 50 rows in the downstream processed_events table — verified by SELECT COUNT(*) matching across all three.

Edge case: Kill the poller process immediately after kafkaTemplate.send() returns its future successfully, but before the UPDATE outbox SET status='SENT' commits. On restart, the poller will re-select that same row as PENDING and republish it — a duplicate delivery. Prove your downstream consumer's processed_events uniqueness constraint silently absorbs the duplicate, and explain why removing manual idempotency and relying on Kafka's enable.idempotence=true producer setting alone would not fix this particular race, since the duplication originates in the poller's commit ordering, not the producer.

Saga Pattern for Distributed Transactions

Two-phase commit (2PC) requires a coordinator to hold locks across every participating database until all parties vote to commit — under microservices, where each service owns its own database and may live across network partitions, those locks become unacceptable availability risks. The Saga pattern replaces one atomic transaction with a sequence of local transactions, each with a defined compensating transaction that semantically undoes it if a later step fails.

Choreography vs. Orchestration Sagas
AspectChoreographyOrchestration
ControlEach service reacts to events from others; no central brainA central orchestrator issues explicit commands
CouplingLoosely coupled, but implicit business logic spread across servicesBusiness logic centralized and explicit in one place
DebuggabilityHard to trace a saga's full path — must follow events across logsEasy — orchestrator's state machine shows exactly where the saga is
Best fitFew participants, simple linear flowsMany participants, complex branching/rollback logic
 
sequenceDiagram
    participant O as Orchestrator
    participant Ord as Order Service
    participant Pay as Payment Service
    participant Inv as Inventory Service

    O->>Ord: CreateOrder
    Ord-->>O: OrderCreated
    O->>Pay: ChargeCard
    Pay-->>O: PaymentFailed
    O->>Ord: CompensateOrder (cancel)
    Ord-->>O: OrderCancelled
java
// Orchestration with Spring State Machine — explicit compensating step
@Configuration
@EnableStateMachine
public class OrderSagaConfig extends StateMachineConfigurerAdapter<SagaState, SagaEvent> {

    @Override
    public void configure(StateMachineTransitionConfigurer<SagaState, SagaEvent> transitions) throws Exception {
        transitions
            .withExternal().source(SagaState.ORDER_CREATED).target(SagaState.PAYMENT_PENDING)
                .event(SagaEvent.CHARGE_CARD).action(chargeCardAction())
            .and()
            .withExternal().source(SagaState.PAYMENT_PENDING).target(SagaState.COMPENSATING)
                .event(SagaEvent.PAYMENT_FAILED).action(compensateOrderAction());
    }

    private Action<SagaState, SagaEvent> compensateOrderAction() {
        return ctx -> orderService.cancel(ctx.getExtendedState().get("orderId", UUID.class));
    }
}
Analogy: A Multi-Leg Honeymoon Booking

Booking a flight, then a hotel, then a rental car is a saga, not a single atomic transaction — no travel website can lock all three providers' inventory at once and commit them together like a database. If the flight and hotel book fine but the rental car is sold out, you don't unbook the flight by magic; you call the airline and request a refund — a compensating transaction that undoes the earlier local success on its own terms, which may even have its own fee or delay. An orchestration saga is a human travel agent who calls each provider in sequence and personally issues the cancellation calls if something downstream fails; a choreography saga is the equivalent of each provider's confirmation email automatically triggering the next booking website, with no single travel agent watching the whole trip.

Deep Insight: Compensation Failure & Saga Rollback Complexity
Homework: Orchestrated Order Saga with Forced Compensation Failure

Build: Three services — Order, Payment, Inventory — coordinated by a Spring State Machine orchestrator. Happy path: CreateOrder → ChargeCard → ReserveInventory → Complete. Failure path: if inventory reservation fails, the orchestrator must issue a RefundPayment compensating command followed by CancelOrder.

Expected output: Forcing an inventory failure (return HTTP 409 from a mocked Inventory Service) should produce orchestrator logs showing state transitions PAYMENT_COMPLETED → COMPENSATING → REFUNDED → CANCELLED, and the order's final DB status should be CANCELLED, never left at PAYMENT_COMPLETED.

Edge case: Make the RefundPayment compensating call itself fail (simulate the payment gateway timing out during refund). Document how your orchestrator handles a failed compensation — this is the hardest part of Saga design, since there's no further compensation for a compensation. The correct production answer is to move the saga into a terminal MANUAL_INTERVENTION_REQUIRED state with full context persisted, and alert an on-call human, rather than silently retrying a compensating action indefinitely.

CQRS & Event Sourcing

Command Query Responsibility Segregation splits the model that handles writes (commands, validated against business invariants) from the model that handles reads (denormalized projections, optimized for query patterns). Event Sourcing is the natural write-side companion: instead of persisting current state, you persist the full ordered sequence of domain events that produced it, and current state becomes a fold over that sequence — replayable at any time to rebuild any read model from scratch.

graph LR
    Cmd[Command: PlaceOrder] --> Agg[Order Aggregate]
    Agg -->|appends| ES[(Event Store
OrderPlaced, ItemAdded, OrderShipped)]
    ES -->|projects| RM1[(Read Model:
Order Summary Table)]
    ES -->|projects| RM2[(Read Model:
Customer Order History)]
    Query[Query: GetOrderHistory] --> RM2

    style ES fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#d2a8ff,color:#fff
    style RM1 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff
    style RM2 fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#50fa7b,color:#fff

Replaying thousands of events on every load is expensive, so production event-sourced systems take periodic snapshots of aggregate state — replay resumes from the nearest snapshot plus only the events appended after it, rather than from event zero.

java
// Event-sourced aggregate, rebuilt from its event stream + snapshot
public class OrderAggregate {
    private UUID orderId;
    private OrderStatus status;
    private long version;

    public static OrderAggregate rehydrate(OrderSnapshot snapshot, List<DomainEvent> eventsSinceSnapshot) {
        OrderAggregate agg = new OrderAggregate(snapshot);
        eventsSinceSnapshot.forEach(agg::apply); // fold events on top of snapshot
        return agg;
    }

    public void apply(DomainEvent event) {
        switch (event) {
            case OrderPlaced e -> { this.status = OrderStatus.PLACED; }
            case OrderShipped e -> { this.status = OrderStatus.SHIPPED; }
            default -> { // ignore unrelated event types }
        }
        this.version++;
    }
}

// Projection consumer rebuilds the read-model table from the Kafka event log
@KafkaListener(topics = "order-events", groupId = "order-summary-projector")
public void project(DomainEvent event) {
    orderSummaryProjection.apply(event); // idempotent upsert into denormalized read table
}
Analogy: An Air Traffic Control Flight Log

Air traffic control never stores "where the plane currently is" as the sole fact — it logs every clearance, altitude change, and heading correction as an immutable timestamped event. The plane's current position is simply the result of replaying that log forward. A controller handing off a shift doesn't get a single "current state" snapshot with no history — they get the recent log plus a position summary, exactly like an event-sourced aggregate rehydrating from a snapshot plus subsequent events. CQRS is the difference between that raw clearance log (the write side, used by controllers issuing commands) and the simplified radar display pilots and supervisors actually look at (the read side, a fast projection optimized for "what does the sky look like right now," not "what's the full audit trail").

Deep Insight: Snapshot Strategy & Projection Replay Cost
Homework: Snapshot-Optimized Aggregate with Replayable Projection

Build: An event store table (aggregate_id, sequence_no, event_type, payload) for an Order aggregate, plus a snapshot table written every 50 events. A Kafka projection consumer that builds an order_summary read table from the same event stream.

Expected output: Loading an aggregate with 500 events should read at most 1 snapshot row plus ≤49 trailing events (never all 500), measurable by adding a counter to your event-fetch query and asserting it stays under 50. Truncating and replaying the entire order-events topic from offset 0 into a fresh, empty order_summary table should reproduce row-for-row identical results to the original table.

Edge case: Run the projection consumer twice over the same event range (simulating an at-least-once redelivery after a consumer crash mid-batch). If your projection uses a naive INSERT per event rather than an idempotent upsert keyed on (aggregate_id, sequence_no), document the duplicate rows that appear in order_summary, and explain why event sourcing's replayability guarantee is worthless without idempotent projections — replay is only safe if applying the same event twice produces the same end state as applying it once.

Phase 7

CI/CD, DevOps, Cloud Deployment & Code Maintenance

7.1 Version Control & CI/CD Pipelines

Deep Dive: Why CI/CD Matters

Manual deployments are the single biggest source of production incidents. "It worked on my machine" is not a deployment strategy. Continuous Integration (CI) means every code change is automatically built, tested, and validated. Continuous Deployment (CD) means validated changes are automatically deployed to production. This eliminates human error, ensures repeatable deployments, and enables rapid rollback when issues are detected. A mature CI/CD pipeline is the difference between deploying once per month (with anxiety) and deploying 50 times per day (with confidence).

Git Branching Strategies

Git Branching Strategy Comparison
StrategyBranch StructureBest ForRelease Cadence
GitFlowmain, develop, feature/*, release/*, hotfix/*Scheduled releases, enterpriseWeekly/Monthly
Trunk-Basedmain + short-lived feature branches (<1 day)CI/CD, rapid iterationMultiple per day
GitHub Flowmain + feature branches + PRsWeb apps, SaaSContinuous
Release Flowmain + release branches deployed directlyAzure DevOps shopsOn demand
Analogy: GitFlow is a Restaurant Kitchen

In GitFlow, main is the dining room (what customers see). develop is the prep kitchen (where dishes are assembled). Each feature/* branch is a chef experimenting with a new recipe. When ready, the recipe is merged to develop for tasting (integration testing). When enough recipes are ready, a release/* branch is created for final plating (staging). Only then does it go to main for serving to customers. If a customer finds a bug, a hotfix/* branch is created directly from main and deployed immediately.

GitHub Actions CI/CD Pipeline

sql 
# .github/workflows/main.yml - Production-grade CI/CD pipeline
name: CI/CD Pipeline

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0  # Full history for SonarQube analysis

      - name: Set up JDK 21
        uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with:
          java-version: '21'
          distribution: 'temurin'

      - name: Cache Maven dependencies
        uses: actions/cache@v3
        with:
          path: ~/.m2/repository
          key: ${{ runner.os }}-maven-${{ hashFiles('**/pom.xml') }}
          restore-keys: ${{ runner.os }}-maven-

      - name: Run Unit Tests
        run: ./mvnw test -Dtest="*UnitTest" -Dsurefire.failIfNoSpecifiedTests=false

      - name: Run Integration Tests
        run: ./mvnw verify -Pit -Dspring.profiles.active=test

      - name: Generate Coverage Report
        run: ./mvnw jacoco:report

      - name: Upload coverage to Codecov
        uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3
        with:
          file: ./target/site/jacoco/jacoco.xml
          fail_ci_if_error: true

  build:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up JDK 21
        uses: actions/setup-java@v4
        with:
          java-version: '21'
          distribution: 'temurin'

      - name: Build Docker Image
        run: |
          ./mvnw spring-boot:build-image \
            -Dspring-boot.build-image.imageName=${{ secrets.DOCKER_REGISTRY }}/app:${{ github.sha }}

      - name: Push to Container Registry
        run: |
          echo ${{ secrets.DOCKER_PASSWORD }} | docker login -u ${{ secrets.DOCKER_USERNAME }} --password-stdin
          docker push ${{ secrets.DOCKER_REGISTRY }}/app:${{ github.sha }}
          docker tag ${{ secrets.DOCKER_REGISTRY }}/app:${{ github.sha }} \
                     ${{ secrets.DOCKER_REGISTRY }}/app:latest
          docker push ${{ secrets.DOCKER_REGISTRY }}/app:latest

  deploy:
    needs: build
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    environment: production
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to Kubernetes
        run: |
          kubectl set image deployment/app app=${{ secrets.DOCKER_REGISTRY }}/app:${{ github.sha }}
          kubectl rollout status deployment/app --timeout=300s

Testing Strategy

java 
// JUnit 5 + Mockito: Modern testing patterns
@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)
class OrderServiceUnitTest {

    @Mock private OrderRepository repository;
    @Mock private PaymentService paymentService;
    @InjectMocks private OrderService orderService;

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Should create order when payment succeeds")
    void shouldCreateOrderWhenPaymentSucceeds() {
        // Given (Arrange)
        CreateOrderRequest request = new CreateOrderRequest("PROD-1", 2);
        when(paymentService.process(any())).thenReturn(PaymentResult.success());
        when(repository.save(any())).thenAnswer(i -> i.getArgument(0));

        // When (Act)
        Order result = orderService.create(request);

        // Then (Assert)
        assertThat(result.getStatus()).isEqualTo(OrderStatus.CONFIRMED);
        verify(repository).save(any(Order.class));
        verify(paymentService).process(any());
    }

    @Test
    @DisplayName("Should throw exception when payment fails")
    void shouldThrowWhenPaymentFails() {
        when(paymentService.process(any()))
            .thenReturn(PaymentResult.failed("Insufficient funds"));

        assertThatThrownBy(() -> orderService.create(new CreateOrderRequest("PROD-1", 1)))
            .isInstanceOf(PaymentException.class)
            .hasMessageContaining("Insufficient funds");
    }
}

// Integration Test with @SpringBootTest
@SpringBootTest
@AutoConfigureMockMvc
@ActiveProfiles("test")
@Testcontainers
class OrderControllerIntegrationTest {

    @Container
    static PostgreSQLContainer<?> postgres = new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:16");

    @DynamicPropertySource
    static void configureProperties(DynamicPropertyRegistry registry) {
        registry.add("spring.datasource.url", postgres::getJdbcUrl);
        registry.add("spring.datasource.username", postgres::getUsername);
