A: Implement AuthorizationManager<MethodInvocation> and return an AuthorizationDecision. Register it via @EnableMethodSecurity(proxyTargetClass = true) and a MethodSecurityInterceptor bean. For targeted use, the simpler path is a custom annotation: annotate your method with @RequiresOwnership, write an @Around AOP advice that resolves the resource owner via the method's argument (using reflection to extract the ID), checks against authentication.getPrincipal(), and throws AccessDeniedException on failure. This avoids SpEL complexity and is fully testable with standard Mockito.
Q: How do you test @PreAuthorize without spinning up a full Spring Security context?
A: Use @WithMockUser(roles = "ADMIN") or the more flexible @WithUserDetails("alice") from spring-security-test in a @SpringBootTest or @WebMvcTest. For pure unit tests without a Spring context, you can manually populate the SecurityContextHolder: SecurityContextHolder.getContext().setAuthentication(new UsernamePasswordAuthenticationToken("alice", null, List.of(new SimpleGrantedAuthority("ROLE_USER")))) — and clear it in @AfterEach. For ABAC tests that call a service bean, mock the service with @MockBean returning true/false to test both the authorized and unauthorized path independently of the authorization business logic.
java
// Spring Security 6 custom AuthorizationManager for ABAC
@Component
public class DocumentAuthorizationManager
        implements AuthorizationManager<MethodInvocation> {

    @Override
    public AuthorizationDecision check(Supplier<Authentication> authSupplier,
                                           MethodInvocation invocation) {
        Authentication auth = authSupplier.get();
        Long documentId = (Long) invocation.getArguments()[0];
        CustomUserPrincipal user = (CustomUserPrincipal) auth.getPrincipal();

        boolean granted = documentRepository
            .existsByIdAndOwnerIdAndStatus(documentId, user.getId(), DRAFT)
            || auth.getAuthorities().stream()
               .anyMatch(a -> a.getAuthority().equals("ROLE_ADMIN"));
        return new AuthorizationDecision(granted);
    }
}
JWKS Caching, Key Rotation & Multi-IdP Configurations
JWKS Caching Creates a Dangerous Window After Key Rotation
Spring's NimbusJwtDecoder caches the JWKS response for 5 minutes by default. After an IdP rotates its signing key, tokens signed with the new key will be rejected by your resource server for up to 5 minutes. During this window, all newly issued tokens are invalid — user logins succeed at the IdP but fail at your API. The production fix: configure NimbusJwtDecoder.withJwkSetUri(uri).jwsAlgorithm(RS256).build() with a custom RestOperations that has a 30-second socket timeout, and catch BadJwtException caused by unknown key IDs — trigger a cache refresh by calling jwkSetUri inline and retry verification once.
The issuer-uri Auto-Discovery Can Fail at Startup
When you configure spring.security.oauth2.resourceserver.jwt.issuer-uri, Spring fetches <uri>/.well-known/openid-configuration at startup to discover the JWKS endpoint. If the IdP is temporarily unavailable during a pod restart (common in Kubernetes where services start before their dependencies are ready), your application fails to start. Fix: add a startup retry with spring.security.oauth2.resourceserver.jwt.jwk-set-uri directly (bypasses discovery, more resilient) or configure a readiness probe that also checks IdP connectivity.
Audience Validation Is Disabled by Default — Enable It
A token issued by Keycloak for service-A is cryptographically valid for service-B if they share the same JWKS endpoint. Without audience (aud) claim validation, service-B accepts tokens intended for service-A — this is a token confusion attack. Spring's resource server does not validate the aud claim by default. Enable it: http.oauth2ResourceServer(o -> o.jwt(j -> j.jwtAuthenticationConverter(converter).jwkSetUri(uri))) then add a custom JwtDecoder that includes .withClaimValidator(JwtValidators.createDefaultWithIssuer(issuer)) plus an AudienceValidator.
Q: How do you configure a Spring resource server to accept tokens from multiple IdPs (e.g., Keycloak for employees, Auth0 for customers)?
A: Implement a custom JwtDecoder that inspects the token's iss (issuer) claim without verification, selects the appropriate NimbusJwtDecoder for that issuer from a Map<String, JwtDecoder>, and delegates full verification to it. Register this as a @Bean JwtDecoder and wire it into .oauth2ResourceServer(o -> o.jwt(j -> j.decoder(multiIssuerDecoder()))). This pattern is called a "delegating JWT decoder" and is the standard approach for multi-tenant SaaS APIs accepting external identity providers.
Q: Your Keycloak instance goes down. What happens to your resource server and how do you mitigate it?
A: The resource server continues to validate tokens using its cached JWKS keys — it does not contact Keycloak on every request. So existing tokens continue to be validated successfully until the JWKS cache expires (5 minutes default). After cache expiry, if Keycloak is still down, key refresh fails and new token validations start rejecting. Mitigation: (1) set a longer JWKS cache TTL (e.g. 60 minutes) to cover typical IdP outage windows, (2) implement a Resilience4j circuit breaker around the JWKS refresh HTTP call that falls back to the last successfully cached key set, (3) use a redundant Keycloak cluster behind a load balancer.
java
// Multi-IdP delegating JwtDecoder + audience validation
@Bean
public JwtDecoder multiIssuerDecoder() {
    Map<String, JwtDecoder> decoders = Map.of(
        "https://keycloak.mycompany.com/realms/employees",
            buildDecoder("https://keycloak.mycompany.com/realms/employees", "employees-app"),
        "https://mycompany.auth0.com/",
            buildDecoder("https://mycompany.auth0.com/", "https://api.mycompany.com")
    );
    return token -> {
        var unsafeClaims = JWTParser.parse(token).getJWTClaimsSet();
        String issuer = unsafeClaims.getIssuer();
        JwtDecoder decoder = decoders.get(issuer);
        if (decoder == null) throw new BadJwtException("Unknown issuer: " + issuer);
        return decoder.decode(token);  // full cryptographic verification
    };
}

private NimbusJwtDecoder buildDecoder(String issuerUri, String expectedAudience) {
    NimbusJwtDecoder decoder = NimbusJwtDecoder
        .withIssuerLocation(issuerUri).build();
    OAuth2TokenValidator<Jwt> validators = new DelegatingOAuth2TokenValidator<>(
        JwtValidators.createDefaultWithIssuer(issuerUri),
        jwt -> jwt.getAudience().contains(expectedAudience)
            ? OAuth2TokenValidatorResult.success()
            : OAuth2TokenValidatorResult.failure(new OAuth2Error("invalid_audience"))
    );
    decoder.setJwtValidator(validators);
    return decoder;
}
Authorization Code Flow Internals & PKCE Attack Prevention
The state Parameter Is Your Defense Against CSRF on the Redirect
Before redirecting to the IdP, Spring Security generates a random state value, stores it in the HTTP session (or a signed cookie for stateless setups), and includes it in the authorization URL. When the IdP redirects back to /login/oauth2/code/{registrationId}?code=...&state=..., Spring verifies the state matches the stored value before exchanging the code for tokens. If an attacker crafts a forged callback URL and tricks a victim into visiting it, the state check fails — the authorization code is never exchanged. Without this check, an attacker could pre-generate a code for their own account and trick a victim into associating it with their session (account takeover via OAuth CSRF).
Token Storage in Server-Side OAuth2 Client Creates Session Affinity
When using oauth2Login(), Spring stores the OAuth2AuthorizedClient (containing access + refresh tokens) in the HTTP session via HttpSessionOAuth2AuthorizedClientRepository. In a multi-instance deployment without sticky sessions, a request hitting a different instance than the one that performed the login won't find the stored client — OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager.authorize() returns null and downstream API calls fail. Fix: replace with JdbcOAuth2AuthorizedClientService (database-backed) or RedisOAuth2AuthorizedClientRepository (shared session store) so any instance can access the stored tokens.
PKCE code_verifier Must Be Cryptographically Random and 43–128 Characters
RFC 7636 specifies the code_verifier must be a cryptographically random string of 43–128 characters from the set [A-Z a-z 0-9 - . _ ~]. Using a predictable verifier (e.g., a UUID v4, which only has 122 bits of entropy but is structured and guessable if the PRNG is weak) reduces security. Spring Security generates compliant verifiers using SecureRandom automatically — you don't implement this yourself. For SPAs implementing PKCE in JavaScript, always use crypto.getRandomValues(), never Math.random(), which is not cryptographically secure.
Q: What is the difference between oauth2Login() and oauth2Client() in Spring Security, and when do you use each?
A: oauth2Login() handles the complete user authentication flow — it redirects unauthenticated users to the IdP, handles the callback, creates a Spring Authentication (as OAuth2AuthenticationToken), and establishes the user session. Use it when your application is the user-facing frontend and needs to authenticate users. oauth2Client() handles token management for a service that already has an authenticated user and needs to call downstream APIs on their behalf — it stores, refreshes, and provides access tokens via OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager. In practice, most web apps use both: oauth2Login() for authentication and oauth2Client() for downstream API propagation.
Q: Your user's access token expires mid-session while they're using the app. How does Spring handle this transparently?
A: If configured with OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager using DefaultOAuth2AuthorizedClientManager (servlet) or AuthorizedClientServiceReactiveOAuth2AuthorizedClientManager (reactive), Spring automatically detects expired access tokens when authorize() is called and uses the stored refresh token to obtain a new access token — updating the stored OAuth2AuthorizedClient. This happens transparently without user interaction, provided: (a) a refresh token was issued (requires offline_access scope with some IdPs), (b) the refresh token hasn't expired, and (c) the AuthorizedClientRepository is shared across instances (see session affinity note above).
java
// Stateless OAuth2 authorized client repository (Redis-backed, no sticky sessions)
@Bean
public OAuth2AuthorizedClientRepository authorizedClientRepository(
        OAuth2AuthorizedClientService clientService) {
    return new AuthenticatedPrincipalOAuth2AuthorizedClientRepository(clientService);
}

@Bean
public OAuth2AuthorizedClientService authorizedClientService(
        ClientRegistrationRepository registrations,
        JdbcOperations jdbc) {
    // JdbcOAuth2AuthorizedClientService stores tokens in DB — shared across all instances
    return new JdbcOAuth2AuthorizedClientService(jdbc, registrations);
}

// OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager with auto-refresh
@Bean
public OAuth2AuthorizedClientManager authorizedClientManager(
        ClientRegistrationRepository clients,
        OAuth2AuthorizedClientRepository repo) {
    OAuth2AuthorizedClientProvider provider =
        OAuth2AuthorizedClientProviderBuilder.builder()
            .authorizationCode()
            .refreshToken()     // auto-refresh expired access tokens
            .build();
    DefaultOAuth2AuthorizedClientManager manager =
        new DefaultOAuth2AuthorizedClientManager(clients, repo);
    manager.setAuthorizedClientProvider(provider);
    return manager;
}
CORS Preflight Failures & Spring Security 6 CSRF Deferred Token Model
CORS and Spring Security Must Both Be Configured — One Without the Other Breaks
If you configure CORS only in WebMvcConfigurer.addCorsMappings() and forget http.cors() in your SecurityFilterChain, preflight OPTIONS requests will be blocked by Spring Security before they reach the MVC layer — the CORS headers are never added and the browser blocks the actual request. Conversely, configuring http.cors() without a CorsConfigurationSource bean results in a permissive default that may allow unintended origins. Always configure both and test preflight with curl -X OPTIONS http://localhost:8080/api/data -H "Origin: https://app.mycompany.com" -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: POST" -v — the response must include Access-Control-Allow-Origin.
Spring Security 6 Changed CSRF Token Handling to Deferred Loading
In Spring Security 5, the CSRF token was loaded eagerly on every request and always written to the session/cookie. In Spring Security 6, loading is deferred — the token is only loaded if something actually reads it during request processing. This means GET requests that don't explicitly access the CSRF token attribute will not trigger the cookie to be set in the response. SPAs that rely on reading the XSRF-TOKEN cookie from a GET request break silently on Spring Security 6 upgrades. Fix: write an explicit filter that calls ((CsrfToken) request.getAttribute(CsrfToken.class.getName())).getToken() on the designated CSRF cookie-loading endpoint.
allowedOrigins("*") with allowCredentials(true) Is a Configuration Error
Setting allowedOrigins("*") (wildcard) combined with allowCredentials(true) is explicitly rejected by the CORS specification — browsers won't honor it, and Spring will throw an IllegalArgumentException at startup in Spring 5.3+. The wildcard tells browsers to allow any origin; allowCredentials tells browsers to send cookies/auth headers — these two cannot coexist because allowing credentials to any origin would let any website silently make authenticated requests on your behalf. Always enumerate explicit allowed origins in production. Use allowedOriginPatterns("https://*.mycompany.com") if you need subdomain wildcards.
Q: Should CSRF protection be disabled for a fully stateless JWT API?
A: Yes, and this is correct and safe. CSRF attacks exploit the browser's automatic credential-sending behavior — specifically cookies and HTTP Basic auth. A stateless JWT API that requires clients to set a custom Authorization: Bearer <token> header is not vulnerable to CSRF, because browsers do not automatically add custom headers to cross-origin requests (they only auto-send cookies and default headers). The cross-origin request must go through a CORS preflight, which your server controls. Therefore, calling csrf.disable() on a stateless JWT API is correct — but only if you truly never use cookies for authentication. If your JWT is in an HttpOnly cookie, CSRF defense must remain enabled.
Q: How do you test that your CORS configuration correctly allows your SPA origin and blocks others?
A: Write two MockMvc tests using Spring's CORS test support: mockMvc.perform(options("/api/data").header("Origin", "https://app.mycompany.com").header("Access-Control-Request-Method", "POST")).andExpect(header().string("Access-Control-Allow-Origin", "https://app.mycompany.com")) — should return 200. Then the same request with Origin: https://evil.com — should return 403 or a response without the Access-Control-Allow-Origin header, causing browsers to block it. Also test that OPTIONS preflight is permitted without authentication (Spring Security must not require a JWT for preflight).
java
// Spring Security 6: force CSRF cookie to be written on GET /api/csrf
@Component
public class CsrfCookieFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter {

    @Override
    protected void doFilterInternal(HttpServletRequest request,
                                        HttpServletResponse response,
                                        FilterChain chain)
            throws ServletException, IOException {
        // Force deferred CSRF token to be rendered to the XSRF-TOKEN cookie
        CsrfToken csrfToken = (CsrfToken) request.getAttribute(CsrfToken.class.getName());
        if (csrfToken != null) {
            csrfToken.getToken();  // triggers deferred load and commits cookie to response
        }
        chain.doFilter(request, response);
    }
}
Cache Failure Modes & Distributed Lock Patterns
@Cacheable on a @Transactional Method Has a Silent Bug
If you annotate a method with both @Cacheable and @Transactional, the cache population happens after the method returns but before the transaction commits. In high-concurrency scenarios, a second thread can read the newly cached value before the transaction has committed — reading uncommitted data from the cache. In practice, this is rare because transactions are short. But it's a real bug in financial contexts: a balance update is cached before the DB commit confirms, and a concurrent read sees the new balance while the DB rollback is still possible. Fix: separate the cached read from the transactional write into different methods, never combining both annotations on a method that does writes.
Cache Key Collision Across Different Methods Is a Silent Data Corruption Bug
Spring's default cache key is computed from the method arguments. If two @Cacheable methods in different services use the same cache name and both take a Long id parameter, they share the same key space — findProductById(42L) and findOrderById(42L) in the same "entities" cache will collide and overwrite each other. Always use distinct cache names per entity type ("products", "orders") and consider prefixing keys when multiple methods share a cache: key = "'product:' + #id".
Redis Serialization Mismatches After Class Refactoring Break Deserialization
When using GenericJackson2JsonRedisSerializer, the class name is embedded in the serialized JSON: {"@class":"com.myapp.ProductDto","id":42,...}. If you rename, move, or delete the ProductDto class, all existing cache entries fail to deserialize and throw SerializationException on every cache hit — effectively breaking your service until the cache is flushed. Fix: use a custom ObjectMapper without default typing (GenericJackson2JsonRedisSerializer(mapper) with mapper.deactivateDefaultTyping()), serialize to/from DTOs explicitly, and version your cache names (e.g. "products:v2") whenever the DTO schema changes.
Q: How do you implement probabilistic early expiration (PER) to prevent cache stampedes without distributed locks?
A: PER is an algorithm where each cache read probabilistically decides to refresh early, before the TTL actually expires. The formula: currentTime - (ttlRemaining * beta * log(random())) — if this value exceeds the cache entry's creation time, proactively refresh. With beta=1 and a 30-minute TTL, refreshes start happening randomly in the last few minutes. This spreads the DB load across multiple threads as expiry approaches, preventing the hard cutoff spike. Implement by storing the creation timestamp alongside the cached value and running the PER check on every @Cacheable hit — return the cached value and trigger an async background refresh if PER triggers.
Q: @CacheEvict(allEntries=true) on a high-traffic cache — what's the risk?
A: allEntries=true atomically evicts every entry in the named cache. If that cache holds 50,000 entries serving 10,000 RPS, a single write operation (e.g., a config update) causes an instant "cold cache" state where all subsequent requests simultaneously miss and hammer the database. This is a self-inflicted Cache Avalanche. Fix: use targeted eviction (key="#entity.id") whenever possible. When wholesale eviction is genuinely needed (e.g., permission table changed), combine it with a background job that pre-warms the top N most-requested keys immediately after eviction, before live traffic hits the cold cache.
java
// Bloom filter for cache penetration prevention (Redisson)
@Component
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class ProductLookupService {

    private final RedissonClient redisson;
    private final ProductRepository repository;

    private RBloomFilter<Long> bloomFilter;

    @PostConstruct
    public void initBloomFilter() {
        bloomFilter = redisson.getBloomFilter("product-ids");
        bloomFilter.tryInit(10_000_000L, 0.01);  // 10M entries, 1% false-positive rate
        repository.findAllIds().forEach(bloomFilter::add);
    }

    @Cacheable(value = "products", key = "#id")
    public ProductDto findById(Long id) {
        // Reject IDs that definitely don't exist — no DB hit
        if (!bloomFilter.contains(id)) {
            return null;
        }
        return repository.findById(id).map(mapper::toDto).orElse(null);
    }
}
Thread Pool Sizing, @Async Proxy Pitfalls & ShedLock Under Node Failure
The Default @Scheduled Executor Is a Single Thread — Silent Task Pileup
Without configuration, Spring uses a single-threaded ThreadPoolTaskScheduler for all @Scheduled methods. If you have three scheduled tasks and task A takes 60 seconds while tasks B and C are set to run every 30 seconds, tasks B and C are delayed for the duration of task A — they execute back-to-back immediately after A finishes, creating execution bursts. In production, this manifests as intermittent log spikes and missed SLA windows on time-sensitive jobs. Always configure a multi-threaded ThreadPoolTaskScheduler and set setPoolSize(N) where N >= number of concurrent scheduled jobs you expect to run simultaneously.
@Async Exceptions Are Silently Swallowed Without an AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler
When an @Async method with return type void throws an exception, it is silently swallowed by default — no log, no error, no stack trace. This is because the exception occurs on a background thread after the calling thread has already returned. For CompletableFuture-returning methods, the exception is captured inside the future and only surfaces if the caller calls .get() or .join(). Fix: implement AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler and register it via AsyncConfigurer.getAsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler() — this ensures all unhandled async exceptions are logged and alerted.
ShedLock Does Not Guarantee Exactly-Once — It Guarantees At-Most-Once Per Lock Window
ShedLock prevents concurrent execution across nodes during the lock's active window. But if a node crashes after acquiring the lock and the task partially executed, lockAtMostFor releases the lock, and the next scheduled trigger re-runs the task — potentially re-processing the same data. ShedLock provides at-most-once execution per tick, not exactly-once. For jobs that must not re-process (e.g., nightly billing runs), your task itself must be idempotent: track processed items by a unique run ID, skip already-processed records, and use database-level upserts or insert-or-ignore semantics.
Q: How do you size the ThreadPoolTaskExecutor for @Async tasks in an I/O-bound microservice?
A: For I/O-bound tasks (HTTP calls, DB queries, email sending), the optimal thread count is much higher than CPU count — typically CPU cores * (1 + wait_time / compute_time). If each async email send takes 200ms and CPU processing takes 2ms, the ratio is 100, so on a 4-core machine: 4 * (1 + 100) = ~400 optimal threads. In practice, cap at 50–100 for typical services and monitor queue depth (ThreadPoolTaskExecutor.getActiveCount() and getQueueSize() via Actuator). Set CallerRunsPolicy as the rejection handler — it backpressures the caller thread when the pool is full rather than dropping work silently.
Q: ShedLock is using JDBC as a lock provider. What happens during a database failover?
A: If the database is unavailable when ShedLock tries to acquire the lock (e.g., during a primary failover in a Postgres HA setup), the lock acquisition throws a DataAccessException. By default, ShedLock catches this and skips the job execution — treating a DB failure as "lock not acquired." This is safe (prevents duplicate runs) but means your scheduled jobs silently don't run during DB outages. For critical jobs (report generation, batch processing), add a @Scheduled fallback that tracks missed executions in a separate recovery table and re-runs them once DB connectivity is restored.
Q: How do you test that an @Async method actually runs asynchronously (on a different thread) in an integration test?
A: Use a CountDownLatch: configure the async method to call latch.countDown() after executing. In your test, call the service method, then call latch.await(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS) — this blocks the test thread until the async method completes. Verify the thread name used by the async method by injecting a AtomicReference<String> and setting it to Thread.currentThread().getName() inside the async method. Assert the stored name starts with your configured threadNamePrefix (e.g., async-), proving it ran on a pool thread, not the caller's thread.
java
// AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler — prevent silent @Async failures
@Configuration
@EnableAsync
public class AsyncConfig implements AsyncConfigurer {

    @Override
    public AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler getAsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler() {
        return (ex, method, params) -> {
            log.error("@Async method {} threw uncaught exception: {}",
                      method.getName(), ex.getMessage(), ex);
            alertingService.sendAlert("AsyncTaskFailure", method.getName(), ex);
        };
    }
}

// Integration test: verify @Async runs on a named pool thread
@SpringBootTest
class EmailServiceAsyncTest {

    @Autowired private EmailNotificationService emailService;

    @Test
    void shouldRunOnAsyncThread() throws InterruptedException {
        CountDownLatch latch = new CountDownLatch(1);
        AtomicReference<String> threadName = new AtomicReference<>();

        emailService.sendWelcomeEmailWithCapture("test@example.com", threadName, latch);

        boolean completed = latch.await(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS);
        assertThat(completed).isTrue();
        assertThat(threadName.get()).startsWith("async-");
    }
}
DLX Retry Storms & Poison Pill Architecture
A Naive DLX Without TTL Staggering Creates a Retry Storm
If your dead-letter routing sends a failed message straight back to the same queue with no delay, and the consumer fails again instantly, you get a tight infinite loop that pins a CPU core and floods your logs — effectively a self-inflicted denial of service. Production DLX setups always route through an intermediate "parking lot" queue with a TTL (e.g. 30s, then 5min, then 30min) so retries back off exponentially, and cap the retry count using a header counter (x-death array) read by the consumer before deciding to give up permanently.
Manual Ack Mode Without basicNack Discipline Causes Silent Message Loss
When a @RabbitListener throws an unhandled exception under default Spring AMQP error handling, the message is requeued and redelivered indefinitely by default — but if you catch the exception and forget to call either basicAck or basicNack, the message sits unacknowledged until the channel closes, then gets redelivered to a different consumer with the broker unaware it was "in flight." Always wrap listener bodies in try/catch with an explicit ack decision on every code path.
Queue Length Limits Interact Badly With Unbounded DLQs
Production RabbitMQ deployments set x-max-length on primary queues to prevent unbounded memory growth during an outage, but teams frequently forget to set the same limit on the DLQ. A poison-pill bug that dead-letters thousands of messages per minute can silently exhaust disk space on the DLQ node hours before anyone notices the primary queue looks healthy.
Q: Your team sees the same order processed twice downstream of a RabbitMQ consumer. Walk through the most likely root cause.
A: The consumer almost certainly processed the message, then crashed or lost its connection before calling basicAck. RabbitMQ has no way to know the work was done — its contract is "redeliver any unacknowledged message to someone else." This is at-least-once delivery by design, not a bug in RabbitMQ. The fix is making the consumer's business logic idempotent (e.g., a dedupe table keyed on a message ID), not trying to make RabbitMQ deliver exactly once, which it fundamentally does not guarantee.
Q: How do you prevent a single malformed message from blocking an entire queue's processing?
A: Detect deserialization or validation failures explicitly and call channel.basicNack(tag, false, false) (requeue=false) so the broker routes the message to the configured Dead Letter Exchange instead of redelivering it to the head of the same queue. Without this, a single bad message that always throws gets redelivered, fails again, and blocks every message behind it in that consumer's prefetch window.
Q: What's the trade-off of setting prefetch count too high on a RabbitMQ consumer?
A: A high prefetch (basic.qos) lets the broker push many unacknowledged messages to one consumer for throughput, but if that consumer crashes, all of those in-flight messages get redelivered elsewhere at once — a thundering herd. It also means one slow consumer can hoard messages that a faster, idle consumer could have processed. Most production systems tune prefetch to a small multiple of the consumer's concurrency, not an arbitrarily large number.
Q: A topic exchange binding of "orders.*" isn't matching "orders.payment.failed". Why?
A: * matches exactly one word between dots, not zero-or-more. "orders.payment.failed" has two words after "orders", so it needs "orders.*.*" or, more robustly, "orders.#" where # matches zero or more words. This is one of the most common AMQP routing-key bugs in code review.
Q: How would you test that your DLX routing actually works before relying on it in production?
A: Write an integration test using Testcontainers' RabbitMQ module: publish a message your listener is guaranteed to reject (e.g., unparseable JSON), then assert via the management HTTP API or a second test consumer bound to the DLQ that exactly one message lands there within a timeout. Also assert the original queue's message count returns to zero, proving the bad message didn't get stuck retrying forever.
Offset Management, Rebalancing & Exactly-Once Cost
Auto-Commit Is a Silent Data-Loss Trap Under Load
With enable.auto.commit=true, offsets commit on a fixed interval (default 5s) regardless of whether your business logic actually finished processing the records in that poll batch. If your consumer crashes mid-batch after the timer already committed, those records are gone — they'll never be redelivered, because Kafka now believes they were consumed. Production systems disable auto-commit and commit manually only after the side effect (DB write, downstream publish) has durably succeeded.
Partition Rebalancing Pauses the Entire Consumer Group
The default eager rebalancing protocol revokes every partition assignment across the whole consumer group before reassigning any of them — even consumers unaffected by the membership change stop processing during the rebalance window. Cooperative sticky assignment (Kafka 2.4+, CooperativeStickyAssignor) only reassigns the specific partitions that need to move, dramatically shrinking the throughput dip during scale-up/scale-down events.
Exactly-Once Semantics Has Real Latency and Complexity Cost
Kafka's idempotent producer (enable.idempotence=true) eliminates duplicate writes caused by producer retries, and transactions (read_committed isolation) extend that across a read-process-write cycle. But transactional writes require coordinator round-trips per transaction and reduce achievable throughput compared to fire-and-forget at-least-once publishing — teams should measure whether their actual correctness requirement is "exactly-once end-to-end" (rare, expensive) or "at-least-once plus idempotent consumer" (usually sufficient and far cheaper).
Q: Your consumer group's lag spikes every time you deploy a new pod. What's happening and how do you reduce it?
A: Each pod restart triggers a rebalance, and with the default eager assignor, the entire group's consumption pauses while every partition is revoked and reassigned. Switching to CooperativeStickyAssignor limits the disruption to only the partitions actually changing hands. Additionally, tuning session.timeout.ms and using a graceful shutdown hook that calls consumer.close() (which proactively leaves the group) avoids waiting for the full session timeout before the coordinator notices the consumer is gone.
Q: Explain the difference between at-least-once, at-most-once, and exactly-once in terms of where the ack happens relative to processing.
A: At-most-once commits the offset before processing — if processing fails, the record is lost and never retried. At-least-once processes first, then commits — if a crash happens between processing and commit, the record is reprocessed, causing duplicates. Exactly-once requires the offset commit and the side effect to be atomic with each other, typically via Kafka transactions writing both the output record and the consumer offset in one transaction, or via an idempotent sink that makes duplicates harmless.
Q: A teammate proposes increasing partition count from 6 to 60 "for more parallelism." What do you push back on?
A: More partitions increase parallelism ceiling but also increase open file handles and memory on brokers, increase end-to-end latency for `min.insync.replicas` acknowledgment, and — critically — partition count can only be increased, never decreased, on an existing topic without recreating it, and increasing it breaks key-based ordering guarantees for any key whose partition assignment changes (since partition = hash(key) % partitionCount). Size for projected peak throughput per consumer, not an arbitrary large number.
Q: How do you achieve exactly-once delivery from a Kafka consumer into a Postgres database?
A: You can't get true exactly-once across two different systems without making the sink idempotent — Kafka transactions only guarantee exactly-once within Kafka (offset + output topic). The standard pattern is at-least-once delivery into Postgres combined with an idempotent write: either an upsert keyed on a unique event ID, or storing the last-processed offset per partition in the same transaction as the business write, so a reprocessed record is a no-op.
java
// Cooperative sticky assignor + manual offset commit configuration
@Bean
public ConsumerFactory<String, PaymentEvent> consumerFactory() {
    Map<String, Object> props = new HashMap<>();
    props.put(ConsumerConfig.PARTITION_ASSIGNMENT_STRATEGY_CONFIG,
              CooperativeStickyAssignor.class.getName());
    props.put(ConsumerConfig.ENABLE_AUTO_COMMIT_CONFIG, false);
    props.put(ConsumerConfig.ISOLATION_LEVEL_CONFIG, "read_committed");
    return new DefaultKafkaConsumerFactory<>(props);
}

// Exactly-once read-process-write using Kafka transactions
@Transactional("kafkaTransactionManager")
@KafkaListener(topics = "raw-events")
public void transform(ConsumerRecord<String, RawEvent> record) {
    EnrichedEvent enriched = enrichmentService.enrich(record.value());
    kafkaTemplate.send("enriched-events", record.key(), enriched);
    // offset commit for "raw-events" is folded into THIS transaction automatically
}
Zero-Downtime Schema Migration Strategy
Auto-Registration in Production Is a Production Incident Waiting to Happen
Leaving auto.register.schemas=true on production producers means any developer's typo or unreviewed field rename becomes a live schema version the moment a service deploys, with no review gate. Mature setups disable this in prod, validate schema changes via a CI step that calls the registry's /compatibility endpoint against the PR's .avsc file, and register approved versions explicitly as part of the deployment pipeline.
Renaming a Field Is Indistinguishable From Delete + Add
Avro has no native "rename" operation — a field rename is, to the compatibility checker, simultaneously removing one field and adding another. Under BACKWARD compatibility this is only safe if the new field has a default and the old field also had one (so its removal is non-breaking). Teams that actually need renames typically use Avro's aliases array, which lets a field declare prior names it should also match against in older payloads.
Consumer-First Deployment Order Is Non-Negotiable Under BACKWARD Compatibility
If you deploy a producer emitting a new field before any consumer can tolerate it, nothing breaks immediately — but if you ever need to downgrade the producer or roll a consumer back to a version older than the field's introduction, that consumer is reading data shaped to a contract it never agreed to. The safe sequencing is always: register schema → deploy consumers tolerant of the new shape → only then deploy the producer that starts emitting it.
Q: A consumer throws AvroTypeException after a routine producer deploy. What's your triage process?
A: First check the Schema Registry's version history for the topic's subject to see exactly what changed and when. Compare the failing record's schema ID (embedded in the first 5 bytes of the Avro payload, before the binary data) against the consumer's compiled schema version. The most common cause is a field removed without a default, or a type change (e.g., int to long) that the registry's compatibility check should have blocked — meaning either the check was bypassed, or compatibility mode was set looser than the team assumed.
Q: Why does Confluent's serialized Avro payload not include the full schema on every message?
A: Each message is prefixed with a 1-byte magic number plus a 4-byte schema ID resolved from the Schema Registry, not the schema itself. The deserializer fetches (and caches) the full schema from the registry by that ID once, then reuses it for every subsequent message referencing the same ID. This keeps per-message overhead at 5 bytes instead of kilobytes of repeated schema text, which matters enormously at Kafka's typical message volumes.
Q: What compatibility mode would you choose for a topic consumed by a third-party partner you don't control the deploy schedule of?
A: FULL compatibility, since you can't coordinate a consumer-first or producer-first rollout with an external party — you need every schema change to be safely readable by both old and new consumers and producers simultaneously, with no assumed deployment ordering at all. This is more restrictive (fewer kinds of changes are allowed) but it's the only mode that doesn't require trust in the partner's deploy timing.
Q: How do you test schema evolution before it reaches production?
A: Run a Schema Registry container (Testcontainers has a Confluent module) in CI, register the current production schema version, then submit the proposed new .avsc via the compatibility check endpoint as part of the build. Fail the build on any is_compatible: false response. Pair this with a contract test that serializes a record with the old schema and deserializes it with the new one (and vice versa) to catch subtleties the registry's static check might miss.
Outbox Transaction Boundaries & Poller Race Conditions
The Outbox Table's Growth Rate Is Often Underestimated
Teams frequently forget the outbox table needs its own retention/archival job — every domain write adds a row, and without a cleanup process removing rows marked SENT after some retention window, the table grows unbounded and eventually slows down the poller's SELECT ... WHERE status='PENDING' query as it scans past millions of historical SENT rows, unless that query is properly indexed on status.
Polling Introduces Latency the Domain Write Doesn't Have
A 500ms poll interval means every event has up to 500ms of added latency before it even reaches the broker, on top of normal publish latency — fine for most business events, unsafe for anything latency-sensitive. Debezium-based CDC removes this lag by tailing the database's write-ahead log directly and streaming changes to Kafka Connect within milliseconds, at the cost of operating a CDC connector instead of a simple scheduled job.
Multiple Poller Instances Can Double-Publish Without Row Locking
Running more than one application instance with the same @Scheduled poller means both instances can select the same PENDING rows in the same polling tick and both publish them. The standard fix is a SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED query (Postgres) so each poller instance only grabs rows no other instance currently holds a lock on, rather than relying on a single-instance assumption that breaks the moment you scale horizontally.
Q: Why can't you just publish to Kafka directly inside the same @Transactional method as the database write, instead of using an outbox table?
A: A Kafka publish and a JDBC transaction commit are two independent network operations with no shared atomicity primitive — there's no way to make "commit the DB row" and "the broker durably has this message" succeed or fail together. If the DB commits but the Kafka call times out (or vice versa), you've silently lost an event or fabricated one that never happened. The outbox table converts the second write into a row in the *same* database, which the local transaction *can* make atomic with the domain write.
Q: What's the advantage of Debezium CDC over a polling-based outbox publisher?
A: CDC tails the database's transaction log directly, so it sees every committed outbox row essentially in real time without running repeated SELECT queries against the table, eliminating both the polling latency and the query load on the production database. It also naturally handles ordering correctly since it processes the log in commit order, whereas a polling query needs careful ORDER BY and locking to avoid the same guarantee breaking under concurrent inserts.
Q: How do you guarantee the outbox poller doesn't publish the same row twice when scaled to 3 instances?
A: Use SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED when selecting the PENDING batch — each instance's transaction locks the rows it selects, and other instances' identical queries skip over those locked rows entirely rather than blocking or re-selecting them. Combine this with marking rows SENT inside the same transaction that performed the publish-and-lock, so a crash mid-batch leaves the row PENDING and unlocked for the next poll, rather than stuck.
Q: A downstream consumer's processed_events table is growing without bound just to support idempotency checks. How would a senior engineer address this?
A: Recognize that idempotency dedupe keys only need to be remembered for as long as duplicate delivery is plausible — typically the maximum retry/redelivery window of your pipeline, not forever. Add a retention job (or a TTL if using Redis instead of a relational table) that purges dedupe keys older than that window, since a duplicate arriving a year later isn't a realistic failure mode for most systems and isn't worth indefinite storage cost.
Compensation Failure & Saga Rollback Complexity
Compensating Transactions Are Not True Rollbacks
A compensating transaction undoes the business effect of a step, not necessarily its database state precisely. Refunding a charged card doesn't erase the original charge — it issues a new, separate refund transaction. Any other system observing both transactions (an accounting export, a customer's bank statement) sees two events, not zero. Saga design must account for this visible "scar tissue," which is part of why sagas are unsuitable for domains demanding true all-or-nothing invisibility.
Compensations Can Themselves Fail, and There Is No Compensation for a Compensation
If a refund call times out during the compensating step, retrying it blindly risks a double refund if the original call actually succeeded server-side before the timeout. Production sagas need idempotent compensating actions (refund requests carrying an idempotency key) and a terminal "stuck" state with full saga context persisted for human intervention when automated compensation can't be confirmed to have completed.
Choreography Sagas Lose Global Visibility as Participant Count Grows
With 3 services reacting to each other's events, choreography is simple to reason about. With 8+ services, there's no single place to look to answer "where is saga #4521 right now" — you have to reconstruct the path by correlating event logs across every service. This debuggability cliff is the main reason orchestration (with an explicit state machine) tends to win as systems grow, even though it introduces a more centralized component.
Q: Design review: a teammate's saga has the Inventory service publish events that the Order service listens for, and vice versa, with no orchestrator. What's your main concern?
A: A pure choreography design with bidirectional event listening between two services creates implicit coupling that's hard to trace — and risks event cycles if error handling isn't carefully scoped (Service A's failure event triggers Service B's compensation, which itself emits an event Service A reacts to, etc.). I'd push for either a clear "one direction owns the saga" convention, or recommend orchestration once more than 2 participants are involved, since debugging a multi-hop event chain in production incident response is significantly harder than reading a state machine's current state.
Q: How do you make a compensating "refund payment" call safe to retry?
A: Generate a unique idempotency key per compensation attempt tied to the saga instance and step (e.g., sagaId + "-refund"), and pass it to the payment gateway's idempotent refund endpoint. The gateway then guarantees that retried calls with the same key return the original result rather than issuing a second refund, which is essential since network timeouts make "did it actually fail, or did the response just not arrive" fundamentally ambiguous from the caller's side.
Q: When would you choose Axon Framework over hand-rolling an orchestrator with Spring State Machine?
A: Axon provides built-in event sourcing, a saga lifecycle framework with automatic event correlation, and command/event bus infrastructure out of the box — appropriate when you're already committing to CQRS/event sourcing as an architecture and want the saga and aggregate machinery to share infrastructure. Spring State Machine is lighter weight and a better fit when you just need an explicit state machine for orchestration logic without adopting full event sourcing across your domain model.
Q: What's a concrete way to test that your saga's compensation logic actually works, beyond the happy path?
A: Build integration tests that deliberately fail each participant step in turn (using mocked downstream services or WireMock fault injection) and assert the saga reaches the correct terminal compensated state with the correct compensating calls issued in the correct order. Critically, also test what happens when the compensating call itself fails or times out, asserting the saga lands in a manual-intervention state rather than retrying forever or silently marking itself complete.
java
// Idempotent compensating action with terminal manual-intervention fallback
private Action<SagaState, SagaEvent> refundPaymentAction() {
    return ctx -> {
        UUID sagaId = ctx.getExtendedState().get("sagaId", UUID.class);
        try {
            paymentClient.refund(sagaId, idempotencyKey(sagaId, "refund"));
        } catch (PaymentGatewayException ex) {
            sagaRepository.markStuck(sagaId, "REFUND_FAILED", ex.getMessage());
            alerting.pageOnCall("Saga " + sagaId + " requires manual refund review");
            throw new SagaCompensationFailedException(sagaId, ex);
        }
    };
}
Snapshot Strategy & Projection Replay Cost
Read Models Are Eventually Consistent, Not Eventually Optional
Because projections are built asynchronously from the event stream, a client that writes a command and immediately queries the read model can see stale data — the projection simply hasn't caught up yet. This is a real UX problem, not a theoretical one; production systems solve it either by returning the write-side result optimistically to the caller, or by having the client poll/subscribe for the projection to catch up to a known event version before considering the operation "visible."
Snapshot Cadence Is a Direct Trade Between Storage and Replay Latency
Snapshotting every event minimizes replay time to near-zero but multiplies storage and write load. Snapshotting only every 10,000 events keeps storage lean but means a cold load of a long-lived, high-activity aggregate (e.g., a years-old customer account) might still replay thousands of events. Most production systems pick a cadence (commonly every 50–500 events) based on measured p99 load latency for their busiest aggregates, then revisit it as event volume grows.
Schema Changes to Event Payloads Are Permanent Commitments
Unlike a normal database column you can migrate, an event already written to the store is immutable forever — you cannot retroactively "fix" an old event's shape. Event-sourced systems handle this with upcasters/event versioning (a transformation layer that converts old event shapes to the current shape during replay) rather than ever mutating historical events, since mutating the source of truth defeats the entire purpose of event sourcing.
Q: A read model shows stale data for about 200ms after a write under normal load, but customers are complaining about a multi-second delay during peak traffic. How do you investigate?
A: Check whether the projection consumer group is falling behind — measure Kafka consumer lag on the projection's topic during peak load specifically. The most common cause is the projection's database writes (often single-row upserts) becoming the bottleneck under peak event volume; the fix is usually batching projection writes or scaling the projector's consumer instances, not touching the write side at all, since CQRS specifically isolates this kind of read-side scaling problem from the command side.
Q: How would you add a brand-new read model six months after launch, for a query pattern nobody anticipated?
A: This is exactly what event sourcing is designed for — replay the entire historical event log from offset zero through a new projection consumer that builds the new read table from scratch. Because the event log is the durable source of truth, you never need to backfill from the old read models or guess at historical state; the new projection derives correct historical data the same way the original ones did.
Q: Your team wants to change an event's field from a string customerName to a structured customerName/customerId pair. How do you do this without breaking replay of old events?
A: Never edit the historical event records. Introduce a new event version (e.g., OrderPlacedV2) and write an upcaster that transforms any OrderPlacedV1 encountered during replay into the V2 shape in memory (filling customerId with a sentinel or a lookup result) before it reaches aggregate or projection logic. New events going forward are written as V2 directly; old V1 events stay exactly as they were written, forever.
Q: When is CQRS overkill for a service?
A: When the service has simple CRUD access patterns, low read/write ratio skew, and no genuine need for independent read/write scaling or multiple specialized read representations of the same data. CQRS (and especially event sourcing) adds real operational complexity — eventual consistency handling, replay tooling, schema versioning discipline — that's only worth paying for when query patterns are numerous and divergent, or when full audit history is itself a business requirement, not just a nice-to-have.
java
// Snapshot write triggered every 50 events, used to bound replay cost
@Service
public class SnapshotPolicy {

    private static final int SNAPSHOT_INTERVAL = 50;

    public void maybeSnapshot(OrderAggregate agg) {
        if (agg.getVersion() % SNAPSHOT_INTERVAL == 0) {
            snapshotRepository.save(new OrderSnapshot(
                agg.getOrderId(), agg.getVersion(), agg.toSnapshotState()
            ));
        }
    }
}
Split-Brain, Health-Check False Positives & Chaos Experiment Design
Split-Brain During Active-Active Network Partition
In an active-active database cluster (e.g., Galera Cluster, CockroachDB), a network partition between nodes causes a split-brain scenario: both halves of the cluster continue accepting writes, believing the other half is down. When the partition heals and the cluster tries to reconcile, it discovers conflicting writes for the same primary key on different nodes. The resolution strategy must be defined upfront: last-write-wins (lossy), manual conflict resolution (operator-intensive), or CRDT-based merge (only works for commutative operations). The prevention is quorum: require a write to succeed on a majority of nodes (e.g., 3 of 5) before acknowledging it. A minority partition cannot reach quorum and rejects writes entirely — trading availability for consistency during the partition.
Cascading Health-Check False Positives During a DB Brownout
When the database enters a brownout (accepts connections but responds slowly — p99 > 10s), every application instance's deep readiness probe starts timing out and returning DOWN. The load balancer removes all instances from rotation simultaneously. Now 100% of traffic receives 503s — a self-inflicted outage caused by the health-check system trying to protect users. The production defense: separate DB connectivity from readiness at the health check layer. Use a dedicated <100ms fast-path health check for the load balancer (checks only the app process) and relegate the full deep check to a degradation alert that pages SRE without triggering LB removal.
Chaos Experiments Must Have a Defined Steady State First
Teams that run chaos experiments without a defined steady state produce results that are uninterpretable. The experiment "kill a pod and see what happens" tells you nothing if you can't quantify what "normal" looks like. A proper chaos experiment defines the steady state (p99 latency < 200ms, error rate < 0.1% over 5 minutes), the hypothesis (the system will maintain steady state if one of the 5 order-service pods is terminated), the method (Chaos Mesh pod kill with blast radius scoped to the order-service namespace), and the rollback (if error rate exceeds 5% for 30 seconds, terminate the experiment). Without this structure, chaos engineering is just breaking things and hoping you learn something.
Q: An active-passive PostgreSQL setup has a 5-second replication lag. The primary crashes. The ops team promotes the replica immediately. How many seconds of data loss is guaranteed, and what could have prevented it?
A: Up to 5 seconds of data loss is possible — any transactions committed on the primary in the last 5 seconds were not yet replicated to the replica. Prevention: configure synchronous replication (synchronous_standby_names = '1 (replica1)') so the primary does not acknowledge commits until at least one replica confirms WAL receipt. The trade-off is write latency: every commit now waits for a round-trip to the replica (~1ms on LAN). The architectural choice between async (low write latency, non-zero RPO) and sync (higher write latency, near-zero RPO) is a business decision, not a technical one.
Q: You have a 99.9% SLO and an error budget of 43 minutes/month. A deployment last Tuesday caused 38 minutes of elevated errors. How does this affect your deployment cadence for the rest of the month?
A: You have 5 minutes of error budget remaining for the month. Under a standard SRE error-budget policy, this triggers a deployment freeze: all non-critical feature deployments are paused until the budget resets at month end. Engineering effort shifts entirely to reliability improvements. The value of the error budget model is exactly this self-enforcing behavior — it creates a direct feedback loop between deployment frequency and reliability, without requiring executive decisions on every deployment.
Q: Describe how you would implement distributed tracing for a request that spans an API gateway, 3 Spring Boot microservices, and a Kafka consumer — including what you'd look for in the trace to diagnose a p99 latency regression.
A: Instrument all services with OpenTelemetry Java agent (zero-code for Spring Boot via Micrometer Tracing bridge). The API gateway injects a W3C Trace Context header (traceparent) on every incoming request. Each Spring service propagates the trace ID through Kafka message headers (traceparent) so the consumer's span correctly links to the producer's span in the same trace. Export spans to Jaeger or Grafana Tempo. For p99 regression diagnosis: filter traces by percentile in Tempo, find traces in the p99 bucket, expand span timeline to identify which service contributes the spike. Common findings: a DB query that appears fast on p50 but has lock contention on p99, a Kafka consumer that blocks on serialization, or a downstream HTTP call with no timeout that occasionally hangs.
Q: What is the difference between an SLO breach and an SLA breach, and why does that distinction matter for on-call escalation?
A: An SLO (Service Level Objective) is an internal engineering target — a warning threshold before things get bad. An SLA (Service Level Agreement) is a contractual commitment to customers with financial penalties on breach. Breaching an SLO means the error budget is being consumed; it triggers a reliability review and potentially a deployment freeze. Breaching an SLA triggers account credits, contract review, and potentially customer churn. On-call escalation should trigger on SLO breach (while there's still time to recover before SLA breach), not wait for the SLA to breach. The SLO should be set meaningfully tighter than the SLA — typically SLO = SLA + 1-2 nines of headroom.
yaml
# Chaos Mesh 2.x: pod-kill experiment targeting a specific deployment
apiVersion: chaos-mesh.org/v1alpha1
kind: PodChaos
metadata:
  name: order-service-pod-kill
  namespace: chaos-testing
spec:
  action: pod-kill
  mode: one           # kill exactly one pod at a time
  selector:
    namespaces: [production]
    labelSelectors:
      app: order-service
  scheduler:
    cron: "@every 10m"   # kill one pod every 10 min during business hours
---
# Expected steady state: error rate stays below 0.1% during pod kill
# Verify: check Prometheus alert rule fires if error_rate > 0.5% for 2m
# Rollback: kubectl delete podchaos order-service-pod-kill
Redlock Controversy, Hot Slot Failure & Multi-Region Cache Consistency
Martin Kleppmann's Redlock Critique and What It Actually Means in Practice
In 2016, Martin Kleppmann published a thorough analysis arguing that Redlock does not provide the safety guarantees Antirez claimed, primarily because it assumes that lock expiry is based on wall-clock time and that clocks are synchronized. If a GC pause or OS time skew causes a client to hold a lock past its TTL (from the Redis nodes' perspective), two clients can simultaneously believe they hold the lock. Antirez's rebuttal: Redlock is designed for "efficiency" (preventing duplicate work) not "correctness" (preventing double-execution of critical sections with external effects). The production conclusion: use Redlock for cache stampede prevention and deduplication (where duplicate execution is expensive but not catastrophically wrong). Use a purpose-built coordination service (ZooKeeper, etcd with fencing tokens) for correctness-critical distributed locks where a "ghost lock" would cause data corruption.
A Hot Hash Slot Saturates One Redis Primary Despite Even Sharding
Redis Cluster assigns hash slots to primaries. If a small number of keys are accessed at extremely high rate (e.g., a global leaderboard key, a flash-sale inventory counter), those keys all map to one slot on one primary — and that primary's CPU pins at 100% while the other primaries idle. This is the same hot-partition problem as database sharding. Solutions: Key spreading with application-side fan-out — split the counter into N keys (e.g., inventory:flash:0 through inventory:flash:7), increment a random one per request, read by summing all 8. Local aggregation — aggregate increments in-process (Caffeine) and flush to Redis in batches (e.g., every 100ms). Replica reads — configure Lettuce's read preference to REPLICA for read-heavy hot keys, routing reads away from the primary.
Multi-Region Cache Invalidation Is Fundamentally an Eventually Consistent Problem
When you run Redis Cluster in two geographic regions (e.g., us-east and eu-west) with async cross-region replication, a cache key invalidated in us-east will not immediately be invalidated in eu-west. The replication lag is typically 50–150ms under normal conditions, but can spike during cross-region link congestion. During that window, eu-west serves stale data. This is not a bug — it is the inherent trade-off of choosing AP over CP in the CAP model. The correct architecture is to accept bounded staleness (set TTL aggressively short for sensitive data, e.g., 5 seconds) or use Redis Enterprise's Active-Active geo-replication with CRDT-based conflict resolution, which allows writes in both regions and merges deterministically.
Q: Your product page is cached with a 10-minute TTL. A flash sale starts and 50,000 users hit the product page simultaneously exactly as the TTL expires. Describe two different stampede prevention strategies and the trade-offs of each.
A: Strategy 1 — Distributed lock (Redisson): on cache miss, acquire a lock, rebuild the cache, release. Other threads wait and then hit the now-populated cache. Pro: zero duplicate DB queries. Con: all 50,000 threads block until the one lock holder finishes; p99 latency spikes during the rebuild window (~200ms). Strategy 2 — XFetch probabilistic early expiration: each thread independently evaluates whether to recompute early based on a probability formula that increases as TTL approaches zero. Spreads the rebuild across several minutes before the actual expiry, so the stampede moment never exists. Pro: no lock contention, no p99 spike. Con: slightly more total DB queries (multiple early refreshes instead of exactly one per TTL cycle); harder to reason about.
Q: You're designing a rate limiter for an API using Redis. The product requirement is "no more than 100 requests per user per sliding 60-second window." Describe the Redis data structure and commands you'd use, and identify one failure mode.
A: Use a Redis Sorted Set keyed by ratelimit:{userId}. On each request: ZADD ratelimit:{userId} {timestamp} {request_id} to record the request, ZREMRANGEBYSCORE ratelimit:{userId} 0 {now - 60000} to evict expired entries, ZCARD ratelimit:{userId} to count requests in the window, and set expiry EXPIRE ratelimit:{userId} 61 to auto-clean the key. If the count exceeds 100, reject the request. Wrap all three commands in a Lua script for atomicity. Failure mode: if the Redis node holding the key fails between the ZADD and ZCARD commands (without a Lua script), the request is recorded but not counted, allowing a burst above the limit. The Lua script atomicity fix prevents this specific race.
Q: When would you choose Memcached over Redis in 2025, and what would make you regret that choice six months later?
A: You'd choose Memcached for a pure K/V cache workload where you need maximum throughput from a single instance (Memcached is multi-threaded, Redis is single-threaded for commands), all values are simple strings or serialized blobs, and you never need persistence, pub/sub, Lua scripting, or TTL-per-field semantics. You'd regret it when: the team needs to implement a rate limiter (Sorted Set), a leaderboard (Sorted Set), a session with partial updates (Hash), or a pub/sub notification system — all native Redis primitives that require complex client-side logic or a second caching layer if you're on Memcached.
yaml
# Redis 7.x Cluster configuration — redis.conf for a 3-primary / 3-replica cluster
port 6379
cluster-enabled yes
cluster-config-file nodes.conf
cluster-node-timeout 5000        # failover if primary unreachable for 5s
cluster-migration-barrier 1      # replica only migrates if source still has ≥1 other replica
cluster-require-full-coverage no # serve partial cluster (AP) vs reject all writes (CP)
#                                   set to 'yes' for CP mode during node failure
maxmemory 8gb
maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru    # evict LRU keys when maxmemory hit (vs noeviction)
lazyfree-lazy-eviction yes       # async key deletion to avoid blocking the event loop
hz 20                            # higher background task frequency for faster expiry
activerehashing yes
Hot Shards, Cross-Shard ACID, & Online Resharding Failures
A Shard Key That Looked Evenly Distributed in Testing Hotspots in Production
A team shards their e-commerce orders table on product_category_id — 200 categories, spread across 20 shards. In load tests with synthetic traffic distributed evenly across categories, every shard gets ~5% of traffic. In production, "Electronics" accounts for 45% of orders during Black Friday. Shard 7 (which owns Electronics) receives 45% of all writes; 19 other shards idle. The fix requires resharding — a major, risky operation — because the shard key is fundamentally wrong for the actual traffic distribution. The lesson: validate shard key cardinality against real production access logs, not synthetic test data. Use a shard key that correlates with the natural unit of work (user or order ID for user-generated content), not with a business category whose popularity is inherently uneven.
2PC Holds Locks Across Shard Boundaries, Creating Deadlock Potential
In a two-phase commit across shards, the coordinator instructs all participant shards to acquire row locks during the PREPARE phase. These locks are held until the coordinator sends COMMIT or ABORT in phase 2. If the coordinator is slow (GC pause, network congestion) or another transaction is holding locks on a cross-cutting set of rows, a cross-shard deadlock can form that no single shard's deadlock detector can resolve (because each shard only sees its local lock graph). Production systems that use 2PC across shards require a global deadlock detector (Spanner uses wound-wait; CockroachDB uses a distributed deadlock detection algorithm) or they set aggressive lock timeouts and retry on deadlock errors at the application layer.
Online Resharding With Double-Write Can Produce Invisible Data Divergence
During a double-write resharding migration, a bug in the backfill job silently skips rows where created_at < 2024-01-01 due to a timestamp comparison bug (off-by-one on timezone conversion). The migration completes "successfully" — all new writes are on the new shards. Reads switch to the new shards. Weeks later, a customer service query finds order history pre-2024 missing for 3% of customers — their orders were on the affected shards and the backfill never copied them. The prevention: after backfill, run a row-count and checksum comparison between old and new shards before switching reads. Use something like SELECT count(*), sum(crc32(order_id)) FROM orders WHERE customer_id BETWEEN X AND Y on both old and new shards and verify the counts and checksums match within each customer range.
Q: You need to perform a cross-shard aggregation: total revenue for the last 30 days across all customers. You have 32 shards. Describe the architecture that handles this without query fan-out at request time.
A: Pre-aggregate via a CDC-driven materialized view: use Debezium to stream order insert/update events from all 32 shards into Kafka. A Kafka Streams or Flink job consumes the events and maintains a running aggregate in a separate analytics store (ClickHouse, BigQuery, or even a dedicated PostgreSQL aggregation table). The GET /analytics/revenue/30d endpoint reads from the pre-computed aggregate — a single-row lookup, sub-millisecond. The trade-off: the aggregate is eventually consistent (typically <1 second lag). For business dashboards, this is acceptable. For financial reconciliation requiring exact real-time numbers, a coordinated scatter-gather with explicit shard quorum is unavoidable, and you accept the O(N shards) latency cost.
Q: An ERP customer insists on "complete data isolation" from other tenants for compliance reasons. They also want their own database backup schedule and point-in-time recovery. What tenancy model do you recommend, and what does that cost?
A: Database-per-tenant (dedicated PostgreSQL instance). Schema-per-tenant shares a DB process and therefore shares backup/recovery configuration and cannot guarantee isolation at the process level. A dedicated instance gives the tenant their own WAL, their own backup schedule (pg_basebackup on their schedule), their own PITR timeline independent of other tenants, and process-level isolation (a memory corruption bug in one tenant's query cannot leak to another). The cost: each dedicated instance consumes base memory (~256MB+ for PostgreSQL shared_buffers), disk, and monitoring overhead. For 500 tenants, this is 500 managed database instances — significant operational cost, typically only economical for enterprise-tier customers where the contract value justifies the per-tenant infrastructure spend.
Q: What is a fencing token in distributed locking, and why do Redlock critics argue that without fencing tokens, no Redis-based lock is safe for critical sections?
A: A fencing token is a monotonically increasing integer issued by the lock service when a lock is granted. The token is sent with every operation to the protected resource. The resource validates that the incoming token is greater than the last seen token and rejects stale requests. The scenario without fencing tokens: client A acquires lock (token 33), gets paused by GC for 60 seconds, lock TTL expires, client B acquires lock (token 34) and completes its work, client A wakes up and still thinks it holds the lock (it checks isHeldByCurrentThread() — true! — because it hasn't interacted with Redis since acquisition), and proceeds to execute the critical section concurrently with client B's completion. With fencing tokens: client A's request to the resource is rejected because token 33 < 34. Redlock does not provide fencing tokens, which is the core of Kleppmann's critique.
sql
-- PostgreSQL 17: Row-Level Security for multi-tenant isolation
-- All queries automatically filtered to current tenant — even if app forgets WHERE

CREATE TABLE orders (
    id          BIGINT PRIMARY KEY GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY,
    tenant_id   TEXT   NOT NULL,
    customer_id BIGINT NOT NULL,
    amount      NUMERIC(12,2) NOT NULL
);

-- Enable RLS
ALTER TABLE orders ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- Policy: rows are visible only if tenant_id matches the current session variable
CREATE POLICY orders_tenant_isolation ON orders
    USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant'));

-- In Spring Boot: set session variable via connection decorator
-- conn.createStatement().execute("SET app.current_tenant = '" + tenantId + "'");

-- CRITICAL: Create the index on (tenant_id, ...) or RLS filtering is a full table scan
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_tenant_customer
    ON orders (tenant_id, customer_id, id DESC);
